
# Section 1: Introduction to Langchain


1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:06,320
Welcome to the first Section of our course building and testing AI agents, rags and chatbots using

2
00:00:06,320 --> 00:00:11,180
Lang chain with Olama and local large language models.

3
00:00:11,210 --> 00:00:16,970
I know the heading of this course is quite big, but as the heading suggests, the entire course itself

4
00:00:16,970 --> 00:00:22,520
is going to be so deep, and we are going to be talking about a lot of details about how we can build

5
00:00:22,550 --> 00:00:27,470
AI agents, rags and chatbots in much, much detailed fashion.

6
00:00:27,500 --> 00:00:33,530
We'll also be seeing how we can test these using the testing techniques, which are available to ensure

7
00:00:33,530 --> 00:00:39,410
that these agents, or rags and chatbots, does not hallucinate and gives the precise answer that we're

8
00:00:39,410 --> 00:00:40,220
looking for.

9
00:00:40,640 --> 00:00:45,230
But before we get into all these details, let's first understand what is this Lang chain?

10
00:00:45,710 --> 00:00:52,220
Well, Lang chain is a framework for developing applications powered by large language models.

11
00:00:52,640 --> 00:00:56,420
And you can see that this is the architecture of this Lang chain itself.

12
00:00:56,420 --> 00:01:00,630
And don't worry about the architecture right now because we're just starting up over here.

13
00:01:00,630 --> 00:01:04,380
But you will see that these are things that we are going to be commonly using.

14
00:01:04,380 --> 00:01:07,590
While we talk about the link chain in this entire course.

15
00:01:07,590 --> 00:01:10,680
One is the link chain itself, which is the library.

16
00:01:10,680 --> 00:01:17,940
And then it's going to be a link graph, which is going to be helpful for you to use it with the agents

17
00:01:17,940 --> 00:01:19,260
and powering the agents.

18
00:01:19,260 --> 00:01:23,670
And that is going to be a lot of integration components which is used by the link chain.

19
00:01:23,700 --> 00:01:28,830
These components are going to be something that we'll be talking in our upcoming lectures of this section.

20
00:01:28,830 --> 00:01:32,910
And there are going to be long chain platforms which are going commercial platform, which are mainly

21
00:01:32,910 --> 00:01:37,710
used for deployment purpose, which you can use to deploy your large language model application and

22
00:01:37,710 --> 00:01:39,930
can be widely used by the consumers.

23
00:01:39,930 --> 00:01:45,240
And there is one of the most important thing which is going to be common for all these components like

24
00:01:45,270 --> 00:01:48,540
architecture components and deployment is going to be the Lang Smith.

25
00:01:48,540 --> 00:01:54,090
And this Lang Smith is very, very important because this is going to be helpful for debugging playground

26
00:01:54,120 --> 00:01:58,470
prompt management annotation testing and monitoring purpose.

27
00:01:58,470 --> 00:02:04,340
And this also commercial, as you can see over here, but you can use the free version for our development

28
00:02:04,340 --> 00:02:06,560
that we are going to be doing in this particular course.

29
00:02:06,560 --> 00:02:09,020
So this is the complete framework of the link chain.

30
00:02:09,020 --> 00:02:14,030
I know it is very, very high level by now, but once we get into this link chain more deeper, you'll

31
00:02:14,030 --> 00:02:17,240
understand these components slowly and steadily.

32
00:02:17,240 --> 00:02:21,650
But first of all, let's understand what are the things that we are going to be using this link chain

33
00:02:21,650 --> 00:02:22,130
for.

34
00:02:22,130 --> 00:02:28,790
Well, using this link chain we can build chatbots like ChatGPT, frequently asked questions, customer

35
00:02:28,790 --> 00:02:29,750
support applications.

36
00:02:29,750 --> 00:02:33,710
So all these things we can build using this link chain.

37
00:02:33,740 --> 00:02:40,820
We can also build a rag which is nothing but retrieval augmented generation which will extend the large

38
00:02:40,820 --> 00:02:46,580
language models knowledge with external data source like documents and knowledge sources.

39
00:02:46,580 --> 00:02:49,940
And we can also use this link chain for building AI agents.

40
00:02:49,940 --> 00:02:54,740
So the agents that can plan, reason and execute the action that you're looking for.

41
00:02:54,770 --> 00:02:59,700
And you can also use the link chain for AI powered search, which is like an intelligent search using

42
00:02:59,700 --> 00:03:04,050
the natural language, which will be using embedding and vector database to make this happen.

43
00:03:04,080 --> 00:03:09,600
It can be used for text summarization and content generation as it names data extraction and processing,

44
00:03:09,600 --> 00:03:11,970
and also for AI powered code generation.

45
00:03:11,970 --> 00:03:18,180
So we can write automated tests using the playwright or selenium, or for that matter, using this lang

46
00:03:18,210 --> 00:03:18,720
chain.

47
00:03:18,720 --> 00:03:21,270
So you see that this is very, very powerful.

48
00:03:21,270 --> 00:03:25,770
All these applications that you're going to be building are going to be powered by this Lang chain.

49
00:03:25,770 --> 00:03:32,730
Even browser use one of the application, which is mainly used for performing web data extraction,

50
00:03:32,730 --> 00:03:34,260
uses Lang chain a lot.

51
00:03:34,260 --> 00:03:37,080
And we talked about something called as Lang Smith over here.

52
00:03:37,080 --> 00:03:42,030
As I told you, this is one of the most important key component of this entire journey that we are going

53
00:03:42,060 --> 00:03:43,170
to be doing in this course.

54
00:03:43,170 --> 00:03:50,010
So Lang Smith is an evaluation and debugging platform for large language model applications built by

55
00:03:50,010 --> 00:03:51,300
the Lang Chain team itself.

56
00:03:51,300 --> 00:03:57,540
So it helps developers test, optimize, and monitor their AI application by providing powerful tools

57
00:03:57,560 --> 00:04:03,080
for tracing, logging, debugging and benchmarking different large language model components.

58
00:04:03,080 --> 00:04:07,160
And we are going to be using this language with quite a lot in this course, as I told you before.

59
00:04:07,700 --> 00:04:12,590
So in this course, as you can see over here, we are going to be doing all these operations.

60
00:04:12,620 --> 00:04:16,310
We are going to first understand the basic building blocks of long chain.

61
00:04:16,310 --> 00:04:20,660
This is going to take a couple of sections to understand how things will work, and then how we can

62
00:04:20,660 --> 00:04:27,170
create our own long chain chains and how we can use with the large language models run the chains.

63
00:04:27,170 --> 00:04:33,380
And also we can use these syntax that we have learned over here, the concepts that we have learned

64
00:04:33,380 --> 00:04:34,340
from here.

65
00:04:34,340 --> 00:04:41,660
And then we'll implement these to build the chatbots, build rags or retrieval augmented generations.

66
00:04:41,690 --> 00:04:45,410
We can also be building agents or the AI agents.

67
00:04:45,410 --> 00:04:49,940
And then finally we'll be testing all these things that we have built over here.

68
00:04:50,120 --> 00:04:54,620
And you will see that all these components that we are going to be doing over here are going to be done

69
00:04:54,620 --> 00:05:01,530
with the all Arma and local large language models, and the local large language models can be llama

70
00:05:01,530 --> 00:05:05,250
three R2 or Dbsk R1 or Qn 2.5 model.

71
00:05:05,250 --> 00:05:13,290
It can be of anything as long as your machine can execute or support running these large language models

72
00:05:13,290 --> 00:05:19,200
within your local machine, you can use any number of parameters, so you can see that you can use 8

73
00:05:19,200 --> 00:05:22,350
billion parameter or 2 billion parameter, or 6 billion parameter.

74
00:05:22,350 --> 00:05:24,870
It depends upon how your machine can handle those.

75
00:05:24,870 --> 00:05:27,780
And we are going to be using that in this particular course.

76
00:05:27,780 --> 00:05:32,400
I'm going to be prevalently using the llama 3.28 billion parameter mostly.

77
00:05:32,400 --> 00:05:36,270
If your machine can handle more than that, you are very happy to use them.

78
00:05:36,270 --> 00:05:37,710
And guess what?

79
00:05:37,710 --> 00:05:44,550
The more and more billion parameters your large language model supports, the better your language applications

80
00:05:44,550 --> 00:05:47,940
response is going to be and more accurate the response is going to be.

81
00:05:47,940 --> 00:05:51,780
And as I said, let's talk about the prerequisite of this course.

82
00:05:51,960 --> 00:05:55,020
We are going to be using Windows or Mac operating system.

83
00:05:55,020 --> 00:06:00,440
So I'm going to be mostly using Mac operating system because I run everything in my Mac OS as well as

84
00:06:00,440 --> 00:06:05,870
I have Mac, Apple M1 machine, so I will be using that as a host machine for all the execution.

85
00:06:05,900 --> 00:06:10,490
And we also need to have Python for execution, because the entire operation that we are going to be

86
00:06:10,490 --> 00:06:12,950
using in the long chain is going to be Python.

87
00:06:12,950 --> 00:06:17,240
Long chain also has JavaScript version, but we are going to be using the Python version here.

88
00:06:17,240 --> 00:06:20,000
And then you need to have a good machine configuration.

89
00:06:20,030 --> 00:06:28,310
Of course it can be M1 or higher, or maybe windows with a GPU like 208 0 or 3 080, or even 4090 for

90
00:06:28,340 --> 00:06:28,850
that matter.

91
00:06:28,850 --> 00:06:33,950
If you have any of these GPU, which is amazing, you will have a very good inferencing time and it

92
00:06:33,950 --> 00:06:36,170
should be very, very faster when you execute them.

93
00:06:36,200 --> 00:06:41,150
You also need to have Visual Studio Code, of course, and Olama, because Olama is the one that we

94
00:06:41,150 --> 00:06:46,520
are going to be using as a platform to run the local large language models.

95
00:06:46,520 --> 00:06:50,450
So these are the precursor of this course guys, and I'm quite excited with this course for you.

96
00:06:50,450 --> 00:06:55,820
So talk to you in our next lecture where we are going to be talking about why lamb-smith in first place.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,850
In this lecture, we'll understand why.

2
00:00:01,880 --> 00:00:02,960
Lang chain.

3
00:00:04,130 --> 00:00:08,480
Well, lang chain can help us do quite a lot of different operations.

4
00:00:08,510 --> 00:00:12,830
The main purpose of Lang Chain is it standardizes the component interfaces.

5
00:00:12,950 --> 00:00:19,700
As you know, there are growing number of models coming up every single day, like OpenAI's GPT model

6
00:00:19,700 --> 00:00:24,650
and cloud anthropic model, and Google's Gemini model and Microsoft's Pi model.

7
00:00:24,650 --> 00:00:30,140
And then there is suddenly Chinese deep sea model, and there is also Qnn model.

8
00:00:30,140 --> 00:00:32,300
And there are even more models coming up lately.

9
00:00:32,300 --> 00:00:36,980
This year, while there is going to be a new model from Russia and other countries are going to come

10
00:00:36,980 --> 00:00:37,790
up pretty soon.

11
00:00:37,790 --> 00:00:42,320
So you see that all these models are going to be keep on growing every single time.

12
00:00:42,320 --> 00:00:47,840
And the related components of the AI application has resulted in a wide variety of different APIs that

13
00:00:47,840 --> 00:00:50,720
the developer need to learn and use every single time.

14
00:00:50,720 --> 00:00:56,420
And this diversity can make it challenging for developers to switch between providers or combine components

15
00:00:56,420 --> 00:01:02,400
when building applications, and Lang Chain exposes a standard interface for key components, making

16
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:04,560
it so easy to switch between these providers.

17
00:01:04,590 --> 00:01:07,950
So basically, this long chain is agnostic to any of the model.

18
00:01:07,950 --> 00:01:13,140
And it supports all these model by providing an interface for all of these models.

19
00:01:13,140 --> 00:01:18,600
And there is also this observability and evaluation pattern available in the long chain by default.

20
00:01:18,600 --> 00:01:23,220
So as an application becomes more and more complex, it becomes increasingly difficult to understand

21
00:01:23,220 --> 00:01:28,800
what is really happening within these applications and how these applications are going to be querying

22
00:01:28,800 --> 00:01:30,690
with these large language models.

23
00:01:30,690 --> 00:01:34,740
And now with the power of this language, Smith.

24
00:01:35,310 --> 00:01:40,650
The observability and the evaluation is just embedded inside the chain itself.

25
00:01:40,650 --> 00:01:45,660
And as it said, let's talk about the standardization of the component and interfaces even more.

26
00:01:45,690 --> 00:01:51,570
But as you see, long chain provides a consistent interface for working with with chat models from different

27
00:01:51,570 --> 00:01:58,630
providers like Anthropic Cloud, OpenAI, Alarma, Microsoft Azure, Google Vertex, and more, and

28
00:01:58,630 --> 00:02:05,440
using either long chains, message format or Openeye format, you can make a consistent messaging of

29
00:02:05,440 --> 00:02:07,450
the data from your application.

30
00:02:07,450 --> 00:02:10,330
And also there is a standard tool calling API.

31
00:02:10,360 --> 00:02:16,660
So basically the standard interface for binding tools to model access tool called request made by these

32
00:02:16,660 --> 00:02:18,850
model is going to be very, very easy.

33
00:02:18,850 --> 00:02:24,430
And also Lang Chain has got standard APIs for structured output, which can be helpful for you to get

34
00:02:24,430 --> 00:02:30,970
a structured response from the large language models, something like a JSON response or text response

35
00:02:30,970 --> 00:02:32,740
or a string response that you're looking for.

36
00:02:32,770 --> 00:02:39,250
And also Lang chain provides support for async programming, efficient batching, and a rich streaming

37
00:02:39,250 --> 00:02:44,590
API, so you can stream the response from the large language model much, much easily just by using

38
00:02:44,590 --> 00:02:46,300
the stream method which is available.

39
00:02:46,300 --> 00:02:50,290
It is so easy and I will show you how these things work, and I'm quite excited to show you how these

40
00:02:50,290 --> 00:02:51,370
things work, really.

41
00:02:51,400 --> 00:02:56,230
And finally, integration with Lang Smith for monitoring and debugging production grade application

42
00:02:56,230 --> 00:03:01,660
based on the large language model is also amazing, and this is something I keep on telling from last

43
00:03:01,660 --> 00:03:02,170
lecture.

44
00:03:02,170 --> 00:03:08,200
You will be seeing how amazing they are and as I said, the entire standard model integration is going

45
00:03:08,200 --> 00:03:09,640
to look something like this.

46
00:03:09,640 --> 00:03:14,080
And you can see that the long chain has got the integration with the long chain or lemma, or long chain

47
00:03:14,110 --> 00:03:16,360
OpenAI or long chain anthropic.

48
00:03:16,360 --> 00:03:19,570
And any model that is going to come up, they are going to be supported.

49
00:03:19,570 --> 00:03:21,130
Guys, this is so cool.

50
00:03:21,130 --> 00:03:23,410
So that is the power of the long chain itself.

51
00:03:23,410 --> 00:03:28,390
They are going to keep on building all these integration embedded inside their platform.

52
00:03:28,390 --> 00:03:31,420
That way it supports all these different models.

53
00:03:31,420 --> 00:03:36,790
Even the deep sea, which just came like couple of weeks before, is going to be supported or even supported

54
00:03:36,790 --> 00:03:38,620
already with the long chain.

55
00:03:38,620 --> 00:03:45,100
So you can just use that particular integration within your large language model based applications

56
00:03:45,850 --> 00:03:47,830
and finally interfaces.

57
00:03:47,830 --> 00:03:51,670
You see that long chain has got this standard interfaces as well.

58
00:03:51,700 --> 00:03:55,940
All you have to do in order to invoke a large language model, It's just called the method called as

59
00:03:55,940 --> 00:03:56,870
Invoke method.

60
00:03:56,870 --> 00:04:00,710
And if you want to stream the data from the large language model, you just have to call the stream

61
00:04:00,710 --> 00:04:01,040
method.

62
00:04:01,040 --> 00:04:03,200
It can be of any large language models.

63
00:04:03,200 --> 00:04:08,240
All these models are going to just comply with the long chain interfaces, which is going to make things

64
00:04:08,240 --> 00:04:09,320
more cleaner.

65
00:04:09,350 --> 00:04:14,630
And also there is a batch support and even more different supports available with this interfaces.

66
00:04:14,930 --> 00:04:15,890
That's amazing.

67
00:04:15,890 --> 00:04:21,290
And that is the reason why we need to go with the long chain while working with the large language model

68
00:04:21,320 --> 00:04:23,060
based application and beyond.

69
00:04:23,060 --> 00:04:29,090
That long chain also has support for standard input outputs, tool calling, standard output, formatting,

70
00:04:29,120 --> 00:04:31,100
observability, catching, and more.

71
00:04:31,100 --> 00:04:36,500
So you see that this thing is just keep on going to expand while the platform develops and while the

72
00:04:36,500 --> 00:04:37,370
library develops.

73
00:04:37,370 --> 00:04:42,770
All these standardization is going to happen for your large language model based application.

74
00:04:43,040 --> 00:04:46,370
That's the power of the long chain.

75
00:04:46,370 --> 00:04:52,640
And this and that is the reason why we should be going with the long chain while building large language

76
00:04:52,640 --> 00:04:54,110
model based applications.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:03,560
In this lecture we are going to talk about Lange Chain's ecosystem.

2
00:00:04,850 --> 00:00:10,220
So Lange Chain library and its surrounding ecosystem is what makes Lange chain so special for building

3
00:00:10,220 --> 00:00:12,230
large language model based applications.

4
00:00:12,260 --> 00:00:18,950
So Lange chain has got two most important component, which is the Lange, Smith and Lange graph.

5
00:00:18,980 --> 00:00:25,640
I mean, apart from the Lange chain library itself, these two are the next most important component

6
00:00:25,640 --> 00:00:28,490
that the Lange chain has got in its ecosystem.

7
00:00:28,490 --> 00:00:34,640
So Lange Smith is mainly used for tracing and evaluating your large language models, applications,

8
00:00:34,640 --> 00:00:38,960
and intelligent agent to help you move your prototype to production.

9
00:00:38,960 --> 00:00:44,480
That is what this Lange Smith is all about, and this is mainly used for evaluation for every single

10
00:00:44,480 --> 00:00:50,030
step that you perform in the Lange chain, while you're writing the library and how your application

11
00:00:50,030 --> 00:00:52,520
really behaves under the hood, that is, what is this?

12
00:00:52,520 --> 00:00:53,060
Lange Smith?

13
00:00:53,060 --> 00:00:55,970
And you are going to be using this quite a lot in this entire course.

14
00:00:55,970 --> 00:00:57,740
And the next one is the Lange graph.

15
00:00:57,770 --> 00:01:03,710
I mean, Lange graph is a library for building stateful multi actor applications with a large language

16
00:01:03,710 --> 00:01:07,190
models used to create agents and multi-agent workflow.

17
00:01:07,190 --> 00:01:10,730
And this is again going to be very very important and useful.

18
00:01:10,730 --> 00:01:15,480
While you are going to be creating an agent and you will be understanding how this workflow of the agent

19
00:01:15,480 --> 00:01:22,530
can be controlled, binded with different tools and how you work with those agents for your production

20
00:01:22,530 --> 00:01:23,250
ready application.

21
00:01:23,250 --> 00:01:25,590
So that is what this line graph is all about.

22
00:01:25,590 --> 00:01:30,750
I'm going to quickly show you how this Lang Smith is going to look like, and how you can get started

23
00:01:30,750 --> 00:01:35,250
with this in this particular lecture, so that while we start working with the Lang Smith, you will

24
00:01:35,250 --> 00:01:38,310
have no surprise what this Lang Smith is all about.

25
00:01:38,310 --> 00:01:38,730
All right.

26
00:01:38,730 --> 00:01:42,540
So this is the Lang Chain website, as you can see over here.

27
00:01:42,540 --> 00:01:45,900
And this Lang chain has got Lang chain, lang Smith and Lang graph.

28
00:01:45,900 --> 00:01:47,940
And these are the three components that they have got.

29
00:01:47,970 --> 00:01:52,800
As I told you in the ecosystem, this Lang Smith and land graph are the most important component apart

30
00:01:52,800 --> 00:01:54,840
from the Lang chain library itself.

31
00:01:54,840 --> 00:01:59,760
So if you go to the Lang Smith over here, it's going to bring up this particular page over here.

32

And they give you the information of how this particular Lang Smith is going to be useful.

33

Like you can see that how many people have signed up and how many logs are being traced every single

day, and how many monthly active team members are there, and what are the things that it does and

35

all those details is available.

00:02:24,870 --> 00:02:27,510

And there is also a default, uh, tracing project, which is something that is not going to show you

any information, but the one that you are seeing over here, this is your automation.

00:03:34,800 --> 00:03:39,570

65

further with even more information.

66
00:03:55,380 --> 00:03:59,430
What you're seeing over here is just the basic operation, but it's going to keep on increasing even

67

further.

68

So that's about the Lange Smith for us.

with.

# Section 3: Running Local Large Language Model (LLM) in local Machine with Ollama


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,140 --> 00:00:01,820
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,820 --> 00:00:08,270
And in this Section we are going to talk about Olama and how we can make use of this Olama for building

3
00:00:08,300 --> 00:00:13,310
a large language model based application using our local large language model.

4
00:00:13,310 --> 00:00:21,020
So we are going to use this Olama as the as the medium to build our large language model based application

5
00:00:21,020 --> 00:00:24,950
using long chain with the local large language model.

6
00:00:24,950 --> 00:00:27,680
So this is what is the power of this olama itself.

7
00:00:27,680 --> 00:00:30,800
And I'm going to quickly show you how these things are going to work behind the scene.

8
00:00:30,800 --> 00:00:38,210
So if you just go to the Olama comm website over here, this is the page which I'm talking about, or

9
00:00:38,210 --> 00:00:42,620
this is the application that I'm talking about, which is going to be helpful for you to run the large

10
00:00:42,620 --> 00:00:45,620
language models locally within your own machine.

11
00:00:45,620 --> 00:00:51,350
So if you have a mac OS or Linux or Windows, you can just go ahead and download this particular application

12
00:00:51,350 --> 00:00:55,550
within your machine, and then you can start using all the models within your machine.

13
00:00:55,550 --> 00:00:56,960
That is amazing, right?

14
00:00:56,960 --> 00:01:04,540
You don't really have to sign up with the OpenAI's API to call an API for accessing the large language

15
00:01:04,540 --> 00:01:09,310
models, like how you usually do by paying so much of money, those things are not even required.

16
00:01:09,310 --> 00:01:15,040
So if you just go to the Openai.com and if you just go to their API platform over here, you see that

17
00:01:15,040 --> 00:01:16,720
there is this API login comes in.

18
00:01:16,720 --> 00:01:22,780
So if you just go and hit this API login, I have already signed up for this particular OpenAI login

19
00:01:22,780 --> 00:01:23,440
over here.

20
00:01:23,470 --> 00:01:25,540
I mean you can see there are some pricings available.

21
00:01:25,540 --> 00:01:32,590
So for every single, uh, model that you use with their signed up, uh, models API, you're going

22
00:01:32,620 --> 00:01:38,380
to be paying this much money for these many input that you're going to be entering, like for 1 million

23
00:01:38,380 --> 00:01:38,890
tokens.

24
00:01:38,890 --> 00:01:44,200
These are things that these are the money that you have to pay for these particular large language models

25
00:01:44,200 --> 00:01:50,170
while you're going to use that, if it is hosted in their own platform like OpenAI platform or Cloud

26
00:01:50,170 --> 00:01:52,510
Anthropic Platform or Google Gemini platform.

27
00:01:52,510 --> 00:01:55,540
So you have to sign up and you have to pay money to them.

28
00:01:55,540 --> 00:01:59,800
But if you're going to be building a large language model application, and if you don't want to spend

29
00:01:59,800 --> 00:02:06,870
these many money, then you can just host, or maybe just can run your large language model within your

30
00:02:06,870 --> 00:02:08,490
local machine itself.

31
00:02:08,490 --> 00:02:12,990
And that is the power of this particular olama, because it will let you do that for you.

32
00:02:12,990 --> 00:02:18,150
So I have already downloaded the Olama within my local machine in my Mac operating system.

33
00:02:18,150 --> 00:02:21,570
So it is very, very easy for me to just start using it.

34
00:02:21,870 --> 00:02:23,310
I mean, it's very straightforward.

35
00:02:23,310 --> 00:02:26,070
Just go ahead and click this download based on your operating system.

36
00:02:26,070 --> 00:02:27,780
The download is going to happen for you.

37
00:02:27,780 --> 00:02:30,690
And because I am using Mac OS, I can go and click this one.

38
00:02:30,720 --> 00:02:34,800
If you're going to use Linux or Windows, just go ahead and select the relevant operating system and

39
00:02:34,800 --> 00:02:36,780
download the tools that you're looking for.

40
00:02:37,020 --> 00:02:38,910
And finally the models.

41
00:02:38,910 --> 00:02:41,160
This is the most important part over here.

42
00:02:41,160 --> 00:02:46,470
If you just go to the models over there, this particular models that you are seeing over here are all

43
00:02:46,470 --> 00:02:51,870
the models, like the full blown models available within the Olama site itself.

44
00:02:51,870 --> 00:03:03,290
So you can use a deep seek R1 or llama three dot 3 or 4, or llama three R2 and Mistral Q1 Q1 2.5.

45
00:03:03,320 --> 00:03:07,220
You see that all the models that you name it, it's available over here.

46
00:03:07,220 --> 00:03:13,250
And most importantly, you can also see that there are options where you can just select specific model

47
00:03:13,250 --> 00:03:17,510
which has specific for embedding or vision or even tool support.

48
00:03:17,510 --> 00:03:22,820
So if you have any of the agents that you're going to be building, and then if you wanted to use the

49
00:03:22,820 --> 00:03:25,250
model for tool support, you can use that as well.

50
00:03:25,280 --> 00:03:31,250
Guys, you see that 3.3 70 billion parameter has got the tool support model over here which is amazing.

51
00:03:31,280 --> 00:03:32,540
You can use that as well.

52
00:03:32,540 --> 00:03:39,080
And if I'm going to use this deep one, the most popular model to date is going to be very, very straightforward

53
00:03:39,080 --> 00:03:39,380
as well.

54
00:03:39,380 --> 00:03:42,590
You can choose a specific billion parameter.

55
00:03:42,590 --> 00:03:45,950
And then you can start working with this particular model as well.

56
00:03:45,950 --> 00:03:50,570
If you're going to be using this model for building your local large language model applications.

57
00:03:51,710 --> 00:03:53,810
So that is about the Obama for you.

58
00:03:53,810 --> 00:03:56,660
And I have already downloaded it and I have already installed it.

59

So please go ahead and install it before our next lecture because we are going to start using it from

00:04:01,340 --> 00:04:02,450
our next lecture.

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:00,440
All right.

2
00:00:00,440 --> 00:00:04,310
So hopefully you have already downloaded the ulama within your local machine and you have installed

3
00:00:04,310 --> 00:00:04,580
it.

4
00:00:04,580 --> 00:00:05,750
So which is great.

5
00:00:05,750 --> 00:00:09,980
And if you have not please go ahead and do it again, because that is something required for our next

6
00:00:09,980 --> 00:00:12,350
discussion that we are going to be doing in this particular lecture.

7
00:00:12,350 --> 00:00:18,710
So as you can see over here, I can choose the model that I wanted to run within my local machine.

8
00:00:18,710 --> 00:00:24,620
So I can choose 7 billion parameter or 8 billion parameters, 14 billion parameter, 32 billion parameter,

9
00:00:24,620 --> 00:00:27,800
all the way up to seven 671 billion parameter.

10
00:00:27,800 --> 00:00:30,800
Within my machine, you see that there is a drop down over here.

11
00:00:30,830 --> 00:00:35,240
It's going to show you the size of this particular parameter and the model.

12
00:00:35,240 --> 00:00:38,150
So 7 billion parameter has got 4.7 gig.

13
00:00:38,150 --> 00:00:45,170
And if you see 671 billion parameter it is 404 GB that you have to have within your machine.

14
00:00:45,170 --> 00:00:51,260
I'm telling you based on the parameter, the complexity of the transformer model increases and based

15
00:00:51,260 --> 00:00:56,420
on the complexity, it also increases the the processing power.

16
00:00:56,420 --> 00:01:00,620
And also you need to have quite a lot of quantization execution support.

17
00:01:00,620 --> 00:01:04,580
So you can see that for a 7 billion parameter I have the model over here.

18
00:01:04,580 --> 00:01:09,440
And you see that the model has got a quantization version of two.

19
00:01:09,460 --> 00:01:15,280
And it also has got the the total headcount over here which is 28.

20
00:01:15,280 --> 00:01:19,630
So totally it's going to have 28 different types of headcounts like classifications that it's going

21
00:01:19,660 --> 00:01:21,250
to support in this particular model.

22
00:01:21,250 --> 00:01:25,960
And it also has got a label of 7 billion because it's a 7 billion parameter.

23
00:01:25,960 --> 00:01:31,420
And the same thing is going to keep changing based on the type of parameter they're going to choose.

24
00:01:31,420 --> 00:01:34,720
For instance, if you're going to choose like 671 billion parameter.

25
00:01:34,720 --> 00:01:40,030
And if you choose this one over here, you see the number of headcounts might have increased quite a

26
00:01:40,030 --> 00:01:41,260
lot this time.

27
00:01:41,260 --> 00:01:45,850
So the headcount right now is 128 as opposed to 14 it was before.

28
00:01:45,850 --> 00:01:49,090
And the headcount kV is also 128.

29
00:01:49,240 --> 00:01:52,870
So it is going to support quite a lot of different classifications.

30
00:01:52,900 --> 00:01:56,560
While you're going to be using this particular model within your machine.

31
00:01:56,560 --> 00:02:01,810
And also the size is also increases based on that the quantization increases.

32
00:02:01,810 --> 00:02:08,080
And if the quantization increases, obviously you need to have a lot of GPU power, CPU power, Ram

33
00:02:08,080 --> 00:02:09,340
power within your machine.

34
00:02:09,370 --> 00:02:15,460
I will say most of our machines are not really built to run this 671 billion parameter or 70 billion

35
00:02:15,460 --> 00:02:21,680
parameter and even 32 billion parameter we can probably limit until 14 billion or 8 billion at the max.

36
00:02:21,680 --> 00:02:25,250
And I'm using Apple M1 Max machine over here.

37
00:02:25,250 --> 00:02:30,350
And I have got 64 GB of memory and it is quite good in the inferencing.

38
00:02:30,350 --> 00:02:32,270
So I'm just going to use this particular machine.

39
00:02:32,270 --> 00:02:38,150
But if you have uh, a windows machine or same kind of machine.

40
00:02:38,150 --> 00:02:41,060
So probably we can just limit with the eight GB.

41
00:02:41,060 --> 00:02:47,690
And if you don't really have a powerful machine, I will recommend you to use a lower version of the

42
00:02:47,690 --> 00:02:48,380
parameter.

43
00:02:48,380 --> 00:02:54,410
But the more lower parameter that you use in the model, the less predictable output that you can actually

44
00:02:54,410 --> 00:02:59,360
get out from the particular model, because the model really doesn't perform quite well if you use smaller

45
00:02:59,360 --> 00:03:01,400
model within your local machine.

46
00:03:01,400 --> 00:03:09,590
So either try to get a GPU for that matter, like 2080 or 30 80 or 40, 90, 40, 90 is going to be

47
00:03:09,590 --> 00:03:10,880
very costly for you for sure.

48
00:03:10,910 --> 00:03:16,970
3080 at least max, or maybe 2080 if you just get any of these Nvidia graphics card within the machine,

49
00:03:16,970 --> 00:03:18,230
you can probably use that.

50
00:03:18,230 --> 00:03:22,850
And then you can try to run whatever that we're going to be discussing in this particular course.

51
00:03:22,850 --> 00:03:24,980
But as I said, that's about it.

52
00:03:24,980 --> 00:03:30,190
That's the thing that we are going to be using within our machine, and we'll see how we can.

53
00:03:30,400 --> 00:03:33,700

54
00:03:33,700 --> 00:03:38,770
So that is how you can understand how these models are really working behind the scene.

55
00:03:39,100 --> 00:03:45,250
So if I choose any other model, not just the deep sea, let's say a llama 3.3, they also have got

56
00:03:45,280 --> 00:03:47,950
like 70 billion parameter, as you can see over here.

57

And if I'm going to choose some other models like you see that llama 3.1, there is a 405 billion parameter,

58
00:03:55,030 --> 00:03:58,510
which is 243 GB of storage itself.

59
00:03:58,510 --> 00:04:02,530
And it is going to, uh, it's going to have quite a lot of different operations that you are going

60
00:04:02,560 --> 00:04:03,790
to be doing over there.

61
00:04:03,790 --> 00:04:10,420
And let's see what is the total headcount that I have got like 128 headcount, as you can see over here.

62
00:04:10,420 --> 00:04:15,370
And the headcount cave is just eight, whereas in deep sea it was 128 as you know.

63
00:04:15,370 --> 00:04:17,950
So yeah, it depends upon the model.

64
00:04:18,010 --> 00:04:21,040
The things are going to keep changing and also the complexity changes.

65
00:04:21,040 --> 00:04:26,890
And so is your machine's performance reduces based on the type of parameter and the model that you really

66
00:04:26,890 --> 00:04:27,670
use.

67

Well, as I said in our next lecture, we are going to see how we can start using any one of the models

in our local machine and also try to play around with that.

billion parameter, because it's completely gone.

15
00:00:49,880 --> 00:00:53,330
And similarly, if I want to delete this particular model, I know this is completely useless.

16
00:00:53,330 --> 00:01:01,970
So I can just say Olama and then RM and I can just say this particular model to delete, it's gone.

17
00:01:01,970 --> 00:01:06,980
And now if I just say hola, my list, that particular model is not there anymore.

18
00:01:06,980 --> 00:01:12,130
And if I wanted to do something like, uh, gonna get some information on this particular model.

19
00:01:12,130 --> 00:01:13,870
I can also do that as well.

20
00:01:13,870 --> 00:01:21,520
So I can just say show and any one of the model, let's say this, uh, deep sea carbon model, you

21
00:01:21,520 --> 00:01:28,840
see that it's going to show me what architecture this model is and what is the parameter support and

22
00:01:28,840 --> 00:01:35,230
what is the context length and what is the embedding length, what is the quantization and also what

23
00:01:35,230 --> 00:01:37,450
are the parameters supports available over there.

24
00:01:37,450 --> 00:01:40,780
So all these informations are going to be shown for me over there.

25
00:01:40,780 --> 00:01:44,830
So these are the things that you can see that you can see using this Allama tool.

26
00:01:44,830 --> 00:01:50,050
And I mean this Allama is pretty much like how you can use like the Docker container.

27
00:01:50,050 --> 00:01:51,310
How do you manage the docker.

28
00:01:51,310 --> 00:01:52,390
How do you run the docker.

29
00:01:52,390 --> 00:01:56,710
Everything that you do in the Docker world, same thing you can do with the Allama.

30
00:01:56,710 --> 00:02:00,760
It's pretty much like the docker of the large language model.

31
00:02:00,760 --> 00:02:04,660
You can run everything from this particular Allama over here.

32
00:02:04,690 --> 00:02:06,550
That's about this Allama tool guys.

33
00:02:06,550 --> 00:02:12,220
And starting our next lecture, we're going to see how we can make use of this Allama large language

34
00:02:12,220 --> 00:02:19,090
model from our local machine and then use it with the Lang chain for building our large language model.



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:06,320
In this lecture, we are going to see how we can use this olama to act as an API server, which you

2
00:00:06,320 --> 00:00:12,830
can use to retrieve the the model or communicate with the model using an API request.

3
00:00:12,830 --> 00:00:20,150
So basically you can see that using this Olama and you can see that they have got this option called

4
00:00:20,150 --> 00:00:22,190
as serve.

5
00:00:22,220 --> 00:00:24,020
So Olama serve.

6
00:00:24,050 --> 00:00:26,630
So we just hit enter.

7
00:00:26,630 --> 00:00:31,580
You will see that currently my port is bound with this particular IP address.

8
00:00:31,580 --> 00:00:37,490
So basically I'm already running the Olama in the service as like up and running all the time.

9
00:00:37,490 --> 00:00:40,880
That's the reason why it says that the the address is already bound.

10
00:00:41,240 --> 00:00:46,880
And but if you're going to be running this olama serve in your terminal, you will notice that it is

11
00:00:46,880 --> 00:00:51,020
going to be starting as a service for you by listening to this particular port.

12
00:00:51,050 --> 00:00:52,610
Number 11434.

13
00:00:52,610 --> 00:00:57,590
So this is the port number that we are going to be using quite a lot, starting our next Section while

14
00:00:57,590 --> 00:00:58,880
we communicate with the Olama.

15
00:00:58,880 --> 00:01:01,160
So the way we are going to write the code in our starting.

16
00:01:01,160 --> 00:01:07,100
Our next Section is going to be talking to the large language model with the Olama only using the API

17
00:01:07,130 --> 00:01:08,990
server, which is this API server.

18
00:01:08,990 --> 00:01:13,010
So when I say API server, we're just going to go over here.

19
00:01:13,010 --> 00:01:21,620
And if I just type localhost of the port number 11434, and if I just say API and if I hit enter oh

20
00:01:21,620 --> 00:01:22,100
sorry.

21
00:01:23,450 --> 00:01:26,450
And if I hit enter like that you'll see that the olama is running.

22
00:01:26,450 --> 00:01:28,670
So you get the response like Olama is running.

23
00:01:28,670 --> 00:01:35,870
And if you see just go to the uh, 143 API generate and you can also pass the body over here.

24
00:01:35,900 --> 00:01:43,880
So if I just go to my browser and if I just search for Olama, you'll notice that these are the API

25
00:01:43,910 --> 00:01:45,710
documentation of the Olama.

26
00:01:45,710 --> 00:01:52,220
And you can see that they have got all the details of the APIs that you can use with the Olama over

27
00:01:52,220 --> 00:01:55,430
here, you see that they have got the in the generate.

28
00:01:55,430 --> 00:01:59,990
You can pass the model and the prompt that you want to ask, and then you get the response from the

29
00:01:59,990 --> 00:02:00,850
particular model.

30
00:02:00,850 --> 00:02:07,420
So if I just go to the postman over here and let's say if I'm going to ask the question, uh, something

31
00:02:07,420 --> 00:02:11,500
like this, and the model is going to be llama 3.2 that they have used.

32
00:02:11,500 --> 00:02:12,850
And let's see if I have got the model.

33
00:02:12,850 --> 00:02:14,590
Yeah, I have got the llama 3.2.

34
00:02:14,620 --> 00:02:17,230
So I'm just gonna leave it as it is.

35
00:02:17,260 --> 00:02:24,430
And I'm going to say, why is Sky, uh, blue and this is going to be a post operation that we have

36
00:02:24,430 --> 00:02:25,000
to do.

37
00:02:25,000 --> 00:02:31,240
And if I just ask this question, you'll notice that we are going to get a response from the model over

38
00:02:31,240 --> 00:02:34,990
here, the large language model that we are getting over here.

39
00:02:34,990 --> 00:02:41,560
But you see that we are getting the response in like one by one, uh, like one word at a time as a

40
00:02:41,560 --> 00:02:42,070
response.

41
00:02:42,070 --> 00:02:45,190
But we can stream this particular response as well if we wanted to.

42
00:02:45,220 --> 00:02:49,630
So if you just go back to the documentation over here, this is the response that you get.

43
00:02:49,630 --> 00:02:55,090
And over here they also have got something called a stream as a false, uh, over here.

44
00:02:55,120 --> 00:02:57,460
And I'm just going to leave the stream as false over here.

45
00:02:57,460 --> 00:03:01,220
And now if I try to send it over here, you will notice that it's not really streaming the data, but

46
00:03:01,250 --> 00:03:05,120
it's going to send you all the data in a chunk for you immediately.

47
00:03:05,120 --> 00:03:05,720
Something like this.

48
00:03:05,720 --> 00:03:07,760
And you see that we get the response over here.

49
00:03:07,760 --> 00:03:10,310
So this is how we get the entire response.

50
00:03:10,310 --> 00:03:11,900
So why is the sky blue.

51
00:03:11,930 --> 00:03:16,040
It says that the reason why the sky appears blue is a fascinating phenomenon that involves a combination

52
00:03:16,040 --> 00:03:17,900
of physics, lights and our atmosphere.

53
00:03:17,900 --> 00:03:19,490
And it gives all the details over there.

54
00:03:19,490 --> 00:03:21,530
So this is how we can see that.

55
00:03:21,530 --> 00:03:30,290
We can use the the APIs of the llama to communicate with our large language model using Olama.

56
00:03:30,290 --> 00:03:31,370
So olama serve.

57
00:03:31,400 --> 00:03:35,270
You need to run to make sure that you can communicate with your Olama.

58
00:03:35,270 --> 00:03:39,470
So starting our next section, we are going to communicate with this Olama with a large language model

59
00:03:39,470 --> 00:03:40,790
only using these APIs.

60
00:03:40,790 --> 00:03:46,250
So either you run them as a service like me, like how I'm doing it, or you can run this Olama serve

61
00:03:46,250 --> 00:03:52,940
command in the terminal so that you can now start communicating with your, uh, with the lamp chain

62
00:03:52,970 --> 00:03:54,830
with the olama starting in the next section.

63
00:03:54,830 --> 00:04:00,980
So this ends the Section of the Olama and how you can use Olama to run your large language models.

00:00:36,560 --> 00:00:42,710
able to communicate with the large language model using the chat olama from the long chain.

10
00:00:42,710 --> 00:00:48,710
And then we did an invoke and we sent the query and then it gave us the response back.

11
00:00:48,710 --> 00:00:55,730
And also with the connectivity of the Langschmidt, we could able to get the, uh, traceability and

12
00:00:55,730 --> 00:01:00,080
observability metrics into the Langschmidt portal.

13
00:01:00,080 --> 00:01:02,490
And that's going to look something like this for us over here.

14
00:01:02,490 --> 00:01:04,410
So user sends a request to the Lang chain.

15
00:01:04,410 --> 00:01:11,910
And Lang chain in turn sends the request to the local large language model using the chat or llama.

16
00:01:11,910 --> 00:01:19,710
And then we get the response back from the, uh, from the large language model to the Lang chain.

17
00:01:19,710 --> 00:01:23,700
And we also record the traceability in the Lang Smith.

18
00:01:23,700 --> 00:01:25,470
And then we get the response to the user.

19
00:01:25,500 --> 00:01:25,680
Right.

20
00:01:25,710 --> 00:01:28,800
So that is what is really happening all these days for us over here.

21
00:01:28,800 --> 00:01:36,750
But again, the response is just coming from the local large language model via Olama is exactly what

22
00:01:36,750 --> 00:01:38,760
is being printed for us over here.

23
00:01:38,760 --> 00:01:45,960
As you can see, along with some traceability details and some some more details, pretty much like

But what if while you build a large language model, you need to get the responses like how you want

What if I wanted to pass both the human input and I'm expecting an AI output in this fashion?

00:11:26,420 --> 00:11:28,250

172
00:11:28,250 --> 00:11:31,790

175
00:11:42,080 --> 00:11:43,010
How can I actually do that?

176
00:11:43,010 --> 00:11:46,700
Well, you can do that all using the chat prompt template.

177
00:11:46,700 --> 00:11:53,480

178
00:11:53,480 --> 00:11:56,120
So that is what we are going to be discussing in our next lecture.

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:07,190
So in our last lecture, if you see we were doing as a user, we're passing the the request or the message

2
00:00:07,190 --> 00:00:10,100
to the Lamb-smith via the prompt template.

3
00:00:10,100 --> 00:00:15,560
And then the prompt template was formatting the the input along with the input variables.

4
00:00:15,560 --> 00:00:19,010
And then it was sending it to the large language model.

5
00:00:19,010 --> 00:00:20,990
And we're getting the response back to the lamb-smith.

6
00:00:20,990 --> 00:00:22,580
And then we were sending it to the user.

7
00:00:22,580 --> 00:00:26,360
And of course there was an observability were happening using the lamb-smith for us.

8
00:00:26,390 --> 00:00:29,540
And that is what we were discussing in our last lecture.

9
00:00:29,540 --> 00:00:36,440
But now that we are going to set a role for the large language model itself, and then we will see how

10
00:00:36,440 --> 00:00:37,610
we can get the responses.

11
00:00:37,610 --> 00:00:46,340
So basically when we say role, what I really mean about that is that if I want to say that you large

12
00:00:46,340 --> 00:00:53,870
language model, you are an expert in this particular field, then give me this particular response

13
00:00:53,870 --> 00:00:55,130
in a perfect fashion.

14
00:00:55,130 --> 00:00:56,570
So you can say that as well.

15
00:00:56,570 --> 00:01:01,190
So you can tell or assign a role to the large language model while you ask the question.

16
00:01:01,190 --> 00:01:04,790
So for instance, if I go to the ChatGPT over here.

17
00:01:04,790 --> 00:01:16,850
And then if I ask a question that, uh, can you explain me the financial world, uh, financial status

18
00:01:16,850 --> 00:01:19,700
or maybe financial status of NZ?

19
00:01:20,630 --> 00:01:28,520
Uh, it is going to give me a response over here, like what it is going to be responding like searching

20
00:01:28,520 --> 00:01:30,350
it and getting the responses.

21
00:01:30,350 --> 00:01:33,350
Pretty much like how it does like a Google search for me.

22
00:01:33,380 --> 00:01:33,770
Right.

23
00:01:33,800 --> 00:01:42,800
And now let me just stop this guy and I'm going to say you are an financial expert in NZ economy.

24
00:01:42,890 --> 00:01:51,500
Now give me now explain me the financial uh status of NZ.

25
00:01:51,860 --> 00:01:59,030
So now while I do that over here, you will notice that as a financial expert of New Zealand economy

26
00:01:59,030 --> 00:02:02,420
in 2025, he has the detailed breakdown of the financial status.

27
00:02:02,420 --> 00:02:08,540
So now you see that now this ChatGPT has got a role that he is going to play for us when it responds

28
00:02:08,540 --> 00:02:12,770
and it says that this is the current economic condition, what is the fiscal position?

29
00:02:12,770 --> 00:02:17,780
What is the monetary policy and interest rate policy and investment initiative and the conclusion?

30
00:02:17,780 --> 00:02:19,640
So that is what I'm talking about.

31
00:02:19,640 --> 00:02:27,350
So instead of you getting just like a like a search result from the ChatGPT what it knows, but this

32
00:02:27,350 --> 00:02:32,570
is going to give you even more of an optimized and expected result that you're looking for.

33
00:02:32,570 --> 00:02:39,260
So this kind of roles that you can actually set to the prompt template, and then you can send it to

34
00:02:39,290 --> 00:02:43,700
the ChatGPT from using the prompt template as well.

35
00:02:43,700 --> 00:02:45,530
That is what we're going to be discussing this lecture.

So let's see how we can actually achieve that.

44

So so you can pass the chat prompt template and then you can pass the the data.

But because the data requires a sequence of message, as you can see over here is that there's a square

00:03:57,340 --> 00:04:01,330
here because you see that every single time I tried to run this particular code, it is actually going

00:04:12,520 --> 00:04:16,690

# Section 5: LangChain Chains and Runnables


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,000 --> 00:00:02,070
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:02,070 --> 00:00:08,550
And in this Section we are going to talk about understanding, chaining and runnables in Lange chain.

3
00:00:08,550 --> 00:00:11,970
So this is one of the most important concept that we need to understand.

4
00:00:11,970 --> 00:00:14,670
Because as you know this is a Lange chain.

5
00:00:14,670 --> 00:00:16,830
And there is a chain word there already.

6
00:00:16,830 --> 00:00:19,080
And that is the power of the Lange chain itself.

7
00:00:19,080 --> 00:00:21,210
And that is what we are going to be doing over here.

8
00:00:21,210 --> 00:00:26,670
The chaining mechanism and a runnable are also one of the most important concept that you need to understand.

9
00:00:26,670 --> 00:00:30,600
So let's see what we are going to talk about this runnables in first place.

10
00:00:30,600 --> 00:00:35,520
So if you remember in our last Section while we were working with we had got this what is called as

11
00:00:35,520 --> 00:00:37,650
the Lange Smith Lange chain.

12
00:00:37,650 --> 00:00:44,160
And then there is a template and we were passing the chat prompt template with the role.

13
00:00:44,160 --> 00:00:48,780
And also we were passing it the message to the large language model in the local machine.

14
00:00:48,960 --> 00:00:50,580
And these are the things which are happening.

15
00:00:50,610 --> 00:00:52,860
And now I have added two gear symbol there.

16
00:00:52,860 --> 00:00:58,170
So this gear is nothing but runnables just assume it that that is what these two are.

17
00:00:58,170 --> 00:01:05,360
So this runnables what it does really is that they are responsible for executing an action.

18
00:01:05,360 --> 00:01:11,750
So in in this long chain, every single operation that you perform like prompt.

19
00:01:11,750 --> 00:01:16,760
For example, if you call an invoke methods, if you remember from the prompt we can call the invoke

20
00:01:16,760 --> 00:01:17,270
method.

21
00:01:17,270 --> 00:01:18,680
Then it is a runnable.

22
00:01:18,680 --> 00:01:22,310
And in the large language model we can also call the invoke method.

23
00:01:22,310 --> 00:01:24,380
So basically that is an runnable as well.

24
00:01:24,380 --> 00:01:32,150
And similarly if you're going to be creating probably an agent or a retriever or an output parser,

25
00:01:32,180 --> 00:01:39,440
any of these things that you create in the long chain, these are all considered as runnables.

26
00:01:39,440 --> 00:01:43,040
So just to get back to the details of the runnable.

27
00:01:43,040 --> 00:01:48,890
So in long chain runnable interface is the foundation of working with the long chain component.

28
00:01:48,890 --> 00:01:55,250
And it's implemented across many of them such as language models, output parsers, retrievers, compiled

29
00:01:55,250 --> 00:01:57,560
language, long chain graphs, and more.

30
00:01:57,800 --> 00:02:00,710
And this is the details that you have to cover.

31
00:02:00,740 --> 00:02:02,720
Or maybe you can just read through about it.

32
00:02:02,720 --> 00:02:09,300
But one thing that we have to even understand is the long chain expression language, or LCL.

33
00:02:09,330 --> 00:02:13,080
That is something that we will talk about while we talk about chaining mechanism.

34
00:02:13,080 --> 00:02:18,450
But over here on the Runnables, you will notice that this is the overview of the Runnables.

35
00:02:18,480 --> 00:02:24,930
The runnable way defines the standard interface that allows runnable components to invoke batch, stream

36
00:02:24,930 --> 00:02:26,940
and inspected composed.

37
00:02:27,000 --> 00:02:32,520
You remember in our last Section of our course, the last lecture, we were talking about this what

38
00:02:32,520 --> 00:02:34,830
is called as a streaming operation.

39
00:02:34,860 --> 00:02:39,300
And we also did the invoke operation over here.

40
00:02:39,300 --> 00:02:43,290
We also did the invoke for the templates, which is the prompt templates.

41
00:02:43,290 --> 00:02:49,230
And we can also do the exact same thing for the string output or agents or whatever that we are creating.

42
00:02:49,230 --> 00:02:50,220
We can do the invoke.

43
00:02:50,220 --> 00:02:57,570
So whichever, whichever component that you can do an invoke or stream and bind, then those components

44
00:02:57,570 --> 00:03:03,480
are essentially a runnable component or runnable interfaces or implementing the runnable interfaces

45
00:03:03,480 --> 00:03:04,650
behind the scene.

46
00:03:04,800 --> 00:03:08,400
And that is what this particular thing is.

47
00:03:08,400 --> 00:03:14,310
You can run this particular runnable interface in parallel, as you can see over here with the batch

48
00:03:14,340 --> 00:03:15,060
operation.

49
00:03:15,060 --> 00:03:18,690
And you can also run the Runnables in asynchronous operation.

50
00:03:18,690 --> 00:03:22,200
You can also run the runnable in the streaming operation.

51
00:03:22,230 --> 00:03:28,230
And also you can do runnable with quite a lot of different input and output types, which essentially

52
00:03:28,230 --> 00:03:36,510
means that you can pass the Runnables as an input type to another runnable component, and then you

53
00:03:36,510 --> 00:03:37,650
can perform the operation.

54
00:03:37,680 --> 00:03:39,690
So let me tell you what I really mean about that.

55
00:03:39,690 --> 00:03:47,670
So if you go back to our diagram over here, this is this entire thing, maybe this this entire thing,

56
00:03:48,900 --> 00:03:55,110
we can convert it as a runnable, and then we can convert this runnable output.

57
00:03:55,110 --> 00:03:59,640
And we can pass it to another runnable as an input.

58
00:03:59,640 --> 00:04:03,330
So this can be an input to another runnable that we wanted to create.

59
00:04:03,330 --> 00:04:05,070
And then we can move on from there.

60
00:04:05,070 --> 00:04:06,570
And that is the concept of chaining.

61
00:04:06,570 --> 00:04:08,850
And that is the reason why I don't want to cover it over here.

62
00:04:08,850 --> 00:04:16,170
So basically this runnable input runnable output, you can pass it as an input to another runnable.

63
00:04:16,170 --> 00:04:19,930
And the same thing keeps on going from there on.

64
00:04:19,930 --> 00:04:27,490
So you can keep doing that any further Section over here and then you can chain all these operations.

65
00:04:27,850 --> 00:04:30,190
And that is what is called as the chaining operation.

66
00:04:30,190 --> 00:04:32,410
So basically you can just do like this.

67
00:04:32,410 --> 00:04:34,450
So this is going to be one runnable.

68
00:04:34,450 --> 00:04:39,670
This is going to be another uh runnable over here.

69
00:04:40,000 --> 00:04:41,650
So this is a runnable one.

70
00:04:41,890 --> 00:04:44,800
And this is a runnable two something like that.

71
00:04:44,860 --> 00:04:46,060
Uh where is the text.

72
00:04:46,060 --> 00:04:46,600
There.

73
00:04:46,630 --> 00:04:47,950
Runnable one.

74
00:04:47,950 --> 00:04:51,670
And this is going to be runnable two.

75
00:04:51,700 --> 00:04:58,000
I mean, the entire thing I'm talking about and this can be a runnable three for that matter.

76
00:04:58,000 --> 00:05:07,120
So we can chain all of these, uh, in our actual execution with a chain.

77
00:05:07,120 --> 00:05:11,380
And we call this as chaining operation as well.

78
00:05:11,410 --> 00:05:13,030
I will talk about that later on.

79
00:05:13,060 --> 00:05:18,550
I don't want to confuse you there, but you got the idea of how the Runnables are really created and

80
00:05:18,550 --> 00:05:22,150
working in the actual Lang Chain library.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:05,240
The next concept that I want to talk about is the long chain expression language or LCL.

2
00:00:06,140 --> 00:00:13,850
So if I go to the LCL over here, you will notice that the long chain expression language takes a declarative

3
00:00:13,850 --> 00:00:19,160
approach to build a new runnable from existing runnables.

4
00:00:19,610 --> 00:00:26,210
So this means that you describe your what should happen rather than how it should happen, allowing

5
00:00:26,210 --> 00:00:29,720
the lang chain to optimize the runtime execution of the chain.

6
00:00:30,290 --> 00:00:32,000
This is quite cool, right?

7
00:00:32,000 --> 00:00:36,560
Because as I told you, every single component over here are runnables.

8
00:00:36,560 --> 00:00:41,210
Like even these two can be a runnable or even this.

9
00:00:41,210 --> 00:00:47,150
This alone, like the template alone, can be a runnable, and this LM can alone be a runnable.

10
00:00:47,180 --> 00:00:52,490
Because this, this are all going to call this invoke method or stream method or batch method.

11
00:00:52,670 --> 00:00:57,080
So all these are runnables as well in an isolation.

12
00:00:57,650 --> 00:01:05,060
So if we can run them in isolation, and if we can chain them together, like how the inputs and outputs

13
00:01:05,060 --> 00:01:05,180
are.

14
00:01:05,210 --> 00:01:09,800
Going to happen, then that's what is called as chaining mechanism.

15
00:01:09,800 --> 00:01:10,160
That is what.

16
00:01:10,190 --> 00:01:10,970
Exactly.

17
00:01:10,970 --> 00:01:14,600
This particular, uh LCL is going to explain us.

18
00:01:14,600 --> 00:01:17,240
So we often refer Runnables using.

19
00:01:17,270 --> 00:01:19,220
LCL as a chain.

20
00:01:19,220 --> 00:01:23,000
It is important to remember that the chain is a runnable and it implements the.

21
00:01:23,030 --> 00:01:24,440
Full runnable interface.

22
00:01:24,440 --> 00:01:28,010
What that really means is that the chain you can create for runnable.

23
00:01:28,190 --> 00:01:30,650
And the chain itself is a runnable.

24
00:01:30,980 --> 00:01:31,670
It's confusing.

25
00:01:31,670 --> 00:01:31,880
Right.

26
00:01:31,910 --> 00:01:33,740
Like that is how it is really.

27
00:01:33,740 --> 00:01:34,490
So.

28
00:01:34,520 --> 00:01:41,360
Basically you can chain two runnables and you can assign it to a variable called as chain.

29
00:01:41,390 --> 00:01:47,150
And the chain now becomes a runnable because now runnables all the chain also implements the runnable

30
00:01:47,150 --> 00:01:47,870
interface.

31
00:01:47,870 --> 00:01:48,860
In turn.

32
00:01:48,860 --> 00:01:50,540
That is what is really happening there.

33
00:01:50,690 --> 00:01:58,730
And this chain can now be useful for running in parallel execution, asynchronous operation and simplify

34
00:01:58,730 --> 00:02:04,680
the streaming operation because it is implementing the runnable Honourable interface or honourable interfaces,

35
00:02:04,680 --> 00:02:06,810
like how we saw in our last lecture.

36
00:02:06,810 --> 00:02:13,410
And because runnable interface supports asynchronous streaming and also batch execution.

37
00:02:13,410 --> 00:02:19,800
Uh, over here, the same thing is going to happen for the LCL as well, because it does implement the

38
00:02:19,800 --> 00:02:21,180
runnable interface.

39
00:02:21,180 --> 00:02:24,840
So it is going to have all the features where the runnable interface has got.

40
00:02:25,830 --> 00:02:27,720
So why should I use LCL in first place?

41
00:02:27,720 --> 00:02:30,660
Well, LCL is an orchestration solution.

42
00:02:30,660 --> 00:02:39,300
It allows Lang chain to handle runtime execution of chain in an optimized way, which is amazing because

43
00:02:39,300 --> 00:02:44,700
while we have seen the user runs chain with hundreds of steps in production, we generally recommend

44
00:02:44,700 --> 00:02:50,880
using LCL for simpler orchestration tasks when the application requires complex state management, branching

45
00:02:50,880 --> 00:02:52,890
cycle, or multiple agents.

46
00:02:52,890 --> 00:02:54,090
We recommend that.

47
00:02:54,090 --> 00:02:56,700
Take advantage of the Lang graph.

48
00:02:56,730 --> 00:02:59,100
Well, that is when we're going to be talking about Lang graph.

49
00:02:59,100 --> 00:03:02,570
But you'll notice that at the moment we are not even a single chain.

50
00:03:02,570 --> 00:03:06,920
But in production there are going to be hundreds of chain created.

51
00:03:08,930 --> 00:03:12,680
And that is what is going to happen while we are going to start building things.

52
00:03:12,680 --> 00:03:17,240
So I'll talk about the chain and this until you see the chain, you don't really get this whole concept.

53
00:03:17,240 --> 00:03:21,950
So once we create the chain, you will we will get back to the same page again.

And I will explain you what this LCA is all about.

itself.

So it's basically implements a runnable Because this chart, if you just go back over here, is going

106
00:06:28,730 --> 00:06:32,510
And basically this runnable serializable is going to be a runnable type.

Right.

If you just go keep digging down all the way, it is going to be runnable behind the scene.

So if I go to the chat prompt template, it is going to implement from the base chat prompt template.

00:06:48,650 --> 00:06:53,330
And if you just go all the way to the base chat prompt template, it is going to be calling the runnable

serializable because it can support the, um, the serializable execution.

And it is going to be calling the renewables over there.

And if you're going to be calling the if you're going to be talking about the streaming that we were

discussing in our last section, the last lecture of the course, which is the stream, the stream also

118
00:07:19,900 --> 00:07:25,150
So every single operation that you are seeing over here are going to be a runnable behind the scene

119
00:07:25,150 --> 00:07:25,720
by nature.

00:07:25,750 --> 00:07:26,140
Right.

121
00:07:26,140 --> 00:07:32,230
So while this is a runnable, we can now run instead of doing this.

122
00:07:32,230 --> 00:07:40,150
Like instead of you calling the prompt and then you call the invoke and then pass the prompt over here

123
00:07:41,020 --> 00:07:49,270
instead of doing that operation that you are doing before, you can now pass or chain the output of

124
00:07:49,270 --> 00:07:55,390
the prompt template, we can chain it to the LM as an input, because that is what you're going to essentially

125
00:07:55,390 --> 00:07:56,380
do over here.

So what I'm really telling over here is instead of you writing this code for the invoke operation,

127
00:08:04,300 --> 00:08:08,610
so you do a prompt dot invoke over here, and then you do an LM of invoke over here.

128

So you do two invoke operation.

129
00:08:10,830 --> 00:08:14,940
Instead what you can do you can create a chain.

It can be any name.

131

But I'm just giving chain name.

That is a standard way of doing it.

And over here I'm just going to say I wanted to give the prompt look at that.

134
00:08:26,280 --> 00:08:27,870

In LCL.

You can just do this or there is something called there was something called as LM chain.

Like a.

Sequence runnable parallel and router and stuff.

And now over here we are going to say prompt which is this prompt.

00:09:27,720 --> 00:09:35,160
I'm going to give the input of the output of this prompt as an input to LM.

So that is the chaining mechanism that I'm doing over here.

And even this invoke that I'm doing I'm just going to create um, a prompt.

And then I'm going to be giving it to, uh, sorry, not even the prompt, really, because we don't

have to call the invoke.

numbers because you know that it's a numbers already.

00:11:12,540 --> 00:11:21,630
And if I try to run this particular code, this is just going to work like how it was working even before.

178
00:11:21,780 --> 00:11:29,340
So now you have taken out the complexity of doing two invokes and creating the operation before.

179
00:11:29,340 --> 00:11:36,960
Instead, you can now create one invoke to pass the value, and you can now chain and guess what?

180
00:11:36,960 --> 00:11:40,590
You can keep chaining this any number of runners.

181
00:11:40,590 --> 00:11:44,640
That is the power of this chaining mechanism itself.

182
00:11:44,670 --> 00:11:46,800
And we'll be talking about that like in a minute.

183
00:11:46,830 --> 00:11:48,660
Like how we can do that in our next lecture.

184
00:11:48,660 --> 00:11:50,130
But but guess what this is.

185
00:11:50,130 --> 00:11:53,940
What is the the chaining mechanism that we're talking about over here.

186
00:11:53,940 --> 00:12:00,480
This is how you do the chaining of your runnables in long chain.

187
00:12:00,900 --> 00:12:03,630
So I hope you got the idea like how these are all is done.

188
00:12:03,630 --> 00:12:06,600
And now if I go back to our um.

189
00:12:07,980 --> 00:12:09,750
a Lang Smith over here.

190
00:12:10,680 --> 00:12:11,670
Look at that.

191
00:12:11,670 --> 00:12:16,440
We have got a runnable sequence this time because we're doing a sequence of operations.

192
00:12:16,440 --> 00:12:18,480
So we have got a runnable sequence.

193
00:12:18,480 --> 00:12:26,520
And this runnable sequence is telling me that we are calling an env or the input variable as local machine.

194
00:12:26,520 --> 00:12:30,990
And now we are getting this particular output over here.

195
00:12:30,990 --> 00:12:32,460
And also look at that.

196
00:12:32,460 --> 00:12:38,460
We don't even have any message saying the, the human message or the system message like that, because

197
00:12:38,460 --> 00:12:42,660
those things are going to be taken care over here in the next prompt.

198
00:12:42,660 --> 00:12:44,460
Uh, next message over here.

199
00:12:44,490 --> 00:12:45,150
Look at that.

200
00:12:45,150 --> 00:12:49,140
So there is a chat prompt template, uh, which is this one.

201
00:12:49,170 --> 00:12:51,690
And it has got the system and the human message.

202
00:12:51,690 --> 00:12:56,220
And then it goes to the chat Olama, uh, which is going to be system human.

203
00:12:56,430 --> 00:12:58,470
And then there is a runnable sequence over here.

204
00:12:58,470 --> 00:13:03,600
These are the three things belonging to this particular operation that we are doing over here.

205
00:13:03,600 --> 00:13:08,520
So that is how we can actually work with the chaining mechanism.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

But what is the purpose really?

So for for instance, let's say we get this, uh, this output from this particular chain, which is

model.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,140 --> 00:00:00,980
All right.

2
00:00:00,980 --> 00:00:07,460
So now that we have discussed about multiple chaining and how the chaining works until here, but now

3
00:00:07,460 --> 00:00:13,550
we are going to see how we can actually make an parallel chaining instead of the sequence chaining that

4
00:00:13,550 --> 00:00:14,270
we have been doing.

5
00:00:14,270 --> 00:00:17,000
So these are all runnable sequence that we are doing over here.

6
00:00:17,000 --> 00:00:21,410
So by default value chain like this using the pipe operator over here.

7
00:00:21,410 --> 00:00:24,800
These are all basically a runnable sequence operation.

8
00:00:24,800 --> 00:00:26,930
So it's basically running in sequence over here.

9
00:00:26,930 --> 00:00:29,930
But what if we wanted to run these chains in parallel.

10
00:00:29,930 --> 00:00:32,210
So how do we actually achieve that.

11
00:00:32,210 --> 00:00:39,830
Well in order for you to achieve a parallel execution or runnable parallel execution, you need to use

12
00:00:39,830 --> 00:00:41,390
a runnable parallel class.

13
00:00:41,390 --> 00:00:49,430
So I'm going to say a markdown here as a running chains um chains in parallel.

14
00:00:49,430 --> 00:00:52,220
And I'm going to say tick.

15
00:00:52,220 --> 00:00:54,590
And I'm going to add the code over here.

16
00:00:54,590 --> 00:00:57,710
So now I'm going to run these two in parallel for instance.

17
00:00:57,710 --> 00:01:02,060
So let's just go and copy this entire thing over here.

18
00:01:03,090 --> 00:01:04,740
And I'm going to paste it over here.

19
00:01:04,740 --> 00:01:09,390
So what is exactly parallel execution and why do we really need parallel execution.

20
00:01:09,390 --> 00:01:15,780
So for instance, if you're going to have a response coming up from one LM over here, at the moment

2

00:00:07,010 --> 00:00:10,490

5

So for instance, as you can see in our last lecture while we were trying to do the chaining operation

00:00:29,300 --> 00:00:34,190

Well, we need to have some condition to ensure that this is how I need to run this particular chain.

16
00:01:10,870 --> 00:01:12,160
large language model.

17

And then we need to pass that large language model as a parameter for this chain, and then perform

18
00:01:17,110 --> 00:01:20,860
the operation, which is going to be very, very complex unless until you do that hand coding.

19
00:01:20,860 --> 00:01:27,610
But instead you can actually use what is called as the runnable lambdas to make that happen.

20
00:01:27,610 --> 00:01:32,920
And based on its name, you can easily assume that that is what this, uh, runnable lambda is going

21
00:01:32,920 --> 00:01:33,610
to basically do.

22
00:01:33,640 --> 00:01:38,530
So, for instance, if you remember, in this particular code, we were, uh, doing this, let's just

23
00:01:38,530 --> 00:01:41,920
go up a bit over here chaining multiple chains.

24
00:01:42,430 --> 00:01:46,150
We said, what is the advantage of running the AI model in local machine?

25
00:01:46,150 --> 00:01:51,610
And also we told in the second chain that just analyze the response and get me the headings from this

26
00:01:51,610 --> 00:01:52,510
particular response.

27
00:01:52,540 --> 00:01:52,780
Right.

28
00:01:52,810 --> 00:01:55,480
This is another chain that we had got.

29
00:01:55,510 --> 00:01:58,900
And then we uh we got the response over here.

30
00:01:58,900 --> 00:02:07,410
So what if my my requirement is this where the first response of a chain that we are getting over here.

31
00:02:07,410 --> 00:02:15,210
If it has the length of more than 300, which means it is a bigger token value, and I need to use a

32
00:02:15,210 --> 00:02:17,040
specific large language model.

33
00:02:17,040 --> 00:02:23,760
And if it is less than 300 token or 300 character, for instance, then I have to use another large

34
00:02:23,760 --> 00:02:29,130
language model, maybe a smaller version of large language model, because the larger, bigger, larger

35
00:02:29,130 --> 00:02:31,860
language model is not even required for this kind of operation.

36
00:02:31,860 --> 00:02:36,630
So if I think that is my business case that I wanted to go with, then how do I actually do that?

37
00:02:36,630 --> 00:02:40,350
Well, you can do it using this runnable lambda.

38
00:02:40,350 --> 00:02:46,530
So you see that user inputs the first prompt, which is going to be what is the advantage of running

39
00:02:46,530 --> 00:02:48,900
the AI in the local machine.

40
00:02:48,900 --> 00:02:52,290
It goes to the model in the first chain of the runnable.

41
00:02:52,290 --> 00:02:54,900
And then it gets you the response.

42
00:02:54,900 --> 00:03:00,990
But that particular response that you are getting over here now needs to be deciding which particular

43
00:03:00,990 --> 00:03:03,030
large language model that you need to be running with.

44
00:03:03,030 --> 00:03:09,470
Because if it is less than 300, so probably you don't even need to run this in a bigger, larger language

45
00:03:09,470 --> 00:03:09,890
model.

46
00:03:09,890 --> 00:03:13,280
You maybe find the queue in itself so you can run it over here.

47
00:03:13,280 --> 00:03:19,220
But if you want to run, if the response is more than 300, then you can run in the llama model.

48
00:03:19,220 --> 00:03:24,950
And in order for making this kind of decisions, we are going to be using this runnable lambda to make

49
00:03:24,950 --> 00:03:25,460
that happen.

50
00:03:25,490 --> 00:03:29,120
And that is exactly what we are going to be doing over here.

51
00:03:29,120 --> 00:03:37,160
So let me just add this diagram for your detailed discussion that we have been doing, so that next

52

We have, uh, spoken in this particular discussion.

And probably I need to add one more markdown and I'm going to say uh.

00:04:53,800 --> 00:04:55,570

which is going to be LM two.

00:06:38,350 --> 00:06:43,900
Basically this is going to accept this choose LM method.

So basically using this runnable lambda you can actually execute an Python function over here or Python

102
00:06:54,430 --> 00:06:56,890

So that you could able to run them in the pipeline while you are actually executing, which is the chaining

00:07:04,660 --> 00:07:08,950
So that is the major power of this runnable lambda itself.

So that is the power which I'm talking about LM selector.

00:07:39,150 --> 00:07:39,600
lambda.

And this runnable lambda is going to pass the response which is going to be coming up for us over there.

00:07:58,110 --> 00:08:03,630
Like this is how you do select the large language model using the runnable lambda because it has the

00:08:03,630 --> 00:08:06,330

So this is how we can say that we can choose or run a Python code while running in the chaining operation.

155
00:10:06,580 --> 00:10:13,240
While there is a runnable happening in this chain, you can run a Python code in between using this

156
00:10:13,240 --> 00:10:14,350

That is the power of this runnable lambda itself.

But if I wanted to decorate this particular or make this particular choose LM as a as a chain which

00:01:26,980 --> 00:01:32,020
for this particular method that we are using making as a runnable lambda, I'm just going to write a

23

00:01:35,080 --> 00:01:35,680
That's all.

25
00:01:35,680 --> 00:01:38,050
That is what I'm going to be doing over here.

Add chain.

30
00:01:46,300 --> 00:01:46,990
Yeah.

31
00:01:47,410 --> 00:01:51,490
So because I was writing in this line, actually, that's why, uh, that's it.

You see that now this choose, um, becomes a runnable, uh, for our chain operation that we are doing

33
00:02:00,760 --> 00:02:01,480
over here.

34
00:02:01,510 --> 00:02:02,410

00:02:02,410 --> 00:02:05,110
So you see that this is what I am passing in over here as well.

36
00:02:05,140 --> 00:02:12,070
For this particular method, um, and now if I try to run this particular code, you should see that

the response is going to be pretty much exactly the same, like how we were doing earlier.

So it is just going to work fine as expected over here.

40

So instead of using you, the, uh, the runnable lambda to perform this operation with the LM selector,

41

uh, this time I'm just going to be using the choose LM method directly so that I can able to perform

42
00:02:39,010 --> 00:02:40,180
the operation there.

43
00:02:40,210 --> 00:02:40,660
Right.

That is the major difference between using the runnable lambda uh, method versus the shorthand, uh,

at chain annotation.

00:00:05,150 --> 00:00:11,210

4

chain itself.

6

8

9

prompt over here.

00:01:04,820 --> 00:01:12,110

15
00:01:12,110 --> 00:01:14,030

16

So the reason why this is modifying over here is because it now knows the context of my earlier question,

17

and it is able to perform the additional operation in my second question.

00:01:25,580 --> 00:01:27,890
