That's the reason why the second question, even though I did not ask a lot of details, I could able

20
00:01:33,020 --> 00:01:34,850
to just ask like a follow up question.

21
00:01:34,850 --> 00:01:40,910
This is true for most of the operations that we do in the ChatGPT over here, and we wanted to implement

22
00:01:40,910 --> 00:01:46,430
this similar kind of operation with our, uh, our application as well.

23
00:01:46,430 --> 00:01:49,550
But if I wanted to do that, you can see that for now.

24
00:01:49,550 --> 00:01:56,300
While we as a user ask any question from Lang Chain by passing a chat prompt template, and then if

25
00:01:56,300 --> 00:02:00,650
you send it to the LM, it can able to respond for the first time.

26
00:02:00,650 --> 00:02:07,580
And if you try to ask a follow up question, the model will not understand who you are and there won't

27
00:02:07,580 --> 00:02:11,990
be any response, as the large language model has got no context on it.

28
00:02:11,990 --> 00:02:19,130
So in order to resolve this particular problem of the LM with no context, we need to have what is called

29
00:02:19,130 --> 00:02:22,910
as the message history or the chat message history.

30
00:02:22,910 --> 00:02:30,560
So how do we really implement the chat message history and how we can make that happen in the long chain?

31
00:02:30,560 --> 00:02:35,780
Well, if you can see this particular diagram it will make you more sense over here.

32
00:02:35,780 --> 00:02:42,200
So as you can see, this particular user will have a session ID of 1 to 3, which is pretty much like

33
00:02:42,230 --> 00:02:44,240
how I did in the ChatGPT just now.

34
00:02:44,240 --> 00:02:50,300
So I will be sending a message to Long Chain and then as a prompt template I will be sending a message

35
00:02:50,300 --> 00:02:51,410
to queue model.

36
00:02:51,410 --> 00:02:53,720
It is just going to respond as as usual.

37
00:02:53,720 --> 00:02:56,530
But now the same user the session ID one two, three.

38
00:02:56,560 --> 00:03:02,020
If is going to be asking a follow up question, you will notice that this time the chat prompt template

39
00:03:02,020 --> 00:03:11,140
will go to the message history before it performs any action of sending the message to the model directly.

40
00:03:11,140 --> 00:03:17,740
So you'll notice that every single time before it's going to send any message to the local large language

41
00:03:17,740 --> 00:03:23,920
model, it is going to first get the context or the history information, and then it is going to perform

42
00:03:23,920 --> 00:03:24,550
the operation.

43
00:03:24,550 --> 00:03:31,510
So this way you will know that this particular model will always have a history information before it

44
00:03:31,510 --> 00:03:34,510
gets to answer the next question.

45
00:03:34,510 --> 00:03:37,330
So it could be a follow up question, or it could be a new question altogether.

46
00:03:37,330 --> 00:03:43,510
It doesn't matter, but we will be every single time going to the message history to get what is really

47
00:03:43,510 --> 00:03:46,990
the earlier question was before answering the new question.

48
00:03:46,990 --> 00:03:52,450
So if it is a follow up question, then LM will have the context of the question because it will have

49
00:03:52,450 --> 00:03:56,070
access to the earlier questions in the message history over here.

50
00:03:56,070 --> 00:04:03,630
And that's the reason why the RLM could able to serve the answer for you as a follow up questions answer

51
00:04:03,630 --> 00:04:06,180
which was not something possible earlier.

52
00:04:06,180 --> 00:04:08,820
And now you could able to achieve that over here.

53
00:04:08,820 --> 00:04:13,110
And that is exactly what we are going to be doing in this particular section.

54
00:04:13,110 --> 00:04:18,720
So we are going to see how we could able to achieve the message history operation in this particular

55
00:04:18,720 --> 00:04:19,260
section.

56
00:04:19,260 --> 00:04:25,830
So in order to do that, I have already created a simple chat history Section folder here, and also

57

created the the Jupyter notebook here.

58

59

60

62

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:05,930
So now we are going to start doing some more operation with our chatbot that we have got over here.

2
00:00:05,930 --> 00:00:10,880
So the first thing is I'm going to be performing a series of operation in here.

3
00:00:10,910 --> 00:00:11,120
Right.

4
00:00:11,270 --> 00:00:17,480
So we have to say like uh, hi, uh, chatbot, uh, how are you doing?

5
00:00:17,480 --> 00:00:21,800
So for instance, if a user is going to ask this question and if you're going to send a message over

6
00:00:21,800 --> 00:00:24,890
here, then you need to see a user message.

7
00:00:24,890 --> 00:00:29,570
And then you also need to get the response from the AI chatbot.

8
00:00:29,810 --> 00:00:32,270
And you need to see that particular message as well.

9
00:00:32,270 --> 00:00:38,750
And all these information needs to be stored in a, in a session, like a historical session, so that

10
00:00:38,750 --> 00:00:44,810
you can keep chatting with each other like you and chatbot can, uh, chat with each other so that you

11
00:00:44,810 --> 00:00:46,340
get the response that you're looking for.

12
00:00:46,370 --> 00:00:46,760
Right.

13
00:00:46,760 --> 00:00:53,000
So we already have those kind of operation in our earlier Section of the course while we were doing

14
00:00:53,000 --> 00:00:59,750
the, uh, the runnable, uh, with message history where we store all the historical information.

15
00:00:59,760 --> 00:01:05,460
But but again, in order for us to make use of that with a chatbot, it is going to be a bit tricky.

16
00:01:05,460 --> 00:01:08,310
So we have to do quite a lot of code change over here as well.

17

So let's see all of these one by one in this particular uh section.

18

So the first lecture, the first thing that we are going to be doing in this particular lecture is I'm

19

going to say, okay, if there is a prompt message coming up right, then just give a response now because

20

you got a prompt message.

21
00:01:28,770 --> 00:01:32,610
Let's first get the historical message and the chat information.

22
00:01:32,610 --> 00:01:38,820
So I'm going to get this history um dot invoke I'm going to call this particular method.

23

And again this is the same thing that we did in our last Section as well.

24
00:01:43,050 --> 00:01:49,860
So I'm going to probably copy this uh c dot c dot invoke over here I'm going to pass the prompt.

25
00:01:50,250 --> 00:01:52,230
Uh like what is the distance between the sun and moon.

26
00:01:52,230 --> 00:01:57,900
But we don't want to pass the hard coded value because we're going to get the prompt message from the

27
00:01:57,900 --> 00:01:59,470
chat input that we are passing in.

28
00:01:59,470 --> 00:02:02,470
So I'm going to say prompt over here.

29

And then the config is going to remain exactly the same.

30
00:02:05,740 --> 00:02:09,280
So config is equal to configurable of session id as session id.

31
00:02:09,280 --> 00:02:12,070
So at the moment I'm hardcoding the session id as well.

32
00:02:12,070 --> 00:02:17,200
But we are going to get the session ID eventually from the user during the runtime.

33
00:02:17,320 --> 00:02:17,710
Right.

34
00:02:17,740 --> 00:02:19,990
So that is the first thing that we have to do.

35
00:02:20,170 --> 00:02:28,210
And now I need to store this this information in the, in the street, which is the Streamlit as well,

36
00:02:28,630 --> 00:02:30,820
because that is where the session is maintained.

37
00:02:30,820 --> 00:02:32,110
So that you can see that in the UI.

38
00:02:32,140 --> 00:02:33,340
So how do I do that?

39
00:02:33,340 --> 00:02:42,220
There is a session state in the street or Streamlit where you can store all the session state in one

40
00:02:42,220 --> 00:02:46,750
of the array, and I'm just going to call that as the chat history.

41
00:02:46,780 --> 00:02:47,410
Right.

42
00:02:47,410 --> 00:02:54,220
And this chat history is one of the history that I am maintaining to store all my chat information.

43

So because I have created this chat history over here, I also need to initialize this particular chat

44
00:03:01,270 --> 00:03:02,320
history somewhere.

45

So how do I actually do that?

46
00:03:04,570 --> 00:03:18,880
I can probably just say st dot session state dot chat underscore history is equal to an empty array.

47
00:03:18,910 --> 00:03:19,210
Right.

48
00:03:19,240 --> 00:03:25,000
So this is the initialization that I'm going to be doing so that I can now start using this particular

49
00:03:25,000 --> 00:03:31,000
array to append with the value that I'm looking for.

50
00:03:31,000 --> 00:03:38,470
So basically what I'm going to be appending this time is I'm going to append the um two keys in here.

51
00:03:38,470 --> 00:03:46,120
The first one is the role where I'm going to say the role is going to be user, and I'm going to pass

52
00:03:46,120 --> 00:03:52,780
the content, which is going to be the prompt message, which I'm going to be passing in as a user.

53
00:03:52,780 --> 00:03:59,600
So essentially this is the first session history that you are going to be appending because you as a

54
00:03:59,600 --> 00:04:04,910
user are going to ask a question in here like, hi, how are you doing?

55
00:04:04,910 --> 00:04:07,640
So now this is a user prompt, right?

56
00:04:07,670 --> 00:04:11,090
This is a user question that you need to add in the history, so that the first thing that is going

57
00:04:11,120 --> 00:04:13,940
to appear on this particular UI is going to be the user.

58
00:04:13,940 --> 00:04:15,800
So the role in here is user.

59
00:04:15,800 --> 00:04:19,610
The content is going to be the prompt that you as a user is going to ask.

60
00:04:19,760 --> 00:04:29,510
And then we need to have another start session state dot uh probably chat history.

61
00:04:29,810 --> 00:04:40,910
Uh dot append over here I'm going to be appending the uh, probably the role as assistant or AI or whatever

62
00:04:40,940 --> 00:04:41,720
that you want to use.

63
00:04:41,720 --> 00:04:50,180
So I'm going to say the role as assistant, because this is going to come all the way from the, uh,

64
00:04:50,210 --> 00:04:51,410
from the AI.

65
00:04:51,440 --> 00:04:51,980
Right.

66
00:04:52,010 --> 00:04:54,920
So that's going to be another array.

67
00:04:55,190 --> 00:04:58,200
And we also need to pass the content there.

68
00:04:58,200 --> 00:05:02,850
So what is the content really which is going to be this particular response.

69
00:05:02,880 --> 00:05:03,120
Right.

70
00:05:03,150 --> 00:05:05,580
This is the content that I need to print over here.

71
00:05:05,580 --> 00:05:09,120
So I'm going to say content oops.

72
00:05:12,750 --> 00:05:21,420
Which is going to be the response that I'm going to be getting out from the large language model that

73
00:05:21,420 --> 00:05:22,770
we are getting in from here.

74
00:05:22,800 --> 00:05:23,220
Right.

75
00:05:23,250 --> 00:05:25,950
So that is what I'm going to be printing over here.

76

So now that we have did some code change, uh, save this code over here and let's try to run this and

77

And looks like we have did something wrong.

86

Like how we do that, like Street Dot, right.

So now that we have written something.

69
00:05:08,650 --> 00:05:10,990
to start a whole new conversation over here.

70
00:05:10,990 --> 00:05:22,390
And I'm not going to ask a question over here, like, how big, uh, is sun compared to moon?

71
00:05:22,390 --> 00:05:28,120
So if I ask this, you will notice that this time we're going to get a streaming response as opposed

72
00:05:28,120 --> 00:05:30,160
to like a chunk of message.

73
00:05:30,160 --> 00:05:34,900
It's going to be printed every single time immediately, like after everything is ready.

74
00:05:34,900 --> 00:05:40,250
So which is going to be taking a long lot of time because it is not going to give us, like all the

75
00:05:40,250 --> 00:05:41,720
information immediately.

76
00:05:41,720 --> 00:05:46,490
Rather, we have to wait for the methods to be ready from the LM.

77
00:05:47,240 --> 00:05:53,510
Right now we are getting a streaming response over here, so we can probably ask a follow up question

78
00:05:53,510 --> 00:05:54,590
as well over here.

79
00:05:54,650 --> 00:05:58,430
Uh, how about, uh, Earth over here?

80
00:05:58,430 --> 00:06:00,110
So look at that.

81
00:06:00,110 --> 00:06:05,630
Now it's comparing sun, moon and Earth, and it's giving me all the responses over here.

82
00:06:05,660 --> 00:06:11,750
How can you print them in tabular format.

83
00:06:13,670 --> 00:06:15,230
So look at that.

84
00:06:15,230 --> 00:06:18,710
Now it's printing in a tabular format for sun, moon and Earth.

85
00:06:18,710 --> 00:06:24,440
So you will notice that this time all the historical information like chat, historical information

86
00:06:24,440 --> 00:06:25,730
is being maintained.

87
00:06:25,730 --> 00:06:29,600
And it is all printed for us over here, which is pretty cool.

88
00:06:29,600 --> 00:06:33,740
So this ends our chatbot that we have to build.

89
00:06:33,770 --> 00:06:37,970
I know there is one thing that we have to still build, which is the cosmetic change that I showed you

90
00:06:37,970 --> 00:06:39,800
in the starting of this particular section.

91
00:06:39,920 --> 00:06:43,490
We'll be doing that in our next lecture of this course.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:07,220
So now that I'm going to show you how, I made a bit of a cosmetic change on the the UI that you actually

2
00:00:07,250 --> 00:00:11,390
saw in the starting of this particular, uh, section.

3
00:00:11,390 --> 00:00:17,630
Well, the only cosmetic change that I actually made was I have added probably what I'm going to do.

4
00:00:17,660 --> 00:00:20,300
I'm going to copy this, uh, this whole chatbot.

5
00:00:20,510 --> 00:00:29,990
I'm going to paste it over here and I'm going to say chatbot with cosmetic over here.

6
00:00:29,990 --> 00:00:32,960
And I'm going to start making the changes over here.

7
00:00:32,960 --> 00:00:38,360
So I'm going to probably just do, uh, like a copy paste of code that I have already written.

8
00:00:38,360 --> 00:00:43,760
And I can explain to you what changes that I have, I have actually made over there.

9
00:00:43,760 --> 00:00:50,990
But other than that, the code just remains pretty much exactly the same as opposed to what you have

10
00:00:50,990 --> 00:00:51,680
seen there.

11
00:00:51,680 --> 00:00:54,260
So probably I'm just going to do that.

12
00:00:54,260 --> 00:00:56,720
I'm gonna completely delete it.

13
00:00:56,750 --> 00:00:58,130
I'm going to paste it over here.

14
00:00:58,130 --> 00:01:02,710
So the code that you are seeing over here is exactly the same chord, so it's going to have the same

15
00:01:02,710 --> 00:01:03,400
session history.

16
00:01:03,430 --> 00:01:04,750
It's going to have the same thing.

17
00:01:04,750 --> 00:01:11,590
But only thing is I need a logo, which I probably need to copy, um, from my existing code once again.

18
00:01:11,590 --> 00:01:14,470
And I also need to paste it over here.

19
00:01:14,650 --> 00:01:18,970
So this is the execute automation logo, uh, which I have.

20
00:01:18,970 --> 00:01:20,140
So I'm going to paste that.

21
00:01:20,140 --> 00:01:23,710
And I'm actually going to call that the SD uh, image.

22
00:01:23,950 --> 00:01:28,180
Only thing I'm doing is I'm using a sidebar over here, the sidebar.

23
00:01:28,180 --> 00:01:33,550
And inside the sidebar I'm going to add an image and I'm getting the user input.

24
00:01:33,550 --> 00:01:40,420
And I'm getting the details like how, uh, the answer should be like beginner, expert and PhD.

25
00:01:40,480 --> 00:01:43,780
And I'm going to add an index of zero, which is the starting is beginner.

26
00:01:43,990 --> 00:01:49,000
And I'm going to add the same button like start new chat, uh, and all those things over here.

27
00:01:49,000 --> 00:01:55,450
And I'm also adding a markdown here just to show you like what can I help you today, which is going

28
00:01:55,480 --> 00:02:00,790
to show in the middle of the window, just to give you some more, like a ChatGPT experience.

29
00:02:01,000 --> 00:02:08,690
Uh, and finally, you can also notice that in the template I'm actually adding that you are an and

30
00:02:08,690 --> 00:02:13,520
I'm passing the role in here level user to answer this query.

31
00:02:13,520 --> 00:02:16,400
And then I'm passing in the human prompt.

32
00:02:16,400 --> 00:02:20,660
So this is the only change that I have made apart from what I had before.

33
00:02:20,660 --> 00:02:25,910
So you see that here we just have like human prompt and we had the placeholder of the history.

34
00:02:26,000 --> 00:02:31,160
Uh, but in here we have the placeholder that message placeholder of history over here.

35
00:02:31,310 --> 00:02:34,760
Uh, and we also have a system query.

36
00:02:34,790 --> 00:02:41,360
And the system message is saying that you are an, uh, PSG level or basic beginner level or expert

37
00:02:41,390 --> 00:02:45,350
level user to answer this query and answer this question.

38
00:02:45,350 --> 00:02:49,070
And the remaining just, uh, stays the same.

39
00:02:49,070 --> 00:02:51,230
There is no change on any of that.

40
00:02:51,530 --> 00:02:57,380
Uh, and now, because we have already gone through this code many times, I'm just going to say chat

41
00:02:57,410 --> 00:03:00,860
with, uh, chatbot with cosmetic over here.

42
00:03:00,860 --> 00:03:02,470
And you will see that Voila!

43
00:03:02,470 --> 00:03:04,990
We have got this new UI, just that.

44
00:03:04,990 --> 00:03:08,860
There is a sidebar pane and we have got this details over here.

45
00:03:08,860 --> 00:03:13,030
And the remaining functionality is just, uh, looks pretty much exactly the same.

46
00:03:13,060 --> 00:03:21,940
How big, uh, is, uh, sun compared to moon?

47
00:03:22,180 --> 00:03:24,550
So we get the response immediately over here.

48
00:03:24,550 --> 00:03:25,180
And look at that.

49
00:03:25,180 --> 00:03:27,280
This time the answer is also coming down below.

50
00:03:27,280 --> 00:03:37,870
Instead of having that, uh, in the top and also compare with Earth and Tabular, is it.

51
00:03:40,210 --> 00:03:41,020
So look at that.

52
00:03:41,020 --> 00:03:42,400
It's a stabilizing with Earth.

53
00:03:42,400 --> 00:03:45,640
And it's comparing the detail for me over here which is amazing.

54
00:03:45,640 --> 00:03:46,990
So it is already doing that.

55
00:03:46,990 --> 00:03:55,870
And if I ask the same question in the expert level or maybe in the PhD level, um, how about with Mars?

56
00:03:57,250 --> 00:04:02,080
And now this is going to go a bit more uh, and in the PhD level.

57
00:04:02,080 --> 00:04:06,320
So details are going to be a bit more, uh, bit more complex.

58
00:04:06,320 --> 00:04:11,120
But apparently it's not because we're just asking to compare with Mars over here.

59
00:04:11,390 --> 00:04:18,170
Uh, how about it's, uh, gases in each planet's?

60
00:04:19,340 --> 00:04:20,600
Explain me.

61
00:04:21,860 --> 00:04:23,660
And look at that.

62
00:04:23,660 --> 00:04:27,620
So now it is going bit more like a sea level.

63
00:04:27,620 --> 00:04:29,090
It's going to go deeper.

64
00:04:29,240 --> 00:04:31,130
And then it is comparing everything.

65
00:04:31,130 --> 00:04:33,950
And it's giving me the answer for every single planet.

66
00:04:33,950 --> 00:04:36,650
And there is the summary coming up over here as well.

67
00:04:36,650 --> 00:04:37,490
So see that.

68
00:04:37,520 --> 00:04:38,240
Look at that.

69
00:04:38,240 --> 00:04:44,570
We have successfully built our own chatbot which can run within our local machine.

70
00:04:44,570 --> 00:04:50,240
And also it's using our own local large language model to make this happen, which is amazing.

71
00:04:50,540 --> 00:04:55,820
So this ends this particular section, and I'm sure you have learned quite a lot of details of how you

72
00:04:55,820 --> 00:05:01,880
can build a chatbot from the complete ground up, and also use the chat message history discussion that

73
00:05:01,880 --> 00:05:03,530
we did in our last section.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

# Section 8: Upgrade to LangChain v1.0 - All new features and few breaking changes 


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,200 --> 00:00:06,360
Welcome to the next Section of our course Build and test AI agent tracks chatbots using Lang chain with

2
00:00:06,560 --> 00:00:08,680
Lama and local large language model.

3
00:00:09,120 --> 00:00:14,040
And in this Section we are going to talk about upgrading to Lang chain version 1.0.

4
00:00:14,440 --> 00:00:17,680
So you might think hey Karthik, what exactly is happening right now?

5
00:00:17,680 --> 00:00:20,640
Suddenly we are talking about Lang chain version 1.0.

6
00:00:20,760 --> 00:00:22,960
Why are we really upgrading it isn't it?

7
00:00:22,960 --> 00:00:25,600
Our code is already version 1.0.

8
00:00:25,760 --> 00:00:27,160
Well guess what you may be.

9
00:00:27,160 --> 00:00:35,160
Yes, your code might be version 1.0 because you just executed the code using the code base that you

10
00:00:35,160 --> 00:00:35,880
have got.

11
00:00:36,040 --> 00:00:43,640
But while I recorded this entire course, it was version 0.3. x, so it can be anything from there.

12
00:00:43,640 --> 00:00:49,520
And now during the time of this recording, the Lang chain has released a new version, version 1.0

13
00:00:49,560 --> 00:00:54,640
like 15 days before, and everything has drastically changed from then on.

14
00:00:54,680 --> 00:01:00,860
And that's the reason why I started upgrading this particular Lang chain version, because they have

15
00:01:00,860 --> 00:01:02,860
introduced a lot of new features.

16
00:01:02,860 --> 00:01:08,260
At the same time, they have also introduced quite some breaking changes, and that's the reason why

17
00:01:08,260 --> 00:01:14,780
we are going to talk about how we can actually upgrade our existing code with the new version of Lang

18
00:01:14,820 --> 00:01:19,220
Chain, and in your case, if you are using the source code from Section three, and if you're trying

19
00:01:19,220 --> 00:01:24,580
to run the requirements.txt file before you start installing it, it is going to be very, very easy.

20
00:01:24,620 --> 00:01:28,740
I mean, you are going to be restricted with only one version, which is the latest version of Lang

21
00:01:28,740 --> 00:01:30,060
chain version 1.0.

22
00:01:30,180 --> 00:01:30,900
But guess what?

23
00:01:30,900 --> 00:01:39,100
The lectures that I have recorded are of version 0.3. x, so it can fail while you are using version

24
00:01:39,100 --> 00:01:39,940
1.0.

25
00:01:40,020 --> 00:01:44,420
And that's the reason why in this particular Section I'm going to talk about what are the upgrades coming

26
00:01:44,460 --> 00:01:50,940
to your upcoming sections of this course, and what are the changes that you may expect, and how there

27
00:01:50,940 --> 00:01:56,460
are going to be duplicate videos of the same thing going to come up, but just that with different versions.

28
00:01:56,460 --> 00:02:00,440
For example, this particular course when it recorded was version 0.3.

29
00:02:00,600 --> 00:02:06,280
And there is going to be a subsequent lecture for the same feature, but with version 1.0, so that

30
00:02:06,280 --> 00:02:11,760
you can see that how it was in version 0.3 and how it is in version 1.0.

31
00:02:11,920 --> 00:02:13,960
So that's how things are going to happen.

32
00:02:14,000 --> 00:02:18,880
I know it's kind of pain, but it's not for all the sections and all the lectures, only few of them,

33
00:02:18,880 --> 00:02:21,760
which has really got those breaking changes.

34
00:02:21,760 --> 00:02:27,000
The moment you start realizing the pattern of what has really changed, you know how easy it is to fix

35
00:02:27,000 --> 00:02:29,760
all of them and then you can move on from there.

36
00:02:29,760 --> 00:02:33,760
But I don't really want to just put you in the bus saying that this is the version that you have to

37
00:02:33,800 --> 00:02:38,600
use, like version 0.3, while there is a new version already available in the market.

38
00:02:38,600 --> 00:02:43,040
That's the reason why I'm going to be covering everything from this particular Section as well.

39
00:02:43,080 --> 00:02:48,840
As that said, what exactly are the new feature of version 1.0?

40
00:02:49,680 --> 00:02:56,080
Well, Lang Chain has got many new features and some of the new features are simplified and unified.

41
00:02:56,120 --> 00:02:57,120
Namespace.

42
00:02:57,120 --> 00:03:06,130
Well, now the version 1.0 has simplified the namespace in a way that they can be more easy to recognize

43
00:03:06,130 --> 00:03:07,970
at the same time, more easy to understand.

44
00:03:07,970 --> 00:03:14,530
For example, Lang change agents is now going to be specific for the agents, and there is a lot of

45
00:03:14,530 --> 00:03:16,810
breaking changes happened in this agent.

46
00:03:16,810 --> 00:03:24,130
So while we talk about the agent sections like Section nine or something, you are going to see section

47
00:03:24,130 --> 00:03:30,970
ten or something where you are going to see how you can actually work with the new version of agent.

48
00:03:30,970 --> 00:03:35,130
And also you will see how I recorded with the old version of agent.

49
00:03:35,130 --> 00:03:40,810
So I'm going to put an obsolete tag in that particular lecture so that you don't really have to watch

50
00:03:40,810 --> 00:03:42,730
it if you don't really want it to watch that.

51
00:03:43,130 --> 00:03:49,610
And similarly now they have the Lang chain has improved the the package for the messages.

52
00:03:49,650 --> 00:03:54,610
Now the system message, human message, AI message, everything is going to sit under the lang change

53
00:03:54,650 --> 00:03:56,170
messages namespace.

54
00:03:56,170 --> 00:03:56,230
Space.

55
00:03:56,230 --> 00:03:57,710
Similarly long chain tool.

56
00:03:58,310 --> 00:04:02,150
There is also a re-exported long chain hyphen core as well.

57
00:04:02,190 --> 00:04:06,110
Same thing goes for the long chain message where you can find the tools and things.

58
00:04:06,190 --> 00:04:10,590
Similarly, there is a chat message and embedding message which is very specific for embedding and chat

59
00:04:10,630 --> 00:04:11,190
models.

60
00:04:11,310 --> 00:04:15,390
So yeah, that's how things are going to be there for the Namespacing.

61
00:04:15,390 --> 00:04:18,710
That's one of the feature that they have introduced in version 1.0.

62
00:04:19,750 --> 00:04:25,750
And then in version 1.0, they have also introduced creating agents with Middlewares and more focus

63
00:04:25,750 --> 00:04:32,350
on context engineering to giving the right amount of the data to the agents and to the large language

64
00:04:32,350 --> 00:04:32,670
model.

65
00:04:32,670 --> 00:04:38,390
This is one of the major change that they have did, and I'm going to talk about that separately in

66
00:04:38,390 --> 00:04:39,230
a separate video.

67
00:04:39,230 --> 00:04:40,630
So don't worry about it yet.

68
00:04:40,870 --> 00:04:42,830
And then content block property.

69
00:04:42,830 --> 00:04:44,510
This is another new feature.

70
00:04:44,750 --> 00:04:49,990
For instance, all these days while we have been working, in order to get the text out from a large

71
00:04:49,990 --> 00:04:53,310
language model, we use this dot content property.

72
00:04:53,310 --> 00:04:59,570
So we used to put that result dot content, and that gives you the content of that particular, um,

73
00:04:59,570 --> 00:05:03,810
the result from the large language model, uh, that how we were getting before.

74
00:05:03,810 --> 00:05:08,650
But now they have introduced this content block property, which is very helpful if our large language

75
00:05:08,650 --> 00:05:11,290
model is, uh, reasoning capable model.

76
00:05:11,290 --> 00:05:17,330
I know most of the model these days are reasoning enabled models, for example, the GPT model or the

77
00:05:17,330 --> 00:05:20,770
GPT models altogether, as well as the Claude Sonnet model.

78
00:05:20,770 --> 00:05:23,330
All these models are reasoning models.

79
00:05:23,330 --> 00:05:29,810
So the moment you set reasoning as true in the chat or llama, there you are going to see the reasoning

80
00:05:29,850 --> 00:05:32,530
information from that particular model as well.

81
00:05:32,570 --> 00:05:37,850
Like what exactly the model thought before it answered you the question, something like that.

82
00:05:37,850 --> 00:05:43,570
And if you are using a GPT models like OpenAI models, then there is even more control over the reasoning.

83
00:05:43,570 --> 00:05:50,050
For example, you can set the effort as medium and you can also set it to high and low over here.

84
00:05:50,090 --> 00:05:51,450
So there is an effort property.

85
00:05:51,490 --> 00:05:57,340
And you can also see there is a summary property where you can set the summary as auto concise or detailed.

86
00:05:57,340 --> 00:06:03,380
So that's how the level of control that you can do for the reasoning of the models in the OpenAI.

87
00:06:03,540 --> 00:06:09,180
So this is another change that they have introduced in this particular version of 1.0.

88
00:06:09,420 --> 00:06:14,420
And then the middleware again I'm going to talk about the middleware separately.

89
00:06:14,420 --> 00:06:19,500
But these are the changes that I really wanted to talk about the features that new features over there.

90
00:06:19,700 --> 00:06:24,780
But the breaking changes that we have got over here are these look at that.

91
00:06:24,780 --> 00:06:29,620
These are things that you have not encountered yet, but you are going to start encountering it the

92
00:06:29,620 --> 00:06:35,180
moment we are going to talk about these in our next Section of the course.

93
00:06:35,180 --> 00:06:37,860
So you are going to definitely encounter all these problems.

94
00:06:37,900 --> 00:06:43,340
For example, while we use the Lang change prompts import chat prompt template, it is not going to

95
00:06:43,340 --> 00:06:43,780
work.

96
00:06:43,780 --> 00:06:49,980
Now it has moved to lang chain underscore core dot prompts and import chat prompt template, something

97
00:06:49,980 --> 00:06:50,340
like that.

98
00:06:50,340 --> 00:06:51,580
That's how things are going to work.

99
00:06:51,580 --> 00:06:58,960
Similarly there is going to be a retrieval of relevant documents in our next section, but that's now

100
00:06:58,960 --> 00:06:59,560
obsolete.

101
00:06:59,600 --> 00:07:02,480
Now it is going to be an invoke method.

102
00:07:02,520 --> 00:07:06,760
And similarly there's going to be a long chain of import hub in our next section, which is also going

103
00:07:06,800 --> 00:07:12,040
to be, um, which is also going to be replaced to long chain hub import client, something like that.

104
00:07:12,040 --> 00:07:13,320
So these are the breaking changes.

105
00:07:13,320 --> 00:07:15,960
I'm going to talk about the breaking changes in our next section.

106
00:07:15,960 --> 00:07:22,680
But just on this Section I'm going to talk about some of the new features so that you can be aware that

107
00:07:22,720 --> 00:07:27,720
what changes that you can expect following the next sections of the course.

108
00:07:27,720 --> 00:07:33,480
So catch you in our next Section where we're going to talk about the similar flow that we have left

109
00:07:33,480 --> 00:07:34,640
in our last section.

110
00:07:34,640 --> 00:07:39,680
The flow is still going to remain from our next section, but just that there is going to be an version

111
00:07:39,720 --> 00:07:43,160
0.3 tag there, which means it's an older version.

112
00:07:43,160 --> 00:07:48,960
For the newer version, check out the next subsequent video where you're going to have all of the latest

113
00:07:48,960 --> 00:07:53,080
code that we have got with the breaking changes from Lang Chain.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:01,730
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,730 --> 00:00:08,300

3

applications.

4
00:00:09,080 --> 00:00:15,800
So basically one of the most powerful application enabled by the large language model is the sophisticated

5
00:00:15,800 --> 00:00:17,720
question and answering chatbots.

6
00:00:17,960 --> 00:00:25,100
So while we say sophisticated question and answering if you know that while you try to ask any of the

7
00:00:25,100 --> 00:00:31,490
questions to a large language model over here, you know that you are going to get the response based

8
00:00:31,490 --> 00:00:37,280
upon how this model is being trained by the large data set that it knows.

9
00:00:37,580 --> 00:00:43,760
But what if you are going to be asking a question which are specifically known to your company or your

10
00:00:43,760 --> 00:00:50,840
organization, and you are building a large language model application, especially for your company.

11
00:00:50,840 --> 00:00:57,650
So if you wanted to do these kind of operation, then you need to somehow feed the external source of

12
00:00:57,650 --> 00:01:00,500
data to the large language model.

13
00:01:00,530 --> 00:01:08,450
I mean, you can either fine tune the model and then you can make the data aware with that particular

14
00:01:08,450 --> 00:01:09,590
large language model.

15
00:01:09,590 --> 00:01:18,020
But instead of you doing this way, you can actually make use of the rag where you can supply the specific

16
00:01:18,020 --> 00:01:21,860
source of your information to your large language models.

17
00:01:21,860 --> 00:01:25,790
And that is what we are going to be doing in this particular Section as well.

18
00:01:25,790 --> 00:01:32,000
We are going to be building a retrieval augmented generation application, which is going to read the

19
00:01:32,000 --> 00:01:34,670
PDF files or external files.

20
00:01:34,670 --> 00:01:40,280
And then it is going to enable your large language model with that information.

21
00:01:40,280 --> 00:01:44,960
So it is going to be adding more intelligence and context to your large language model.

22
00:01:44,960 --> 00:01:48,680
So that is what we are going to be discussing in this particular section.

23
00:01:48,680 --> 00:01:54,290
And you can see that this particular Section we are going to discuss two most important part.

24
00:01:54,320 --> 00:01:57,170
The one is the extraction and indexing part.

25
00:01:57,170 --> 00:02:00,540
Another one is going to be the retrieval and generation part.

26
00:02:00,570 --> 00:02:05,490
So this is this both combined is called as actually a rag.

27
00:02:05,490 --> 00:02:10,380
But the one that we are going to be first doing is going to be on the extraction and indexing side.

28
00:02:10,380 --> 00:02:17,730
So you can notice that over here on the extraction and indexing part of it, we are going to be reading

29
00:02:17,730 --> 00:02:19,470
from some external files.

30
00:02:19,470 --> 00:02:21,810
And in this case it's going to be a PDF file.

31
00:02:21,810 --> 00:02:27,720
And then we are going to split these files into multiple chunks.

32
00:02:27,750 --> 00:02:32,970
Now you may ask like Karthik, why do we even need to split the files into multiple chunks?

33
00:02:32,970 --> 00:02:33,990
Well guess what?

34
00:02:34,020 --> 00:02:42,450
While we are going to have a quite a large set of data, we need to split the data into multiple chunks

35
00:02:42,450 --> 00:02:48,540
so that there will be a context window support for your large language model.

36
00:02:48,540 --> 00:02:56,160
And you may know that if you go to this particular link over here especially, it is about the lemma

37
00:02:56,160 --> 00:02:57,810
3.2 information.

38
00:02:57,810 --> 00:03:07,100
You can notice that this particular model can have a context length of around 128 K tokens, and this

39
00:03:07,100 --> 00:03:10,340
is the state of the art in their class on the device.

40
00:03:10,370 --> 00:03:15,890
It can use case can be used on the device like summarization, instruction following and other tasks.

41
00:03:15,890 --> 00:03:21,290
So these kinds of large language model itself supports 128 K of token.

42
00:03:21,290 --> 00:03:28,730
So if you're going to have a more sized data like large big size of data that you are going to be passing

43
00:03:28,730 --> 00:03:36,500
in as a question to these kind of models, then your model is going to struggle and it is going to have

44
00:03:36,500 --> 00:03:38,900
hard time finding the information that you're looking for.

45
00:03:38,900 --> 00:03:41,660
So maybe that information is not going to be even matching.

46
00:03:41,660 --> 00:03:43,610
What is your actual question?

47
00:03:43,610 --> 00:03:52,010
So if our document that we are trying to load has got a long length of data, then the context window

48
00:03:52,040 --> 00:03:56,600
will make the model to struggle to find the information for very long input.

49
00:03:56,600 --> 00:04:03,490
So because of this we need to split the files into smaller chunks, so that it can be easy for the large

50
00:04:03,490 --> 00:04:08,770
language model to find the similarities of the information that you are looking for.

51
00:04:08,770 --> 00:04:14,530
And once we split information into multiple chunks, which is the file data that we have got, we are

52
00:04:14,530 --> 00:04:19,180
then going to embed these data to vectors.

53
00:04:19,180 --> 00:04:20,410
As you can see over here.

54
00:04:20,410 --> 00:04:23,530
And this embedding part is one of the most important part as well.

55
00:04:23,530 --> 00:04:29,560
So this embedding can be done either by using your own technique or by using large language models.

56
00:04:29,590 --> 00:04:34,570
I mean, there are so many different large language models available which does the embedding part much,

57
00:04:34,570 --> 00:04:35,260
much easily.

58
00:04:35,260 --> 00:04:40,480
So we are going to be doing this embedding part, which is going to convert the text into vector dimension

59
00:04:40,480 --> 00:04:42,220
for that particular text.

60
00:04:42,220 --> 00:04:45,340
And then we are going to be storing into a database.

61
00:04:45,340 --> 00:04:48,130
And this database can be any of the database.

62
00:04:48,130 --> 00:04:54,430
It could be a vector based database like fires or chroma or even Azure Cosmos DB.

63
00:04:54,490 --> 00:04:54,910
There.

64
00:04:54,940 --> 00:04:57,510
There are so many database available for doing this operation.

65
00:04:57,510 --> 00:05:00,390
So we are going to be storing the data into this particular.

66
00:05:00,510 --> 00:05:01,200
Database.

67
00:05:01,200 --> 00:05:07,080
So I will also show you quickly like how this, uh, this embedding as well as this.

68
00:05:07,110 --> 00:05:09,750
Database that we are going to be using is going to be looking like.

69
00:05:09,750 --> 00:05:10,380
So if you're just going.

70
00:05:10,410 --> 00:05:13,830
To go to uh, the link chain.

71
00:05:14,100 --> 00:05:17,190
And if I'm going to search for, uh.

72
00:05:19,350 --> 00:05:29,580
F a I s or the files over here, you can see that this is the vector store available, uh, in the long

73
00:05:29,610 --> 00:05:30,060
chain.

74
00:05:30,060 --> 00:05:37,710
So if I stand for Facebook, AI similarity search is a library for efficient similarity search and clustering

75
00:05:37,710 --> 00:05:39,090
of dense vector.

76
00:05:39,090 --> 00:05:44,340
And it contains algorithm that search in a set of vectors of any size and up.

77
00:05:44,370 --> 00:05:50,220
One of the possibility does not fit in the Ram, and it includes supporting code evaluation and parameter

78
00:05:50,220 --> 00:05:50,790
tuning.

79
00:05:50,790 --> 00:05:52,950
That is what this file is all about.

80
00:05:52,950 --> 00:05:57,060
And if you go to this, uh, left hand side, you see that there are vector stores available.

81
00:05:57,060 --> 00:06:00,570
So you will notice that there are many different vector stores available.

82
00:06:00,570 --> 00:06:08,040
So it can be Astra DB, Vector Store, chroma Clickhouse or Databricks, Elastic Store, Search Firestore,

83
00:06:08,070 --> 00:06:11,970
In-memory Vector Store, Milvus and things of that nature.

84
00:06:11,970 --> 00:06:13,020
So there are so many of them.

85
00:06:13,020 --> 00:06:16,530
Even SQL server is also there to perform this operation.

86
00:06:16,530 --> 00:06:19,350
So that is what is the the offline version.

87
00:06:19,350 --> 00:06:23,490
And you can also use the cloud version of the same thing over here that I have got so many different

88
00:06:23,490 --> 00:06:28,770
providers for doing the same kind of operation, and we are going to be actually using what is called

89
00:06:28,770 --> 00:06:34,740
as chroma, because chroma seems to be like, uh, having all the support, like delta ID filtering,

90
00:06:34,740 --> 00:06:38,190
search by vector, search by score, async operation.

91

I mean we are going to use all the potentials anyways.

92

But but yeah, chroma seems to be a good fit.

00:06:43,470 --> 00:06:48,420
Uh, and if I just go and select this chroma, as you can see over here, the chroma is an AI native

open source vector database focused on developer productivity and happiness, which is great.

And the way you can use this database is available over here.

00:07:46,310 --> 00:07:46,460

For instance if you have a CSV Data like a large set of X data.

76
00:04:59,770 --> 00:05:04,720

00:05:04,720 --> 00:05:05,830

00:05:05,830 --> 00:05:09,340

00:05:09,340 --> 00:05:13,790

80
00:05:13,790 --> 00:05:20,570
in secure, isolated containers built for web automation and AI driven use cases.

81
00:05:20,750 --> 00:05:24,710
And most importantly, it will also do capture handling.

82
00:05:24,740 --> 00:05:30,080
It will automatically solve capture for streamline automated workflows, which is awesome.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:00,470
All right.

2
00:00:00,470 --> 00:00:09,080
So in this lecture we are going to talk about how we can now split this particular gigantic 253 pages

3
00:00:09,080 --> 00:00:19,550
document into, uh, into smaller chunks with a set of uh, set of indexes, probably, uh, and how

4
00:00:19,550 --> 00:00:21,110
we can actually achieve that.

5
00:00:21,110 --> 00:00:26,270
Well, in order for us to achieve this operation, we are going to use this, uh, this library from

6
00:00:26,270 --> 00:00:29,540
the long chain with a text splitter.

7
00:00:29,540 --> 00:00:37,190
So if you just go back to long chain over here and if you're going to search for, uh, text splitter,

8
00:00:37,220 --> 00:00:43,940
something like this, uh, you will notice that we have got, um, this text splitter over here.

9
00:00:43,940 --> 00:00:50,480
And these text splitters are basically going to be helping us to do, uh, a lot of text splitting that

10
00:00:50,480 --> 00:00:54,440
we wanted to do this split test and transform documents and things of that nature.

11
00:00:54,440 --> 00:00:59,090
And one of the text splitter that we are going to be using probably is going to be the recursive character

12
00:00:59,120 --> 00:01:00,170
text splitter.

13
00:01:00,170 --> 00:01:03,890
So that is what we are going to be using in this particular section.

14
00:01:03,890 --> 00:01:07,610
So I'm going to say uh, what is this one.

15
00:01:09,050 --> 00:01:12,440
A two hour text splitting.

16
00:01:12,440 --> 00:01:18,320
So I'm going to use the link chain text splitters.

17
00:01:18,320 --> 00:01:25,010
And I'm going to import a recursive character text splitter over here.

18
00:01:25,010 --> 00:01:33,710
So what I use this recursive text splitter I could able to split the text like how I wanted to.

19
00:01:33,740 --> 00:01:42,050
So I can say splitter is equal to recursive character text splitter.

20
00:01:42,050 --> 00:01:49,190
And over here I can now specify the chunk size, because I need to split the text into multiple chunks

21
00:01:49,190 --> 00:01:53,360
so I can split based on the chunk size.

22
00:01:53,360 --> 00:01:58,100
And I'm going to say every chunk needs to be of 1000 characters.

23
00:01:58,280 --> 00:02:05,420
And then I also need to have a chunk overlap over there.

24
00:02:05,420 --> 00:02:07,640
And the overlap is going to be 200.

25
00:02:07,640 --> 00:02:08,750
So what does that really mean?

26
00:02:08,750 --> 00:02:18,150
Basically, you are going to have a chunk of 1000 characters in one of your chunk, and you are going

27
00:02:18,180 --> 00:02:22,380
to have another chunk as well starting from 1001.

28
00:02:22,380 --> 00:02:29,670
But while these texts are going to be split into multiple chunks, there are chances that you may lose

29
00:02:29,670 --> 00:02:33,840
some of the text from exact position where it is starting.

30
00:02:33,840 --> 00:02:41,130
So it is always wise to have a overlap so that even if there is a duplicate wordings or duplicate characters

31
00:02:41,130 --> 00:02:43,560
in two chunks, that doesn't matter.

32
00:02:43,560 --> 00:02:50,640
But your large language model will have more context when it tries to combine those chunks together.

33
00:02:50,640 --> 00:02:57,840
So that's the reason why we need to have this chunks overlap, so that it knows that this is the continuation

34
00:02:57,840 --> 00:03:00,570
of the actual context that it is working with.

35
00:03:00,570 --> 00:03:06,420
And I'm also going to add a start index, uh, which is going to be true.

36
00:03:06,420 --> 00:03:11,520
And this is important that we need to do because we will know what is the index that we're starting

37
00:03:11,520 --> 00:03:11,940
with.

38
00:03:11,970 --> 00:03:12,600
All right.

39
00:03:12,600 --> 00:03:17,730
So these are the things that we are going to have in the recursive character test text split.

40
00:03:17,820 --> 00:03:21,540
And finally I'm going to do the splitting of the documentation.

41
00:03:21,540 --> 00:03:28,900
So I'm going to say all splits is equal to text splitter dot split the document.

42
00:03:28,900 --> 00:03:33,430
So you see that this particular method split document is from this particular text splitter.

43
00:03:33,790 --> 00:03:35,980
And I need to split which document.

44
00:03:35,980 --> 00:03:38,230
Which is this guy the documents that we have got.

45
00:03:38,230 --> 00:03:43,210
So I'm going to say documents that's going to split the entire documents for me.

46
00:03:43,210 --> 00:03:48,910
And now we can just say how much the length of all these particular document is all about.

47
00:03:48,910 --> 00:03:52,390
So I'm going to say all splits and let's try to run that.

48
00:03:52,480 --> 00:03:55,000
This is going to be 640.

49
00:03:55,030 --> 00:04:06,940
You see that now we have splitted this this document of 253 into 640 different, uh, splits and main

50
00:04:06,970 --> 00:04:07,450
chunks.

51
00:04:07,450 --> 00:04:13,870
So each chunks is going to have quite a lot of data in there.

52
00:04:13,870 --> 00:04:16,660
And as I told you, there are like 1000 characters in there.

53
00:04:16,660 --> 00:04:22,900
So it's going to be, um, it's going to be stored in every single chunks for you.

54
00:04:23,050 --> 00:04:26,650
And now we are pretty good to go with this particular chunk.

55
00:04:26,650 --> 00:04:33,460
We can then finally start doing the embedding part of all these chunked data that we have got.

00:00:16,790 --> 00:00:17,780
That's how it does.

6
00:00:17,810 --> 00:00:21,410
And it is more general than a vector store.

7

00:00:28,700 --> 00:00:35,540
create can be created from vector store, but are also broad enough to include a Wikipedia search and

9

And I'm going to say prompt template which is going to be acting as an input for the large language

72

model.

73
00:05:23,080 --> 00:05:26,080
And get me the output in the string parser.

74
00:05:26,110 --> 00:05:30,010
These are things that we did before in our earlier lectures of this course.

00:05:30,220 --> 00:05:32,560
Um, so I'm going to do that over here.

76
00:05:32,560 --> 00:05:34,390
And of course, you know what we need to do.

77
00:05:34,390 --> 00:05:41,440
So I'm going to say, uh, response is equal to chain dot.

78
00:05:41,470 --> 00:05:43,390
You know what it is because it's a runnable.

79
00:05:43,390 --> 00:05:45,940
So we need to call this invoke method.

80
00:05:45,940 --> 00:05:48,460
And over here we need to pass two things right.

81
00:05:48,490 --> 00:05:50,350
The first one is the context.

82
00:05:50,350 --> 00:05:52,600
And the next one is going to be the question.

83
00:05:52,600 --> 00:05:54,040
So these are two things that we need to pass.

84
00:05:54,040 --> 00:06:04,150
So I'm going to say context uh which is going to be the context text that we have got which is this

85
00:06:04,150 --> 00:06:04,720
one.

86
00:06:04,870 --> 00:06:13,240
And we also need to pass the Question, which is going to be the query that we have got, which is the

87
00:06:13,240 --> 00:06:13,600
question.

88
00:06:13,630 --> 00:06:13,900
Right.

89
00:06:13,930 --> 00:06:16,060
So that is the question.

90
00:06:16,630 --> 00:06:20,200
And now we will see what is going to be the response.

91
00:06:20,230 --> 00:06:23,950
So I'm going to say print response over here.

92
00:06:24,280 --> 00:06:25,060
Look at that.

93
00:06:25,060 --> 00:06:31,660
That is our actual code which is going to be retrieving the relevant documents.

94
00:06:31,660 --> 00:06:37,270
And then it's going to stitch all of them together, which is going to be acting as a context text.

95
00:06:37,270 --> 00:06:41,320
And then we are going to pass it to our prompt template for the given context.

96
00:06:41,320 --> 00:06:43,930
And then we're going to ask the question and we're going to get the answer.

97
00:06:43,930 --> 00:06:46,360
So let's see what is going to basically happen.

98
00:06:47,500 --> 00:06:48,610
Oh look at that.

What is this.

00:06:49,630 --> 00:06:51,520

00:06:58,480 --> 00:07:00,190

00:07:04,270 --> 00:07:05,920

00:07:14,900 --> 00:07:15,260

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,120
All right.

2
00:00:01,200 --> 00:00:08,120
This is the first lecture where you're going to see the breaking change of the long chain version 1.0.

3
00:00:08,240 --> 00:00:14,560
So, so far until our last lecture, we have seen everything in version 0.3, which was the one that

4
00:00:14,560 --> 00:00:15,440
I recorded.

5
00:00:15,440 --> 00:00:20,080
But you are using the version 1.0 until your last lecture as well.

6
00:00:20,120 --> 00:00:23,480
Like because the code itself is version 1.0, that is not a problem.

7
00:00:23,480 --> 00:00:29,720
But starting now, which I wrote the code there, it might not have worked for you or it won't work

8
00:00:29,720 --> 00:00:33,760
for you because the recent version of the long chain has screwed that.

9
00:00:33,760 --> 00:00:37,200
So how do you fix this problem that we have got over here?

10
00:00:37,240 --> 00:00:43,440
See, now the the there is going to be an error here saying that the long chain dot prompt could not

11
00:00:43,440 --> 00:00:44,480
be resolved.

12
00:00:44,640 --> 00:00:50,920
And this is happening because there is a breaking change, uh, in the long chain that we need to resolve.

13
00:00:51,200 --> 00:00:55,760
And the way that we can resolve this particular issue is very, very straightforward.

14
00:00:55,960 --> 00:00:58,280
All you have to do it is over here.

15
00:00:58,480 --> 00:01:06,420
Just say lang chain Underscore core and then dot prompts of import chat prompts, which means it is

16
00:01:06,420 --> 00:01:07,260
going to work.

17
00:01:07,260 --> 00:01:09,860
That's the only change in the code that we have to do.

18
00:01:10,020 --> 00:01:14,660
And this is the breaking change which has happened in the version 1.2, unfortunately.

19
00:01:15,540 --> 00:01:20,420
And there is one more change which has happened is that we were using the retriever dot get relevant

20
00:01:20,420 --> 00:01:24,540
document to get the details of the document, but now we can't.

21
00:01:25,020 --> 00:01:34,340
Instead, we have to use the dot invoke method that we always use while we try to perform any of the

22
00:01:34,380 --> 00:01:38,180
chain invocation or large language model invocation, like how we did here.

23
00:01:38,220 --> 00:01:40,540
The same thing goes for the retriever as well.

24
00:01:40,740 --> 00:01:47,340
That is another way of how the long chain team has streamlined the process of invocation.

25
00:01:47,340 --> 00:01:47,700
That's it.

26
00:01:47,700 --> 00:01:48,780
That's the only change.

27
00:01:49,060 --> 00:01:53,420
And now if you try to run this particular code, and because I'm running this code for the very first

28
00:01:53,420 --> 00:01:58,860
time in my machine, at least because I've just downloaded it from the source code.

29
00:01:58,960 --> 00:01:59,440
There.

30
00:01:59,480 --> 00:01:59,920
You see that?

31
00:01:59,920 --> 00:02:04,440
Now it's just installing the Ipy kernel package for me there.

32
00:02:04,920 --> 00:02:06,360
But let that be there.

33
00:02:06,480 --> 00:02:07,800
I'm trying to run this particular code.

34
00:02:07,800 --> 00:02:08,960
It is just going to fail.

35
00:02:08,960 --> 00:02:13,320
The reason why this is failing is because I need to run all the code from the start.

36
00:02:13,360 --> 00:02:14,760
If not, this is not going to work.

37
00:02:14,760 --> 00:02:17,400
So let me just run all of them one by one.

38
00:02:18,160 --> 00:02:21,400
Uh, over here, uh, the extraction as well.

39
00:02:21,800 --> 00:02:27,320
And the splitting operation, embedding operations and the vector stores.

40
00:02:27,720 --> 00:02:28,280
All right.

41
00:02:28,280 --> 00:02:30,480
And then I'm going to run this guy.

42
00:02:31,280 --> 00:02:31,800
Okay.

43
00:02:32,040 --> 00:02:38,960
And the retriever, uh, and finally, if I'm going to run this document retrieval, uh, manually one

44
00:02:38,960 --> 00:02:39,600
more time.

45
00:02:39,600 --> 00:02:44,400
So this time hopefully this is not going to fail, but it's just going to work.

46
00:02:44,400 --> 00:02:46,040
So let's give it some time.

47
00:02:46,240 --> 00:02:48,600
And you can see that the code just works as expected.

48
00:02:48,600 --> 00:02:53,440
So this is the breaking change which has introduced this time over here in this particular piece of

49
00:02:53,440 --> 00:02:54,040
code.

50
00:02:54,040 --> 00:02:58,640
So if you ever see that there is a squiggly line there this video should help.

51
00:02:58,640 --> 00:02:59,320
Thank you.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:00,740
All right.

So now we're going to see how we can just make use of some third party prompt from the from the hub

of the lamp chain, instead of us writing all these prompts over here.

I mean, this is the prompt which I have written inspired from one of the third party prompts, as you

5

can see over here.

So look at that, the rack prompt.

This is the prompt that I just used like inspired.

8
00:00:24,140 --> 00:00:29,960
From here you can just download this prompt from using the lamp chain hub.

9
00:00:29,960 --> 00:00:32,600
And you can pull this particular prompt.

10
00:00:32,600 --> 00:00:36,470
And then you can use this particular prompt just by just one line of code over here.

11
00:00:36,470 --> 00:00:38,330
This is also something very much possible.

12
00:00:38,330 --> 00:00:43,160
So if you just go to the hub over here, you can see that there are different types of prompts available.

13
00:00:43,160 --> 00:00:44,660
You can also create your own prompt.

14
00:00:44,660 --> 00:00:46,070
You can upload it to them.

15
00:00:46,190 --> 00:00:50,600
And yeah there are so many different prompts available like text to SQL for that matter.

So you see that how this prompt has been written over here.

17

Given an input question, first create a synthetic correct dialect query to return to run.

18

Then look at the result and query and return the answer.

19
00:01:02,510 --> 00:01:04,040
The question is here.

20

This is the answer, blah blah blah.

21

This is the table info.

22

This is the few shot examples and stuff.

23
00:01:08,570 --> 00:01:10,040
So look at that.

24
00:01:10,040 --> 00:01:16,250
This is how these kind of, uh, these kinds of, uh, prompts are being created.

25
00:01:16,250 --> 00:01:18,290
And the one that we are going to be using is this guy.

26
00:01:18,320 --> 00:01:21,650
It's downloaded like 21.8 million times, which is amazing.

27
00:01:21,650 --> 00:01:23,870
So I'm going to use this particular prompt.

28
00:01:23,870 --> 00:01:27,290
So for that we just need to use these two lines of code.

29
00:01:27,290 --> 00:01:34,610
So I'm going to go to our VS code over here uh and probably copy this.

30
00:01:34,610 --> 00:01:46,040
And I'm going to paste markdown here uh using uh Lang Chain Hub for prompt.

31
00:01:46,070 --> 00:01:48,230
Write the same code.

32
00:01:48,320 --> 00:01:56,090
Just that this is going to be instead of the chat prompt template, I'm going to just use uh lang chain.

33
00:01:56,090 --> 00:01:59,540
And I'm going to import hub.

34
00:02:00,620 --> 00:02:07,560
And instead of this prompt that we have written over here like this big prompt, I can just say prompt

35
00:02:07,590 --> 00:02:13,890
is equal to, uh, hub dot, pull this particular prompt, that's all.

36
00:02:13,890 --> 00:02:17,880
This is all I have to do over here for the prompt.

37
00:02:17,880 --> 00:02:19,080
I'm going to be passing in.

38
00:02:19,230 --> 00:02:21,990
I'm going to pass the prompt over here and look at that.

39
00:02:21,990 --> 00:02:26,340
For this particular prompt you need you still have the question and the context over here.

40
00:02:26,340 --> 00:02:30,630
So it's going to just match whatever that we are passing in in the invocation part.

41
00:02:30,630 --> 00:02:33,540
So that's going to remain exactly the same.

42
00:02:33,540 --> 00:02:37,080
And I'm going to run this particular code.

43
00:02:37,080 --> 00:02:42,900
And you see that I'm going to get the response over here which is amazing.

44
00:02:42,900 --> 00:02:49,770
So this is how you can see that we can uh, we can use the chain hub, uh, hub to get the prompt over

45
00:02:49,770 --> 00:02:50,370
here.

46
00:02:51,390 --> 00:02:58,800
In our next lecture, I will show you how we can use one more way to retrieve, uh, the, uh, retrieve

the information from our query, our large language model using retrieval QA.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:00,480
Okay.

2
00:00:00,520 --> 00:00:05,280
So this is the next change that we're going to talk about in terms of the Lang chain hub for prompt.

3
00:00:05,280 --> 00:00:10,840
So basically in our last video you might have seen how I wrote the code with the Lang Chain hub to import

4
00:00:10,840 --> 00:00:12,000
the the query.

5
00:00:12,160 --> 00:00:18,520
But this code is not going to work anymore because you can't use the hub dot pull method to import the

6
00:00:18,520 --> 00:00:20,320
prompt that you are seeing over here.

7
00:00:20,320 --> 00:00:25,840
It is not like this way that you need to do moving forward with a new version of the changes.

8
00:00:25,960 --> 00:00:32,040
We can't really use the Lang chain import hub over here, so you know that retriever.

9
00:00:32,040 --> 00:00:37,000
You can't use the get Relevant document like how we did the change uh, earlier.

10
00:00:37,000 --> 00:00:38,920
We need to use the invoke method.

11
00:00:38,920 --> 00:00:41,080
So I'm going to call this invoke method.

12
00:00:41,120 --> 00:00:42,320
That remains fine.

13
00:00:42,480 --> 00:00:46,400
But the moment if I'm going to run this particular code over here, it's going to throw an error saying

14
00:00:46,400 --> 00:00:49,880
that Lang Chain import hub is going to throw you an exception.

15
00:00:49,920 --> 00:00:55,640
Well, this is happening because this Lang chain, uh, import hub doesn't even exist anymore.

16
00:00:55,880 --> 00:00:59,120
So you have to use what is called as Lang chain hub.

17
00:00:59,560 --> 00:01:03,320
And then you need to import a class called as client.

18
00:01:03,320 --> 00:01:06,430
So this is what we should be using moving forward.

19
00:01:06,750 --> 00:01:12,070
And similarly, if we are going to use the client over here, the code will change as well.

20
00:01:12,070 --> 00:01:15,830
So we need to use something like hub is equal to client.

21
00:01:15,830 --> 00:01:18,950
So we need to create an instance of this particular client over here.

22
00:01:19,230 --> 00:01:24,110
And then we're going to use the hub dot pull from here which is going to still work.

23
00:01:24,110 --> 00:01:25,430
So that is fine.

24
00:01:25,430 --> 00:01:28,230
So this is going to be the way that it's going to do.

25
00:01:28,230 --> 00:01:36,430
But the other bad news is that with the hub dot pull method these days, the Lang chain team has made

26
00:01:36,430 --> 00:01:39,590
this to return you like a JSON value.

27
00:01:39,590 --> 00:01:46,430
So we have to load this as a JSON instead of directly loading this as a prompt over here.

28
00:01:46,590 --> 00:01:48,310
So this is another change.

29
00:01:48,310 --> 00:01:49,270
Sorry about that.

30
00:01:49,270 --> 00:01:51,310
We definitely need to do that.

31
00:01:51,310 --> 00:01:55,190
So I'm going to do import uh JSON.

32
00:01:55,510 --> 00:02:04,270
And I also need to import use an um class called as Lang chain core dot.

33
00:02:04,310 --> 00:02:10,670
And there is a class called load where I need this load class.

34
00:02:10,670 --> 00:02:12,540
So this is something that I need as well.

35
00:02:12,540 --> 00:02:19,580
So this is an, uh, this is a function that I need to use to load the JSON before I can start getting

36
00:02:19,580 --> 00:02:20,100
the prompt.

37
00:02:20,100 --> 00:02:24,700
So essentially this particular prompt that you are seeing over here that we got in our last video is

38
00:02:24,700 --> 00:02:26,820
not the prompt that we can directly use it.

39
00:02:27,020 --> 00:02:32,060
Uh, we're going to call this as something like the prompt data over here.

40
00:02:32,500 --> 00:02:40,940
And now we need to, uh, deserialize this to, uh, the way that we can get from that particular prompt.

41
00:02:40,940 --> 00:02:45,860
So I'm going to say prompt, underscore, dict like a dictionary.

42
00:02:46,060 --> 00:02:47,860
And then I'm going to say load.

43
00:02:48,260 --> 00:02:55,780
Uh and there is no from unfortunately that's the reason why I actually need to use this JSON dot load.

44
00:02:55,780 --> 00:02:57,460
And then the prompt data.

45
00:02:57,460 --> 00:03:01,820
So this is how, um, I'm going to be, uh, doing it from here.

46
00:03:01,860 --> 00:03:02,020
Oops.

47
00:03:02,020 --> 00:03:02,260
Sorry.

48
00:03:02,260 --> 00:03:05,420
I just put from here for some reason I said import.

49
00:03:05,700 --> 00:03:06,020
Yeah.

50
00:03:06,020 --> 00:03:07,500
So JSON dot load.

51
00:03:07,500 --> 00:03:10,780
So this is going to load the prompt data that we are getting from the hub.

52
00:03:10,780 --> 00:03:14,140
And once you get this particular uh dictionary of element.

53
00:03:14,140 --> 00:03:19,610
And then we are going to use this particular load method to to load this particular dictionary.

54
00:03:19,610 --> 00:03:20,970
So this is how this is going to work.

55
00:03:20,970 --> 00:03:26,130
So basically I'm going to say prompt is equal to load of this particular prompt.

56
00:03:26,290 --> 00:03:32,010
Uh dictionary of data that I'm actually getting it from the JSON dot load over here.

57
00:03:32,330 --> 00:03:34,050
So these are the changes that I have to do.

58
00:03:34,050 --> 00:03:36,850
So I have to use the client.

59
00:03:37,130 --> 00:03:42,250
And then I need to use the JSON as well as the the load method to perform this operation.

60
00:03:42,250 --> 00:03:49,490
So I try to keep it as straightforward as possible, as much aligned uh, with the hub discussion that

61
00:03:49,490 --> 00:03:50,490
we did earlier.

5

to invoke, uh, and then get the response for you from the large language model.

6

7
00:00:27,520 --> 00:00:31,280

8
00:00:31,520 --> 00:00:32,920

00:00:32,960 --> 00:00:35,800

00:00:35,840 --> 00:00:39,280

11
00:00:39,320 --> 00:00:40,040

00:00:40,040 --> 00:00:42,320

13
00:00:42,480 --> 00:00:43,200

14
00:00:43,440 --> 00:00:46,240

15
00:00:46,240 --> 00:00:53,960
So the way that you can fix this particular code, uh, is you need to create your own perfect, uh,

16
00:00:53,960 --> 00:00:59,320
system prompts as well as the, the runnable in order to make this happen.

17
00:00:59,320 --> 00:01:05,700
happened because as you see over here, we are using the from chain type of the retrieval QA to create

18
00:01:05,740 --> 00:01:10,700

19
00:01:10,700 --> 00:01:17,460

20
00:01:17,460 --> 00:01:18,620
much like the chaining operation.

21
00:01:18,620 --> 00:01:18,980
Like what?

22
00:01:18,980 --> 00:01:24,140
This particular retrieval QA is doing it for us, but we can write it in a custom way this time.

23
00:01:24,140 --> 00:01:26,180
So I'm going to get rid of this entire code.

24
00:01:26,380 --> 00:01:28,700
Uh, we can't even use this anymore.

25
00:01:28,900 --> 00:01:33,620
Uh, and let's, um, let's start writing the code over here.

26
00:01:33,620 --> 00:01:39,300
So I'm just going to copy paste the code, uh, over here instead of me writing it again, because we

27
00:01:39,300 --> 00:01:41,260
have already discussed about the runnable QA.

28
00:01:41,380 --> 00:01:44,420
But the thing is, this code is very straightforward and simple.

29
00:01:44,620 --> 00:01:50,580
All we have to do over here is that we are going to use the same core prompts with chat prompt template

30
00:01:50,820 --> 00:01:54,100
output parsers, which is the string output parser that we discussed before.

31
00:01:54,380 --> 00:02:00,030
And we are using a runnable pass through like how we discussed earlier as well in the chainable in the

32
00:02:00,030 --> 00:02:01,030
chaining and runners.

33
00:02:01,310 --> 00:02:06,510
And just that over here, I'm going to be passing the system prompt here saying you are an assistant

34
00:02:06,510 --> 00:02:07,950
of question answering task.

35
00:02:08,470 --> 00:02:11,710
Use the following piece of retrieval context to answer the question.

36
00:02:11,710 --> 00:02:15,390
If you don't know the answer, say that you don't know.

37
00:02:15,870 --> 00:02:19,670
Use three sentence maximum and keep the answer concise.

38
00:02:19,950 --> 00:02:25,310
So this is the system prompt that I'm writing so that I can get the correct response, like a concise

39
00:02:25,310 --> 00:02:27,470
response every time I'm going to look at it.

40
00:02:27,470 --> 00:02:33,510
And also I'm going to pass the context over here so that now while I use the system prompt, I can just

41
00:02:33,510 --> 00:02:37,750
pass it in the chat prompt template, as you know how we did it earlier.

42
00:02:38,270 --> 00:02:40,750
And once we pass this, this becomes a prompt.

43
00:02:40,950 --> 00:02:46,190
And now I can use this prompt to be passed in the chain over here.

44
00:02:46,230 --> 00:02:46,790
Look at that.

45
00:02:46,790 --> 00:02:52,430
Now we're building a chain instead of the, uh, the, the QA chain that we used from the retrieval

46
00:02:52,430 --> 00:02:52,790
QA.

47
00:02:53,150 --> 00:02:59,410
Uh, and now we have our own chain over here with the context which is retriever.

48
00:02:59,610 --> 00:03:04,330
And I'm going to format the documents using this particular code like how we did earlier.

49
00:03:04,690 --> 00:03:07,650
And then I'm passing the rag chain over here.

50
00:03:07,850 --> 00:03:08,850
So this is the code.

51
00:03:08,850 --> 00:03:14,810
So we have written a custom retriever QA ourself this time because now the retrieval keyword doesn't

52
00:03:14,810 --> 00:03:15,610
exist anymore.

53
00:03:15,850 --> 00:03:20,610
Now we are in a situation to write our own runnable QA to get this particular answer.

54
00:03:20,770 --> 00:03:25,610
And you can see that the code just works like how it was working before, but just that this is our

55
00:03:25,610 --> 00:03:30,010
own custom runnable QA this time like a retriever QA.

56
00:03:30,050 --> 00:03:37,250
This time, instead of using uh that, we are creating a custom retriever QA over here.

57
00:03:37,250 --> 00:03:42,770
So I'm going to say, uh, retrieving data using retriever QA is obsolete.

58
00:03:43,290 --> 00:03:47,130
Uh, now using the chain instead.

59
00:03:47,410 --> 00:03:50,970
Uh, so I'm going to hit okay there.

60
00:03:50,970 --> 00:03:55,660
So that's what is going to be the the working change of version 1.0.

61
00:03:55,700 --> 00:03:56,220
Over here.

62
00:03:56,220 --> 00:03:58,660
So I'm going to add the, um, the.

63
00:04:00,700 --> 00:04:07,380
Breaking changes in long chain version on dot or something like that.

64
00:04:07,740 --> 00:04:15,220
Uh, and I'm going to do the same thing for wherever places that we have actually, uh, tried doing

65
00:04:15,220 --> 00:04:16,300
it over here.

66
00:04:16,300 --> 00:04:26,660

67
00:04:27,180 --> 00:04:27,820
Right.

68
00:04:27,820 --> 00:04:29,740
So I'm going to put that over here as well.

69
00:04:29,900 --> 00:04:31,380
And I hope everything is done.

71

00:04:41,300 --> 00:04:46,820

74
00:04:46,820 --> 00:04:47,980
once again over there.

# Section 10: Tools and Function Calling with LLMs

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:01,730
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,730 --> 00:00:06,620
And in this Section we are going to talk about understanding toolings in large language models.

3
00:00:07,040 --> 00:00:13,730
So tooling is another great concept of the large language model, which can help your large language

4
00:00:13,730 --> 00:00:19,460
model to use external tools and libraries while it performs any action.

5
00:00:20,000 --> 00:00:26,300
I know it sounds crazy that LM is going to use some other external tools to perform an operation, but

6
00:00:26,300 --> 00:00:28,610
the tools are going to be very, very essential.

7
00:00:28,610 --> 00:00:37,460
While you are going to give your LM an extra leverage or mileage to go and search for external data,

8
00:00:37,490 --> 00:00:43,280
probably, or even going to go and perform an action which your large language model is not trained

9
00:00:43,280 --> 00:00:43,790
for.

10
00:00:43,820 --> 00:00:45,740
That is what is this tooling is all about.

11
00:00:45,740 --> 00:00:49,130
So for instance this is a simple example that you can see here.

12
00:00:49,160 --> 00:00:54,350
I'm going to ask like did Donald Trump won the 2024 presidential election.

13
00:00:54,470 --> 00:01:00,470
And if I'm going to pass this via Lang chain with our prompt, and if you're going to ask it to the

14
00:01:00,860 --> 00:01:09,310
model, your model is not going to be responding unless until it has trained with 2024 data in it.

15
00:01:09,310 --> 00:01:15,790
I'm sure most of the models today in 2020 5th February, none of the model has actually been trained

16
00:01:15,790 --> 00:01:17,350
with 2024 data.

17
00:01:17,350 --> 00:01:22,390

00:02:35,350 --> 00:02:37,570

binding for your large language model.

00:03:05,800 --> 00:03:06,970

46
00:03:06,970 --> 00:03:09,070
Donald Trump won the election in 2024.

00:03:09,310 --> 00:03:13,480
Based on the prompt, how you have written over here, the answer is going to be summarized and it's

48
00:03:13,480 --> 00:03:15,580
going to give an up to you over here.

49
00:03:15,610 --> 00:03:17,670
And that is what is the tooling support guys.

50
00:03:17,700 --> 00:03:19,020
And now you got the idea right.

51
00:03:19,050 --> 00:03:24,300
This this is going to give an extra mileage to your large language model with the details that you're

52
00:03:24,300 --> 00:03:29,640
looking for, which is something not your large language model is already trained with.

53
00:03:29,670 --> 00:03:34,380
If your large language model has got no context of that particular question that you're asking, then

54
00:03:34,380 --> 00:03:41,580
this model is going to go and search for the available tool it has got, and then it is going to generate

55
00:03:41,580 --> 00:03:44,190
the answer for you, and it's going to give it to you back.

56
00:03:44,220 --> 00:03:46,470
And that is the power of the tooling support.

57
00:03:46,500 --> 00:03:53,160
So about this tooling we just go to Lang Chain.com website over here and see that under the tools,

58
00:03:53,160 --> 00:03:54,720
the components of the tools.

59
00:03:54,930 --> 00:03:57,060
Uh, there is this tools over here.

60
00:03:57,060 --> 00:04:00,630
So tools are the utilities designed to be called by the model.

61
00:04:01,350 --> 00:04:04,500
Their inputs are designed to be generated by models.

62
00:04:04,500 --> 00:04:08,100
And their outputs are designed to be passed back to the models.

63
00:04:08,130 --> 00:04:12,150
And the toolkit is a collection of tools meant to be used together.

64
00:04:12,300 --> 00:04:18,120
And if you write your own tools, see how to you you would like to contribute to the integration.

65
00:04:18,180 --> 00:04:19,380

So when constructing an agent, you need to provide it with a list of tools that you can use beside

7
00:00:29,960 --> 00:00:33,110
the actual functions that is called.

8
00:00:33,140 --> 00:00:34,880
And the tool consists of several components.

9
00:00:34,880 --> 00:00:38,570
It says name, description, arg, schema, return direct.

10
00:00:38,840 --> 00:00:45,800
And they have got some details over here like how you can write the functions lang chain runners and

11
00:00:45,800 --> 00:00:47,810
subclasses from the base tools.

12
00:00:47,810 --> 00:00:51,860
So these are three different ways that you can actually create a tool.

13
00:00:51,860 --> 00:00:57,290
It can be either a functions like a normal Python function, or you can write a Java like a lang chain

14
00:00:57,290 --> 00:00:57,920
Runnables.

15
00:00:57,920 --> 00:01:02,130
Or you can also use a subclass of base Bayes tool.

16
00:01:02,130 --> 00:01:03,870
And then you can write from there.

17
00:01:03,870 --> 00:01:06,240
So all these things you can do it.

18
00:01:06,270 --> 00:01:08,520
And then you need to create a function.

19
00:01:08,520 --> 00:01:12,750
For instance, if you're just going to create a function all you have to do is just use a decorator

20
00:01:12,780 --> 00:01:17,220
add tool decorator for any of your Python code.

21
00:01:17,220 --> 00:01:20,010
And that becomes a tool already.

22
00:01:20,010 --> 00:01:20,400
Amazing.

23
00:01:20,400 --> 00:01:22,770
That's very easy and straightforward way of doing it.

24
00:01:22,800 --> 00:01:26,550
Or you can also create an async implementation of the tool over here.

25
00:01:26,790 --> 00:01:28,440
And these are the details which is available.

26
00:01:28,440 --> 00:01:34,050
And the one which I was talking about with the uh with the structured tool.

27
00:01:34,050 --> 00:01:35,070
So this is the structure tool.

28
00:01:35,070 --> 00:01:36,930
That is what we are going to be writing anyways.

29
00:01:36,960 --> 00:01:43,650
And they also have got this, uh, creating runnables like tools from Runnables where they have given

30
00:01:43,650 --> 00:01:51,780
like how you can create a runnables like chain as tool, you can make even a chain as a tool and then

31
00:01:51,780 --> 00:01:54,060
you can respond from there on as well.

32
00:01:54,060 --> 00:01:58,020
This is also something that you can do to create a tool.

33
00:01:58,020 --> 00:02:01,800
So you can make an entire runnables as a chain.

34
00:02:01,800 --> 00:02:04,780
And then you can use this subclass based tool.

35
00:02:04,780 --> 00:02:06,250
So you can use this subclass.

36
00:02:06,310 --> 00:02:12,310
You can see that the base tool has been passed in the class method, uh in a class constructor.

37
00:02:12,310 --> 00:02:15,970
And then you can uh you can create the tool from there.

38
00:02:16,000 --> 00:02:18,220
So these are three different ways that you can create a tool.

tool.

are two parameters.

00:03:22,430 --> 00:03:23,090

00:04:14,520 --> 00:04:15,240

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:05,090
In this lecture, we are going to see how we can make use of these tools that we have got, like custom

2
00:00:05,120 --> 00:00:11,480
tools as well as the one that we have downloaded from the community, like Wikipedia tool and use it

3
00:00:11,480 --> 00:00:13,700
with our large language model.

4
00:00:13,700 --> 00:00:16,040
So we are going to see how we can actually achieve that.

5
00:00:16,040 --> 00:00:24,230
And we are going to test if our large language model can actually grab the correct tool for that matter,

6
00:00:24,230 --> 00:00:31,130
because you see that now we have not only mapped Wikipedia, but we have also mapped or we have also

7
00:00:31,340 --> 00:00:33,110
built our own custom tools.

8
00:00:33,110 --> 00:00:40,400
We are going to bind those tools with our large language model, and we are going to see if that all

9
00:00:40,460 --> 00:00:47,660
works, and if the large language model can intelligently select that particular tool that we are supposed

10
00:00:47,660 --> 00:00:50,240
to use, like we are expecting it to use for that matter.

11
00:00:50,240 --> 00:00:52,490
So we will see how that can actually happen.

12
00:00:52,580 --> 00:00:59,600
Well, we have got this list of tools here already, and now we need to map these tool or bind these

13
00:00:59,600 --> 00:01:01,850
tools with our large language model.

14
00:01:01,880 --> 00:01:06,720
And again Lang long chain is so much sophisticated and that is the reason why long chain is so popular,

15
00:01:06,720 --> 00:01:14,910
because it now helps you provide the mechanism to bind your tools with the existing large language model.

16
00:01:14,910 --> 00:01:19,950
So for instance, this particular large language model that you can see over here is the chatbot llama

17
00:01:19,980 --> 00:01:21,570
that we have got over here.

00:01:21,720 --> 00:01:26,550
And I wanted to ask the question to this particular large language model.

00:01:27,120 --> 00:01:30,600
For instance, if I want to ask uh lm dot invoke.

00:01:30,720 --> 00:01:35,250
So response is equal to lm dot invoke.

00:04:12,440 --> 00:04:16,880

00:04:16,880 --> 00:04:23,750
