And because we have got all these messages, I'm sure our large language model can easily execute these

5
00:00:17,180 --> 00:00:20,690
details and get us the response back.

6
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:29,510
So for doing that, I'm just going to go over here, uh, the code and I'm going to say, uh, maybe

7
00:00:29,540 --> 00:00:38,300
final output is equal to uh LM with tools dot invoke.

8
00:00:38,570 --> 00:00:42,050
So I'm going to invoke it and I'm going to pass the entire message.

9
00:00:42,080 --> 00:00:45,170
See this message that I'm going to pass in.

10
00:00:45,260 --> 00:00:47,930
And now this is going to print.

11
00:00:48,050 --> 00:00:57,680
So if I now print the final output dot content over here, you should see that content coming up for

12
00:00:57,680 --> 00:00:57,890
me.

13
00:00:57,920 --> 00:01:03,440
So for that question that you have asked now the LM is going to process and it's going to say based

14
00:01:03,440 --> 00:01:08,610
on the information provided from Wikipedia, Donald Trump did win the 2024 presidential election.

15
00:01:08,640 --> 00:01:09,630
Look at that.

16
00:01:09,660 --> 00:01:18,660
Now, our LM has got the capability to get me the answer based on the tool that we have that we have

17
00:01:18,690 --> 00:01:19,260
bounded.

18
00:01:19,260 --> 00:01:26,220
So our local large language model, which did not even have access to the internet before, has got

19
00:01:26,220 --> 00:01:27,210
the internet access.

20
00:01:27,210 --> 00:01:32,070
And it is getting the details from the tools that it has got, the Wikipedia tools.

21
00:01:32,070 --> 00:01:33,270
And now look at that.

22
00:01:33,270 --> 00:01:36,360
It is getting the response, giving the response to me.

23
00:01:36,360 --> 00:01:45,180
And if I go to Lamb-smith this time, uh, over here, chat Olama, you will notice that it has selected

24
00:01:45,180 --> 00:01:51,780
the Wikipedia and it is giving me the response over here, which is amazing.

25
00:01:51,780 --> 00:01:55,530
So that is what is really happening over here.

26
00:01:55,950 --> 00:02:00,660
So it has called the Wikipedia and it has got me the answer.

27
00:02:00,660 --> 00:02:04,650
So Wikipedia tool is executing over here and in the chat.

28
00:02:04,650 --> 00:02:05,250
Olama.

29
00:02:05,670 --> 00:02:09,900
Um, it has really got that particular answer as this one.

30
00:02:09,900 --> 00:02:13,390
So this is the output that it has given to me over there.

31
00:02:13,660 --> 00:02:16,780
This is fabulous.

32
00:02:16,810 --> 00:02:27,430
And now, if I want to change the question a bit, uh, what is the sum of two and 22, which is 24?

33
00:02:27,430 --> 00:02:34,690
And if I try executing it, executing it, and if I try executing this particular code, the sum of

34
00:02:34,720 --> 00:02:36,580
two and 22 is 24.

35
00:02:36,610 --> 00:02:37,240
Look at that.

36
00:02:37,240 --> 00:02:38,380
So that is also working.

37
00:02:38,380 --> 00:02:43,180
So the custom tool is also being called and invoked and executed.

38
00:02:43,180 --> 00:02:46,540
And it is giving me the response correctly over here.

39
00:02:47,440 --> 00:02:54,130
And if I want to ask another question what is the double of two.

40
00:02:54,910 --> 00:02:56,710
Execute this code block.

41
00:02:56,740 --> 00:03:00,670
Execute this code block and execute this code block.

42
00:03:00,700 --> 00:03:02,590
The double of two is four.

43
00:03:02,590 --> 00:03:06,010
So it has really called the multiply numbers.

44
00:03:06,370 --> 00:03:10,270
And it has given me the answer as four.

45
00:03:11,080 --> 00:03:17,750
So hope you got the idea of how the large language model can invoke the tools that we have built, the

46
00:03:17,750 --> 00:03:23,690
custom tools, and also the community built tools without any hesitations with the code that we have

47
00:03:23,690 --> 00:03:24,530
written over here.

48
00:03:24,530 --> 00:03:34,760
So this is the power of you building a tools and also enabling your local large language model to have

49
00:03:34,760 --> 00:03:39,230
the support of running the tools or binding the tools along with it.

50
00:03:39,230 --> 00:03:45,170
And make sure while you use all these functionality, your large language model has got the tooling

51
00:03:45,170 --> 00:03:46,220
support in it.

52
00:03:46,340 --> 00:03:47,810
That's the end of the tools guys.

53
00:03:47,810 --> 00:03:49,040
Hopefully you got the idea.

54
00:03:49,040 --> 00:03:55,250
There are so many things still need to cover, and I hope you already have the whole wide range of idea

55
00:03:55,250 --> 00:03:57,230
of how you can do everything yourself.

56
00:03:57,230 --> 00:04:03,320
Just go ahead and create your own templates, kind of, uh, tools, uh, like the structure tools,

57
00:04:03,320 --> 00:04:09,140
and also try to even invoke the custom tools from the prompt template that you're going to be creating.

58
00:04:09,140 --> 00:04:11,810
And then you call these tools from within.

59
00:04:11,840 --> 00:04:14,630
You're going to be doing a fabulous job there, I'm sure.

60
00:04:14,630 --> 00:04:21,170
But this has already given you a wide range of idea of how you can make use of the tools within your

61
00:04:21,170 --> 00:04:22,850
large language model application.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━


==================================================================================

# Section 11: Building AI Agents with LangChain



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,020 --> 00:00:01,970
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,970 --> 00:00:04,970
And in this Section we are going to start building an agent.

3
00:00:05,000 --> 00:00:08,360
So what is this agent by themselves?

4
00:00:08,360 --> 00:00:13,310
The large language model can't take any actions, they just output the text.

5
00:00:13,340 --> 00:00:18,620
We already know that a big use case for Lang chain is creating an agents.

6
00:00:18,620 --> 00:00:25,340
So the agents are the system that uses the large language models as a reasoning engine to determine

7
00:00:25,340 --> 00:00:31,940
which action to take and output the inputs necessary to perform the action.

8
00:00:31,940 --> 00:00:37,430
So after executing the action, the results can be fed back to large language model to determine whether

9
00:00:37,430 --> 00:00:40,850
more actions are needed or whether it is okay to finish it off.

10
00:00:40,850 --> 00:00:47,090
This is often achieved via tool calling, and we already saw the tool callings as well in our last section

11
00:00:47,090 --> 00:00:47,900
of this course.

12
00:00:47,900 --> 00:00:53,330
That's the reason why I wanted to cover up this agents part right now, because now you have got all

13
00:00:53,330 --> 00:00:56,180
the nuts and bolts for building an agents.

14
00:00:56,180 --> 00:00:59,330
We have already discussed about LM, we have already talked about tool calling.

15
00:00:59,330 --> 00:01:03,380
The final piece of the puzzle is the agents that we are going to talk about.

16
00:01:03,380 --> 00:01:09,630
So agents, as you can see over here, is going to be helping us to perform some action based on the

17
00:01:09,630 --> 00:01:13,230
tools that we have got, and that is what we are going to be doing over here.

18
00:01:13,230 --> 00:01:19,350
I'm not going to talk about the already available agents, because if you have seen, uh, hugging face

19
00:01:19,350 --> 00:01:22,230
also has got an agent called as smiles agent.

20
00:01:22,230 --> 00:01:27,330
So if you just go and search for hugging face, uh, small, uh, small agents.

21
00:01:27,510 --> 00:01:28,560
Um, yeah.

22
00:01:28,590 --> 00:01:29,430
This one.

23
00:01:29,610 --> 00:01:35,160
Uh, so you see that this is an agent which is going to perform some action for you.

24
00:01:35,160 --> 00:01:40,020
So, uh, if you just go to Conceptual guide, what are the agents?

25
00:01:40,020 --> 00:01:45,300
So any efficient system using I will need to provide some kind of access to the real world.

26
00:01:45,300 --> 00:01:50,850
For instance, the possibility to call a search tool to get, uh, external information, like how we

27
00:01:50,850 --> 00:01:57,270
did in our last, uh, Section where we give where we gave the LM, uh, the external, uh, real world

28
00:01:57,270 --> 00:01:58,650
access with Wikipedia.

29
00:01:58,650 --> 00:02:01,110
That's exactly what they are talking about over here.

30
00:02:01,140 --> 00:02:07,260
Or an act on certain program in order to solve a task, which is what we did in our last section, where

31
00:02:07,260 --> 00:02:14,710
we gave the ability for the agent to, uh, to perform addition, multiplication and subtraction operation

32
00:02:14,710 --> 00:02:17,080
with our custom program that we built.

33
00:02:17,110 --> 00:02:25,210
In other words, heirloom should have a agency which is nothing but the tools that we have.

34
00:02:25,210 --> 00:02:30,880
And the genetic programs are the gateway to the outside world of the Elm, which is amazing.

35
00:02:30,880 --> 00:02:36,340
That's what we have already did most of the time, and they have got some details of how the agents

36
00:02:36,370 --> 00:02:40,570
needs to be one of the things that you need to avoid, and they have got a lot of discussions and details

37
00:02:40,570 --> 00:02:41,110
over here.

38
00:02:41,110 --> 00:02:46,780
But most important thing that you need to understand with this particular LM agents is that they are

39
00:02:46,780 --> 00:02:55,240
going to think and do the action and then perform other action, or maybe calling other tools to complete

40
00:02:55,270 --> 00:02:56,230
a certain task.

41
00:02:56,230 --> 00:03:05,260
So the agents are going to be relentlessly work for you to get the most optimal output from the question

42
00:03:05,260 --> 00:03:12,220
that you have asked via all the available tools and the custom tools, and from the reasoning engine,

43
00:03:12,220 --> 00:03:16,330
which is the large language model, and get you the response that you are looking for, that is the

44
00:03:16,330 --> 00:03:17,350
power of the agents.

45
00:03:17,380 --> 00:03:21,160
Whereas if you just ask a query to your large language model.

46
00:03:21,160 --> 00:03:22,780
It's just going to output it immediately.

47
00:03:22,780 --> 00:03:28,510
Whereas with this tool calls, it is going to keep on calling quite a lot of different tools before

48
00:03:28,510 --> 00:03:30,610
it answers the correct questions.

49
00:03:30,640 --> 00:03:33,160
So you see that this is what the diagram is really.

50
00:03:33,190 --> 00:03:35,170
It is going to keep updating its memory.

51
00:03:35,170 --> 00:03:39,520
And then it is going to again call the tool call to to get the right output.

52
00:03:39,520 --> 00:03:42,070
And then it is going to give the response to you back.

53
00:03:42,070 --> 00:03:44,170
So the final answer is going to come up over here.

54
00:03:44,170 --> 00:03:50,950
So it is going to do a lot of different operation before it gets you the final output, which is amazing.

55
00:03:50,950 --> 00:03:57,130
And that is what we are going to be doing in this particular Section of how we can make use of the agents

56
00:03:57,130 --> 00:04:00,850
in a long chain, but not for you to build the agents.

57
00:04:00,850 --> 00:04:06,130
We are going to start looking at the agents class, which is available in long chain, and we're going

58
00:04:06,160 --> 00:04:06,670
to do that.

59
00:04:06,670 --> 00:04:12,610
But the first use case that we are going to be doing in this particular Section is going to be how we

60
00:04:12,610 --> 00:04:21,550
can replace our existing tool calling and selection logics that we wrote to execute the the tools with

61
00:04:21,550 --> 00:04:23,440
the agents.

62
00:04:24,070 --> 00:04:26,050
We'll be talking about that in our next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,240 --> 00:00:05,360
All right, before we get into the further lectures of this particular section, we are going to talk

2
00:00:05,360 --> 00:00:10,600
about the long chain 1.0 features, which we are going to be talking about in this particular section.

3
00:00:10,920 --> 00:00:16,360
Well, we already discussed about some of the features that the long chain 1.0 has got like simplified

4
00:00:16,360 --> 00:00:22,040
and unified namespace creating agents with middleware and more focused on context engineering.

5
00:00:22,160 --> 00:00:26,560
This is one thing which I really wanted to talk, because we have already talked about the simplified

6
00:00:26,560 --> 00:00:32,520
and unified Namespacing, but we are actually going to start seeing these features in this particular

7
00:00:32,520 --> 00:00:32,880
section.

8
00:00:32,880 --> 00:00:36,440
We will see how we can able to achieve that something like this.

9
00:00:36,440 --> 00:00:41,760
And at the same time, we're also going to be talking about the features like middleware in this particular

10
00:00:41,760 --> 00:00:42,800
Section as well.

11
00:00:42,800 --> 00:00:49,360
So essentially you will notice that there is a new middleware introduced in this particular long chain

12
00:00:49,360 --> 00:00:57,880
version 1.0, where you can limit the total number of calls to your agents via the large language model,

13
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:01,960
the total number of thread which you need to limit for that particular tool.

14
00:01:01,960 --> 00:01:06,590
Call using this tool called limit middleware, as well as the run limit as well.

15
00:01:06,590 --> 00:01:12,510
So if your agent is going to call the tools multiple times and if it's going to overload your large

16
00:01:12,510 --> 00:01:18,790
language model, then this middleware is going to help you to a restriction on that particular call

17
00:01:18,870 --> 00:01:19,590
entirely.

18
00:01:19,790 --> 00:01:21,670
That's what this particular middleware is all about.

19
00:01:21,670 --> 00:01:27,390
But there are many in-built middleware also available in the long chain version 1.0, something like

20
00:01:27,390 --> 00:01:27,670
this.

21
00:01:27,670 --> 00:01:33,150
As you can see over here, the context editing middleware human in loop Middlewares lm tool selector

22
00:01:33,190 --> 00:01:38,150
Middlewares model call limit middleware that we just saw, as well as the model fallback Middlewares,

23
00:01:38,190 --> 00:01:41,470
as well as the tool call limit middleware and summarization middleware.

24
00:01:41,510 --> 00:01:42,070
Something like this.

25
00:01:42,070 --> 00:01:47,390
So these are the middlewares that you can actually use in order to make sure that your large language

26
00:01:47,390 --> 00:01:54,150
models can have the right amount of context and size that is required while you pass it to the large

27
00:01:54,150 --> 00:01:54,910
language model.

28
00:01:54,910 --> 00:01:57,470
And we are going to see some of the Middlewares as an action.

29
00:01:57,470 --> 00:01:59,110
And I will show you how this works.

30
00:01:59,110 --> 00:02:04,150
So these are the features that we have got which we are going to be discussing in this particular section.

31
00:02:04,350 --> 00:02:10,190
And then we're also be talking about some of the breaking change which has brought to the agent side.

32
00:02:10,710 --> 00:02:15,950
This Section is all about agents and we are going to see some of the agents breaking changes.

33
00:02:15,950 --> 00:02:20,870
So I'm going to still keep the older way of working with the agents in version 0.3.

34
00:02:20,910 --> 00:02:26,150
But we're also going to be talking about the new changes that is brought into the agent side.

35
00:02:26,150 --> 00:02:31,150
It's not really like a lot of change, just a package name change and a bit of a code change.

36
00:02:31,150 --> 00:02:34,590
But the remaining code still works as expected.

37
00:02:34,590 --> 00:02:37,190
So it's not a lot of change as well.

38
00:02:37,230 --> 00:02:38,870
But yes, there is a breaking change.

39
00:02:38,870 --> 00:02:42,830
If you try to run the code, it is not going to work in this particular section.

40
00:02:42,830 --> 00:02:47,710
And just to show you in a high level, because we have not even discussed about the agent yet in this

41
00:02:47,710 --> 00:02:48,630
particular section.

42
00:02:48,630 --> 00:02:49,630
But guess what?

43
00:02:49,670 --> 00:02:52,630
This is the change that you may be seeing immediately.

44
00:02:52,670 --> 00:02:57,910
Once you are going to run the older version of code that you are going to be using if you are following

45
00:02:57,910 --> 00:02:59,350
along that particular lecture.

46
00:02:59,350 --> 00:03:03,390
But you know what the code base is actually going to be of version 1.0.

47
00:03:03,430 --> 00:03:08,390
So you may not see this particular error, but the way that I'm teaching in the lecture versus the code

48
00:03:08,390 --> 00:03:13,860
that you will have in the source code Section will be entirely different because I have already upgraded

49
00:03:13,860 --> 00:03:19,940
to the latest version of Lang Chain, and you will notice that the Lang chain dot agents, dot initialize

50
00:03:19,940 --> 00:03:22,980
agent as well as the agent type are no more there.

51
00:03:23,020 --> 00:03:26,180
In the new version of the Lang chain version 1.0.

52
00:03:26,580 --> 00:03:32,020
C initialize agent has been moved to create agent, and there is nothing called as agent type because

53
00:03:32,020 --> 00:03:33,300
it is not required anymore.

54
00:03:33,420 --> 00:03:39,300
And also we used to pass the prompt like a query earlier, and now that's going to be passed as a human

55
00:03:39,300 --> 00:03:41,700
message or system message, something like that.

56
00:03:41,740 --> 00:03:46,500
I know we have not discussed yet any of these so far in this particular section, but the moment you

57
00:03:46,500 --> 00:03:52,340
get there, I will be talking about these, uh, in one of the lecture, and then I'll be talking about

58
00:03:52,340 --> 00:03:54,060
the upgraded version as well.

59
00:03:54,060 --> 00:03:57,220
So you may see there is going to be a difference between both of them.

60
00:03:57,460 --> 00:04:02,220
I'm just giving you a heads up, because this entire course, as I told you, is recorded with version

61
00:04:02,260 --> 00:04:03,060
0.3.

62
00:04:03,220 --> 00:04:08,180
So because of this new breaking change, I'm also going to be covering with version 1.0 as well.

63
00:04:08,220 --> 00:04:09,340
So that is one change.

64
00:04:09,380 --> 00:04:11,820
And I'll also be seeing a few more code changes as well.

65
00:04:11,820 --> 00:04:14,540
And we will talk about them once we get there.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:00,380
All right.

2
00:00:00,410 --> 00:00:06,680
In our last section, we discussed how we can give the ability for the large language model with the

3
00:00:06,680 --> 00:00:14,030
tool bindings to have an external tools like Wikipedia or internal custom methods like addition, subtraction,

4
00:00:14,030 --> 00:00:14,930
multiplication.

5
00:00:14,930 --> 00:00:17,300
And it gave us the answer that we're looking for.

6
00:00:17,330 --> 00:00:18,110
Amazing.

7
00:00:18,380 --> 00:00:27,320
Uh, but we actually did a tool execution logic within our own custom code, because the LLM can anyways

8
00:00:27,320 --> 00:00:33,560
choose the correct tools and it selects that, but it doesn't have the ability to execute it unless

9
00:00:33,560 --> 00:00:35,870
until we create like a prompt.

10
00:00:35,900 --> 00:00:39,740
Remember that's what was really happening in our last section.

11
00:00:39,740 --> 00:00:42,590
So we ended up writing a tool execution logic.

12
00:00:42,620 --> 00:00:47,930
I mean, we created a prompt which has got the tool to be executed and the response from the tool invoke

13
00:00:47,930 --> 00:00:48,380
method.

14
00:00:48,380 --> 00:00:54,410
And then we bounded that in the message, and then we pass it as a prompt to the large language model,

15
00:00:54,800 --> 00:00:57,920
which can then give us the output which is looking for amazing.

16
00:00:57,920 --> 00:01:04,670
But now we're going to remove that logic from here, and we're going to bring that our, uh, agents

17
00:01:04,670 --> 00:01:12,140
to do that operation for us, because I agents has the ability to do the selection of the tools based

18
00:01:12,140 --> 00:01:18,350
on the tool binding and route the query to the right tool, which is amazing.

19
00:01:18,350 --> 00:01:21,500
It's not going to route, but it's also going to execute that for us.

20
00:01:21,500 --> 00:01:26,690
So the the number of lines of code that you're going to be writing over here is going to be tremendously

21
00:01:26,690 --> 00:01:29,480
less compared to what that we have wrote before.

22
00:01:29,480 --> 00:01:34,280
So you'll notice that now if I ask what is the sum of two and four and who is the current president

23
00:01:34,280 --> 00:01:40,700
of USA in 2025, then I'm asking two questions over here, and you will notice that the large language

24
00:01:40,700 --> 00:01:44,180
model is going to ask that to the agent.

25
00:01:44,180 --> 00:01:50,330
And the agent is going to take care of every single operation that you have, and it is going to call

26
00:01:50,330 --> 00:01:56,270
the the available tools that is been bound with this particular large language model.

27
00:01:56,270 --> 00:02:01,400
And it's going to execute them one by one, and it is going to create that response for you, and it

28
00:02:01,400 --> 00:02:04,070
is going to give you both the responses is like this.

29
00:02:04,070 --> 00:02:05,720
This is fabulous, right?

30
00:02:05,720 --> 00:02:10,790
Because you don't even have to write the logics like how you wrote before, and you actually are going

31

00:00:55,040 --> 00:00:59,520
So the way you can actually work out with the new version of Lang Chain is you need to use this method

18
00:00:59,520 --> 00:01:01,040
called as create Agent.

19
00:01:01,400 --> 00:01:01,960
That's it.

20
00:01:02,400 --> 00:01:06,080
And instead of initialize agent you just use create agent over here.

21
00:01:06,450 --> 00:01:09,330
And the tools remains the same.

22
00:01:09,330 --> 00:01:11,490
So that's not really a problem here.

23
00:01:11,890 --> 00:01:15,490
And the LM, uh, is not going to be there.

24
00:01:15,490 --> 00:01:17,330
So there is no property called as LM.

25
00:01:17,570 --> 00:01:20,850
Rather you have got a property called as model.

26
00:01:20,970 --> 00:01:26,130
And here you need to pass your large language model, which is something that we created earlier in

27
00:01:26,130 --> 00:01:26,890
our lecture.

28
00:01:26,890 --> 00:01:28,930
So I'm going to just pass that over here.

29
00:01:29,250 --> 00:01:33,290
And we don't really have any more agent type as well as the verbose.

30
00:01:33,290 --> 00:01:35,370
So these two doesn't exist as well.

31
00:01:35,410 --> 00:01:39,130
I know it's a lot of breaking change this time with this version, but guess what?

32
00:01:39,130 --> 00:01:40,130
They have streamlined.

33
00:01:40,170 --> 00:01:46,450
I mean, the the Lang Chain team has streamlined the way you can create the agents, and it's more simpler

34
00:01:46,450 --> 00:01:47,050
as well.

35
00:01:47,890 --> 00:01:53,770
And the other good thing is you can also pass the system prompt this time where you can say that, uh,

36
00:01:53,810 --> 00:02:03,130
you are a helpful as an assistant, uh, that can provide the answer later to the calculations and always

37
00:02:03,170 --> 00:02:06,810
do a structured final output response in JSON, whatever.

38
00:02:06,850 --> 00:02:08,130
I like this particular system prompt.

39
00:02:08,130 --> 00:02:10,490
I'm just going to keep that guy as it is over here.

40
00:02:10,810 --> 00:02:15,900
And instead of the query that we were passing earlier to ask, what is the sum of two and four?

41
00:02:15,940 --> 00:02:19,940
Did the Donald Trump won 2004 24 presidential election?

42
00:02:20,420 --> 00:02:21,300
Something like this?

43
00:02:21,620 --> 00:02:28,020
So that question, I can actually ask it from the human prompt because this is basically a human prompt

44
00:02:28,020 --> 00:02:28,820
here, right?

45
00:02:29,100 --> 00:02:34,460
So in order to do that, I'm just going to say, um, maybe you know what?

46
00:02:34,500 --> 00:02:38,500
Even better, I can use this particular code.

47
00:02:38,500 --> 00:02:45,140
So instead of the agent dot run over here, I'm just going to say result is equal to agent Dot invoke.

48
00:02:45,140 --> 00:02:48,740
So there is this invoke method which you can do obviously even before.

49
00:02:49,340 --> 00:02:55,780
And now over here, uh, I'm going to say uh, double quotes of message, something like this.

50
00:02:56,220 --> 00:03:01,460
And over here for the query I'm going to pass the human message.

51
00:03:01,460 --> 00:03:07,940
So because I'm going to be passing the human message over here, I also need to import the uh, lang

52
00:03:07,980 --> 00:03:20,150
chain dot sorry long chain underscore core uh, and messages import a human messages, something like

53
00:03:20,150 --> 00:03:20,590
that.

54
00:03:20,590 --> 00:03:23,470
So this is something I really need over here.

55
00:03:23,870 --> 00:03:27,070
And then I'm going to pass this particular human message for the query.

56
00:03:27,110 --> 00:03:29,030
I can just pass the query over here.

57
00:03:29,030 --> 00:03:30,590
Something like this doesn't matter.

58
00:03:30,590 --> 00:03:32,070
So you can pass it something like this.

59
00:03:32,070 --> 00:03:33,110
And it just works.

60
00:03:33,230 --> 00:03:34,550
And this code is obsolete.

61
00:03:34,550 --> 00:03:35,790
So you can get rid of that.

62
00:03:36,070 --> 00:03:36,630
Right.

63
00:03:36,630 --> 00:03:38,590
So this is how you do it.

64
00:03:38,830 --> 00:03:40,030
And this is messages.

65
00:03:40,030 --> 00:03:40,350
Sorry.

66
00:03:40,350 --> 00:03:41,430
It's not message.

67
00:03:41,590 --> 00:03:42,670
Uh all right.

68
00:03:42,870 --> 00:03:45,350
And now let me try to run it over here.

69
00:03:45,350 --> 00:03:48,110
Let's see what's really going to happen this time.

70
00:03:48,110 --> 00:03:52,350
So it's going to take some time over here to get the response.

71
00:03:52,870 --> 00:03:57,510
You can see that now it's getting me the response over here saying uh, the content.

72
00:03:57,550 --> 00:04:00,630
What is the, uh, sum of two and four?

73
00:04:00,790 --> 00:04:03,430
Uh, did Donald Trump won the presidential election?

74
00:04:03,430 --> 00:04:05,470
So this is the human message which is being sent.

75
00:04:05,830 --> 00:04:12,350
And you're going to get a response from the, uh, from the agents over here as well.

76
00:04:12,350 --> 00:04:13,150
So guess what?

77
00:04:13,310 --> 00:04:19,430
Uh, instead of really getting the, uh, the result over here that we used to get all the time, uh,

78
00:04:19,430 --> 00:04:24,950
I'm going to use the content property, which is going to get me the content, but because this is going

79
00:04:24,950 --> 00:04:30,910
to be in version 1.0, you need to pass the message over here.

80
00:04:31,110 --> 00:04:32,310
Uh, something like this.

81
00:04:32,550 --> 00:04:36,950
Uh, and you can pass zero of content, uh, to get the content information.

82
00:04:36,950 --> 00:04:40,830
So this is like the array that you are going to invoke to get the messages alone.

83
00:04:40,830 --> 00:04:46,750
So basically you're storing the human message, uh, into the messages, and then you're going to get

84
00:04:46,750 --> 00:04:49,190
just the content alone from here.

85
00:04:49,190 --> 00:04:53,350
So this is what really, uh, this particular piece of code is doing.

86
00:04:57,110 --> 00:04:59,950
And you can see that, uh, what is the sum of two and four for Donald Trump?

87
00:04:59,950 --> 00:05:03,910
So this is going to be the actual question that you're asking, which is zero.

88
00:05:04,070 --> 00:05:12,230
And the moment you put a minus one and then the content, you are going to get the response, uh, which

89
00:05:12,230 --> 00:05:14,350
is the result of your question.

90
00:05:16,430 --> 00:05:17,630
Which is something like this.

91
00:05:17,630 --> 00:05:21,190
As you can see over here, the sum of two and four is six.

And Trump election was false.

Explanation as the latest uh, available information and substitute for the 94 election has not yet

95

96
00:05:38,560 --> 00:05:44,720
That's the reason why it is still showing you that the, uh, the election has not taken place yet.

97
00:05:44,720 --> 00:05:45,480
That's fine.

98
00:05:45,480 --> 00:05:48,960
And you see that we are getting a response in the JSON format over here.

99
00:05:48,960 --> 00:05:56,920
The reason why this is happening is because you are actually, uh, sending a, uh, prompt saying always

100
00:05:56,920 --> 00:05:59,360
structure your final response in JSON format.

101
00:05:59,360 --> 00:06:02,360
That's the reason why I'm getting things in JSON over here.

102
00:06:02,360 --> 00:06:07,240
So that's the only difference between the create initialize agent versus the create agent method that

103
00:06:07,240 --> 00:06:08,000
we have got.

104
00:06:08,240 --> 00:06:12,480
And the way that we are consuming the method is also a bit changed this time.

105
00:06:12,480 --> 00:06:17,480
So instead of just the result that we used to get because we were using agent Dot run before, and because

106
00:06:17,480 --> 00:06:22,000
this is the agent dot invoke and we are passing everything to a messages.

107
00:06:22,000 --> 00:06:27,920
Now I'm using the messages array, um, the key to pass and then get the value out.

108
00:06:27,920 --> 00:06:29,920
So that is what is really happening over here.

109
00:06:29,920 --> 00:06:34,320
So this is how you can get the response from an agent.

110
00:06:34,320 --> 00:06:34,960
Something like this.

111
00:06:34,960 --> 00:06:36,760
This is the only change that you have got.

1
00:00:00,050 --> 00:00:00,560
All right.

So now we saw how in our last lecture, there was some craziness going on with the, uh, with the question

of the Donald Trump won the 2024 presidential election, it says Kamala Harris has won it, which is

Like this is exactly the same kind of prompt template, this chat prompt template I'm passing in here.

But this time I'm going to use prompt template instead of the query that I was passing in before, and

now I'm going to execute this particular code.

So we'll see what can happen.

that.

And then it is going to go with the add number function.

30
00:01:42,160 --> 00:01:46,000
And then it is going to give me the response as seven which is two plus five is seven.

00:01:46,000 --> 00:01:50,560
And then it is going to go with the uh, with the Donald Trump question.

And again, unfortunately I don't know why is it always saying it's.

No, because because President Donald Trump is the president of 2025 and he won the election in 2024.

Uh, for some reason, it is not giving me.

00:02:01,750 --> 00:02:02,410
That's what I was saying.

Let's ask about what is the, uh, latest movie, uh, of Tom cruise, uh, hitting, uh, theater in

The math problem just amazing.

00:02:48,910 --> 00:02:49,540

This is going to the agent executor.

00:03:00,610 --> 00:03:01,270
as well.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,040 --> 00:00:00,520
All right.

2
00:00:00,520 --> 00:00:08,360
So now that you have seen how the chat prompt template is written in the version, uh, three, uh,

3
00:00:08,360 --> 00:00:14,560
in your last lecture, now we're going to discuss about how we can write the same exact code with version

4
00:00:14,680 --> 00:00:15,920
1.0 as well.

5
00:00:16,160 --> 00:00:18,840
As I told you in version 1.0, things have changed.

6
00:00:18,840 --> 00:00:20,720
So you cannot just use the run method.

7
00:00:20,720 --> 00:00:22,640
You need to use the invoke method.

8
00:00:22,640 --> 00:00:25,480
So I'm going to call the invoke method here.

9
00:00:25,720 --> 00:00:27,600
That's the change with 1.0.

10
00:00:27,600 --> 00:00:31,080
And now if I try to run this code it is still going to fail.

11
00:00:31,080 --> 00:00:36,600
And it's going to tell me that the expected mapping type as input to chat prompt template.

12
00:00:36,920 --> 00:00:39,520
Uh received this particular error over here.

13
00:00:39,520 --> 00:00:45,040
So this is really not going to work for us if we are going to be doing something like this.

14
00:00:45,040 --> 00:00:46,480
As you can see over here.

15
00:00:46,720 --> 00:00:49,680
So how do we actually fix this particular problem.

16
00:00:49,680 --> 00:00:53,320
Well, it's not really a lot of change that we have to do.

17
00:00:53,400 --> 00:00:57,960
The only thing that we have to do it is just use the dot format messages.

18
00:00:57,960 --> 00:01:03,680
So this is one thing which they have just recently introduced in the new version of the Lang chain over

19
00:01:03,680 --> 00:01:05,600
here, uh, something like this.

20
00:01:05,800 --> 00:01:10,170
Uh, I think this has changed automatically by this particular AI that we have got.

21
00:01:10,210 --> 00:01:10,490
Yeah.

22
00:01:10,530 --> 00:01:10,850
Cool.

23
00:01:10,850 --> 00:01:12,570
So that is the only change that we have to do.

24
00:01:12,730 --> 00:01:16,450
And now if you try to execute this, this code is still going to fail.

25
00:01:16,490 --> 00:01:21,890
Because while you try to formulate the message like using the format message over here using the agent

26
00:01:21,890 --> 00:01:28,810
dot invoke, you remember in our last lecture, we talked about how you can do the, uh, agent invoke

27
00:01:28,810 --> 00:01:33,290
with the messages something like this with a human message, uh, like this.

28
00:01:33,290 --> 00:01:35,970
So this is how we should be doing here as well.

29
00:01:36,170 --> 00:01:44,770
So essentially we just have to say messages something like this, uh, over here, uh, and you also

30
00:01:44,770 --> 00:01:50,410
need to use this in a block, uh, something like this, like an array block, so that it can get you

31
00:01:50,410 --> 00:01:52,010
the messages that you are looking for.

32
00:01:52,010 --> 00:01:54,930
So messages will have the data that you're looking for.

33
00:01:55,090 --> 00:02:00,490
And once you have the messages, then we just need to use this guy, the print result of the messages

34
00:02:00,490 --> 00:02:04,850
with minus one of the content, uh, to get the content that you're looking for.

35
00:02:04,850 --> 00:02:09,410
So this is the change that we have to do with version 1.0.

36
00:02:09,450 --> 00:02:14,750
Unfortunately, uh, and as I told you before, the moment you start seeing the pattern of changes,

37
00:02:14,750 --> 00:02:20,310
then you will realize that the things are pretty much exactly the same way, like how they have changed

38
00:02:20,310 --> 00:02:22,350
overall in the application.

39
00:02:22,630 --> 00:02:27,630
So now I'm going to run this particular code one more time to see what is really going to happen.

40
00:02:28,110 --> 00:02:32,350
And you will see that there is no error, at least this time because it looks like the chat prompt template

41
00:02:32,350 --> 00:02:32,910
is working.

42
00:02:32,910 --> 00:02:36,670
So it is going to give you the result for the questions that you have asked.

43
00:02:36,670 --> 00:02:40,190
So you see that the sum of two and five is seven.

44
00:02:40,190 --> 00:02:45,470
And the Tom cruise movie in 2025 is null because it doesn't know what exactly is the new movie.

45
00:02:45,670 --> 00:02:49,750
Uh, but however, there isn't any information of Tom cruise hitting the theater in 2020.

46
00:02:49,750 --> 00:02:54,070
That's what this particular model is going to tell you anyways, because it doesn't have any information

47
00:02:54,070 --> 00:02:54,830
for you.

48
00:02:54,830 --> 00:02:56,310
But yeah, that's the chat prompt.

49
00:02:56,310 --> 00:03:01,710
Template changes for you, so you can't just pass the prompt template directly using the agent dot run

50
00:03:01,710 --> 00:03:07,470
method, you just have to use the agent dot invoke method to pass the prompt there, because you don't

51
00:03:07,470 --> 00:03:12,990
even have a run method, as you can see over here anymore in the agents, that's the only change.

52
00:03:12,990 --> 00:03:14,910
That is the major change which has happened.

53
00:03:14,910 --> 00:03:18,390
So that code that we wrote in our earlier lecture is obsolete.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:06,800
In this lecture, we are going to talk about how we can make use of our agent to perform other operation,

2
00:00:06,800 --> 00:00:11,150
not just about like the current information or anything like that, but we are going to see how we can

3
00:00:11,150 --> 00:00:20,420
build an agent which can do real time data extraction or web page data extraction using a playwright

4
00:00:20,450 --> 00:00:20,930
tool.

5
00:00:20,930 --> 00:00:24,830
So that is what we are going to be discussing in this particular lecture.

6
00:00:24,830 --> 00:00:31,010
So in order to do that I'm going to use a tool which is already built by the community, which is the

7
00:00:31,670 --> 00:00:33,890
uh, let's say lang chain.

8
00:00:36,830 --> 00:00:37,460
R tool.

9
00:00:37,460 --> 00:00:40,280
So if I just go navigate there, this is the tools.

10
00:00:40,580 --> 00:00:46,490
Uh, and if you just go all the way down, you can see that it's a web browsing tool, which is, uh,

11
00:00:46,490 --> 00:00:50,750
there are three tools available and one tool which I really like is the playwright browser tool.

12
00:00:50,750 --> 00:00:57,770
So if I just go choose this tool, you can see that this tool is quite interesting because it has got,

13
00:00:57,860 --> 00:01:00,920
uh, a bundle of toolkits included.

14
00:01:00,920 --> 00:01:06,810
One is the navigate tool, which is going to navigate the browser to any specified URL that you have

00:08:03,990 --> 00:08:08,460
which is very bad because you see that there are some 200 salary as well.

125
00:08:08,490 --> 00:08:11,790
That's the reason why the average has reduced tremendously.

126
00:08:11,790 --> 00:08:17,010
But yeah, now we could able to get this information from the agent over here.

127
00:08:17,070 --> 00:08:23,220
And this is all happening because of the the access of the external browser capability that we have

128
00:08:23,250 --> 00:08:23,460
got.

129
00:08:23,490 --> 00:08:25,650
And we get these information out from here.

130
00:08:25,650 --> 00:08:31,110
Everything is happening within our local large language model where it does the execution.

131

135

136

model.

11

Rather there is something called as model.

We don't even have an agent as such.

And we just need to write a system prompt here just to give a more formulated, uh, message for this

particular guy so that it gets you the response that you are looking for.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,120 --> 00:00:05,000
In this lecture, I'm going to talk about the content block feature of Lang 1.0.

2
00:00:05,320 --> 00:00:11,400
Well, the content block is a new property added recently in this version where you can get the details

3
00:00:11,400 --> 00:00:16,920
of a specific content, like how the large language model is thinking, whether the response has got

4
00:00:16,920 --> 00:00:22,480
any tool calls, whether the response has got any text information, or whether the response has got

5
00:00:22,480 --> 00:00:26,720
any other information which has been returned by the large language model.

6
00:00:27,080 --> 00:00:32,680
Because these days the large language models are multi models and their response will have many different

7
00:00:32,680 --> 00:00:33,160
content.

8
00:00:33,160 --> 00:00:38,600
It can be an image, it can be a video or audio or even text, JSON, whatever it is.

9
00:00:38,680 --> 00:00:44,760
So in order to understand all of these, we need to use the model which has got the reasoning model.

10
00:00:44,760 --> 00:00:50,040
And I can show you how the reasoning model can get you the responses in reasoning as well, like while

11
00:00:50,040 --> 00:00:51,960
it is thinking behind the scene.

12
00:00:52,200 --> 00:00:57,400
So in order to do that, just go up all the way over here in the code and you see that currently the

13
00:00:57,400 --> 00:01:01,450
model that we are using is the GPT OS 20 billion parameter as it just changed.

14
00:01:01,450 --> 00:01:07,250
In the last lecture of this course, you can please use the one model which which can run within your

15
00:01:07,250 --> 00:01:09,970
machine, which is the GPT OS 20 billion parameter.

16
00:01:09,970 --> 00:01:12,370
It is basically a reasoning model.

17
00:01:12,370 --> 00:01:19,570
So if it is a reasoning model, you can just say the model to have a reasoning parameter like reasoning

18
00:01:19,850 --> 00:01:25,810
as true, which means this, uh, large language model can start to reason as well.

19
00:01:25,810 --> 00:01:31,810
So if I'm going to just run this entire code over here and let me try to reset this because I'm just

20
00:01:31,810 --> 00:01:34,610
changing it, I just wanted to make sure that this thing works.

21
00:01:34,610 --> 00:01:36,930
So I'm going to run this particular code over here.

22
00:01:37,450 --> 00:01:43,410
Uh, and I'm going to run this particular code that is just needed, which is the toolings that we need,

23
00:01:43,690 --> 00:01:50,170
uh, and also the playwrights, uh, thing that we need for our code to be executed.

24
00:01:51,130 --> 00:01:55,250
And with this particular code over here, I'm going to add the reasoning part.

25
00:01:55,250 --> 00:01:57,950
So I'm going to add a markdown over here.

26
00:01:58,350 --> 00:02:02,710
And I'm going to say understanding content block.

27
00:02:07,550 --> 00:02:08,550
Something like this.

28
00:02:08,550 --> 00:02:12,310
And now I'm going to add the code for you over here.

29
00:02:12,310 --> 00:02:16,710
So in order to do that I'm going to copy the same exact code that we were doing before something like

30
00:02:16,710 --> 00:02:17,150
this.

31
00:02:17,550 --> 00:02:25,270
And now with this particular content over here, we can also verify whether it has got any reasoning,

32
00:02:25,390 --> 00:02:27,030
uh, element in it.

33
00:02:27,470 --> 00:02:32,190
And the way I can do it is I can just write a for loop over here.

34
00:02:32,470 --> 00:02:35,310
And I'm going to say for, uh, message.

35
00:02:40,230 --> 00:02:45,190
In the result of the messages that we have got over here.

36
00:02:45,390 --> 00:02:50,990
But, uh, even before that, I'm just going to, uh, get the whole thing, something like this.

37
00:02:50,990 --> 00:02:55,480
And instead of the content property, which I'm going to be working all these days.

38
00:02:55,480 --> 00:03:00,440
We are going to now use what is called as the content block property.

39
00:03:01,280 --> 00:03:02,400
Content block.

40
00:03:02,920 --> 00:03:03,640
Something like this.

41
00:03:03,640 --> 00:03:07,200
So this is the property which we are going to be using this time.

42
00:03:07,480 --> 00:03:19,280
And over here, uh, I'm going to say if the uh, the message of the type is equal to oops, not the

43
00:03:19,280 --> 00:03:23,160
tool response, it is going to be tool call.

44
00:03:23,320 --> 00:03:28,160
So if it is a tool call then get me the tool name that you're using.

45
00:03:28,160 --> 00:03:32,960
And also the tool input that you are using something like that.

46
00:03:33,000 --> 00:03:35,680
You can also get the arguments if you wanted to.

47
00:03:35,720 --> 00:03:38,880
So these are the responses used to come from the large language model.

48
00:03:39,240 --> 00:03:42,640
And similarly I'm going to put one more elsif over here.

49
00:03:42,680 --> 00:03:51,600
And I'm going to say if the message of the type, uh is equal to, let's say, reasoning uh, that we

50
00:03:51,600 --> 00:03:52,560
are going to get.

51
00:03:52,560 --> 00:03:52,690
It.

52
00:03:57,130 --> 00:03:58,090
Something like this.

53
00:03:58,450 --> 00:04:03,370
Then print me the reasoning operation, which is going to be reasoning.

54
00:04:03,370 --> 00:04:06,530
So you will have a message with reasoning in it.

55
00:04:07,010 --> 00:04:14,730
And similarly if I'm going to say else if the message, uh, is of a type of text, for example, that

56
00:04:14,730 --> 00:04:16,730
is the response that we are getting all these days.

57
00:04:17,050 --> 00:04:22,090
So if it is going to be a text, then try to print that particular value.

58
00:04:22,130 --> 00:04:27,610
So I'm going to say is equal to text something like this.

59
00:04:28,570 --> 00:04:32,170
Uh, then print me the message of text over here.

60
00:04:32,170 --> 00:04:37,170
So this is the thing that I wanted to pass in, uh, to see how things can be generated.

61
00:04:37,170 --> 00:04:42,890
So this is pretty cool to see how you can also get the output of what the large language model is doing.

62
00:04:42,930 --> 00:04:47,410
What is the reasoning, uh, going behind and what is the response that is going to be coming up?

63
00:04:47,410 --> 00:04:49,730
That's the power of the content block for you.

64
00:04:49,930 --> 00:04:55,780
And now I'm going to run this particular code to see how the reasoning is going to work.

65
00:04:56,020 --> 00:05:02,460
So just let's wait for the execution to happen, and you will notice that we're going to get a JSON

66
00:05:02,460 --> 00:05:06,260
response of the the details that we were looking for.

67
00:05:06,260 --> 00:05:10,380
So the average salary is 4250 which is correct.

68
00:05:10,380 --> 00:05:14,020
And let's go to the text editor here to see the entire response.

69
00:05:14,060 --> 00:05:15,020
Oh that's cool.

70
00:05:15,300 --> 00:05:23,820
Uh, and I'm not seeing this particular part, which is oops, looks like uh, there is an error here.

71

72

Uh, my spelling mistakes.

73
00:05:33,380 --> 00:05:37,540
I think it's because the AI is trying to write things for me, and I'm just losing that.

74
00:05:38,020 --> 00:05:39,500
Let me try to run it again.

75
00:05:52,440 --> 00:05:52,960
There we go.

76
00:05:52,960 --> 00:05:57,880
Now there is no error as such, so hopefully we get the response.

77
00:05:57,880 --> 00:05:58,480
Oh, there we go.

78
00:05:58,520 --> 00:05:58,880
See that.

79
00:05:58,880 --> 00:06:04,240
We have got a reasoning here which says that we have table rows need salary average extract the salary

80
00:06:04,240 --> 00:06:05,560
value of these.

81
00:06:05,600 --> 00:06:06,360
Sum it up.

82
00:06:06,560 --> 00:06:08,120
Uh something like this.

83
00:06:08,120 --> 00:06:10,120
And you get this particular sum.

84
00:06:10,120 --> 00:06:15,320
And then do an average and provide, uh, the JSON with the table data and average.

85
00:06:15,320 --> 00:06:17,160
That's what is really happening over here.

86
00:06:17,200 --> 00:06:17,880
Look at that.

87
00:06:18,200 --> 00:06:19,120
It's pretty cool.

88
00:06:19,120 --> 00:06:26,240
So you can also get a response, uh, in uh over here, along with the reasoning which is going behind

89
00:06:26,280 --> 00:06:29,840
to get this particular output for you, which is amazing.

90
00:06:29,840 --> 00:06:34,440
So that is how you can use the content blocks to understand what's really going on behind the scene

91
00:06:34,800 --> 00:06:35,360
as well.

92
00:06:35,360 --> 00:06:39,560
And these content blocks are very helpful if you're going to be verifying whether the correct tools

93
00:06:39,600 --> 00:06:44,160
been invoked, whether the correct reasoning is going behind the scene, and what is the output which

94
00:06:44,160 --> 00:06:45,560
is going to be coming out as well.

95
00:06:46,240 --> 00:06:48,120
This is the content block for you.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,160 --> 00:00:04,960
Next up we're going to talk about the feature called as middleware, which is recently added in the

2
00:00:04,960 --> 00:00:06,960
version 1.0 of Lang Chain.

3
00:00:06,960 --> 00:00:12,160
So I'm going to talk about the agents middleware, something like this.

4
00:00:12,640 --> 00:00:15,120
Uh, and I'm going to hit okay.

5
00:00:15,360 --> 00:00:17,480
And let's try to add the code.

6
00:00:17,480 --> 00:00:22,040
So in order to work with middleware I'm just going to go copy the code that we have already worked here.

7
00:00:22,040 --> 00:00:27,840
So I'm just going to say Ctrl a Ctrl C and paste it over here.

8
00:00:28,240 --> 00:00:31,720
Um and I'm going to use the middleware this time.

9
00:00:31,720 --> 00:00:37,960
So in order to work with the middleware all you have to do it is just use this, uh, lang chain, uh,

10
00:00:39,920 --> 00:00:41,600
dot agents.

11
00:00:41,880 --> 00:00:44,880
And there is something called as middleware, as you can see over here.

12
00:00:45,040 --> 00:00:52,000
And you can see that when I do an import and control space, um, you will get, uh, quite a lot of

13
00:00:52,000 --> 00:00:55,600
different, uh, agents, for example, the tool called limit agents.

14
00:00:55,600 --> 00:01:00,640
And there is also called as the uh, summarization middleware, something like that.

15
00:01:00,640 --> 00:01:05,240
The one that we are going to be using is the tool called Limit Middleware over here.

16
00:01:05,480 --> 00:01:08,540
And we'll see how we can start using this particular middleware.

17
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:10,700
So in order to use that, it's super easy.

18
00:01:10,980 --> 00:01:16,420
All you have to do is just like give a name for this particular middleware and just define the tool

19
00:01:16,420 --> 00:01:17,940
called middleware over here.

20
00:01:18,380 --> 00:01:20,420
And now I'm going to give some details.

21
00:01:20,420 --> 00:01:23,620
So I'm going to first of all give the name of this particular tool.

22
00:01:23,620 --> 00:01:25,420
So I'm going to say tool name.

# Section 12: Building AI Agent with RAG and Tooling support (Project)


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:01,940
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,940 --> 00:00:08,720
And in this Section we are going to talk about how we can build agents with rag and tool support with

3
00:00:08,720 --> 00:00:11,600
our local large language model.

4
00:00:11,810 --> 00:00:19,700
If you remember, until our last section, we were trying to use our local large language model and

5
00:00:19,700 --> 00:00:27,320
binding all these tools that we created earlier, including custom tools and also the Wikipedia tools

6
00:00:27,320 --> 00:00:29,510
and also playwright tools for that matter.

7
00:00:29,510 --> 00:00:37,310
And then we use these agent to to take the decision based on the query that we ask, or the prompt that

8
00:00:37,310 --> 00:00:39,440
we pass to the large language model.

9
00:00:39,440 --> 00:00:45,350
And then the agent will choose which tool which is appropriate to perform an action.

10
00:00:45,350 --> 00:00:47,720
And then it gives us the response back.

11
00:00:47,720 --> 00:00:55,760
And now in this Section we are going to go a level further where we are going to be doing a bit more

12
00:00:55,790 --> 00:01:02,800
operation, where we are going to be doing the or calling the rag as a tool.

13
00:01:02,800 --> 00:01:06,190
So we are going to create a rag tool this time.

14
00:01:06,220 --> 00:01:12,640
If you remember we were discussing about the rag earlier, which is nothing but the retrieval augmented

15
00:01:12,640 --> 00:01:20,140
generation where we can actually extract the data from external files, split them and embed them and

16
00:01:20,140 --> 00:01:21,430
store them into the database.

17
00:01:21,430 --> 00:01:28,600
And then we use this vector database to query, which can also be passed as a reference to our large

18
00:01:28,600 --> 00:01:35,230
language model, which can be retrieved and generate the answer for us from those stored data.

19
00:01:35,620 --> 00:01:41,260
So you already saw those information in our earlier sections of this course, and we are going to go

20
00:01:41,290 --> 00:01:50,710
a level further this time to use these Rag operations as well, along with our playwright tool, which

21
00:01:50,740 --> 00:01:54,640
actually helps us extract the data from any given web page.

22
00:01:54,640 --> 00:02:01,360
And then it is going to use the knowledge that it has acquired from the Rag or the retrieval augmented

23
00:02:01,360 --> 00:02:05,040
generation, like all the documentation and the knowledge it has got.

24
00:02:05,040 --> 00:02:09,960
And then based on both of these knowledge, it is going to give us the response for us.

25
00:02:09,960 --> 00:02:13,830
So you will see that we are going to use quite a lot of different tools this time, and we're going

26
00:02:13,830 --> 00:02:21,690
to empower our local large language model with all the information that it can then use to serve or

27
00:02:21,690 --> 00:02:25,410
help us serve the information that we are looking for.

28
00:02:25,410 --> 00:02:31,740
So now you will see that our local large language model is more powerful because now it has support

29
00:02:31,770 --> 00:02:39,060
to a lot of different tool bindings over here, along with the retrieval augmented generation data.

30
00:02:39,210 --> 00:02:43,440
So it's going to use this vector data store to retrieve the data as well.

31
00:02:43,440 --> 00:02:49,440
And then you can see that there is going to be a lot of potentials and power going to come for this

32
00:02:49,440 --> 00:02:51,810
particular large language model.

33
00:02:51,810 --> 00:02:58,380
So let's say if I'm going to give a page to this particular large language model asking is this page

34
00:02:58,380 --> 00:03:02,910
has got any bias for this particular web page?

35
00:03:02,910 --> 00:03:02,910
Webpage.

36
00:03:02,910 --> 00:03:09,990
So what is going to do is immediately the Q1 model will not have the ability to go and talk to the actual

37
00:03:09,990 --> 00:03:14,250
page, because the given page is an external internet page that it needs to access.

38
00:03:14,250 --> 00:03:20,970
So it will go and ask the agent, and the agent will know that which tool binding can help us extract

39
00:03:20,970 --> 00:03:24,330
the page information or navigate to that particular page.

40
00:03:24,330 --> 00:03:30,960
And now this guy, the the agent is going to invoke the the playwright tool for us.

41
00:03:30,960 --> 00:03:37,530
And based on that particular information, it is going to then, uh, it is going to invoke this particular

42
00:03:37,530 --> 00:03:42,210
tool, and this playwright is going to perform all the action for us, and then it's going to give the

43
00:03:42,210 --> 00:03:44,100
page information to the agent.

44
00:03:44,100 --> 00:03:50,310
And the next operation we asked is, how about, uh, whether the page is talking about any bias information?

45
00:03:50,310 --> 00:03:55,590
Now, the bias information details is something which is already available with this tool that we are

46
00:03:55,590 --> 00:04:02,070
going to be building in this particular section, where it is going to in turn call the, uh, Rag data,

47
00:04:02,100 --> 00:04:08,570
which is something we already have in our earlier sections, which is going to store the external informations

48
00:04:08,570 --> 00:04:14,870
from the PDF file, and it is stored in here in this particular vector data store, which is the data

49
00:04:14,870 --> 00:04:16,640
which has got all the different information.

50
00:04:16,640 --> 00:04:22,760
So we're going to use this data and then we're going to return it back from the tool to the agent.

51
00:04:22,760 --> 00:04:28,280
And then that agent is going to perform the rest of the operation for us to verify if there is a bias

52
00:04:28,280 --> 00:04:29,360
information there or not.

53
00:04:29,360 --> 00:04:35,150
And then it's going to give the information back to the to the customer or to the person who is asking

54
00:04:35,150 --> 00:04:36,410
this particular question.

55
00:04:36,440 --> 00:04:36,920
Look at that.

56
00:04:36,950 --> 00:04:38,900
There is so many things going on over here.

57
00:04:38,930 --> 00:04:40,880
I know this is a lot of different things.

58
00:04:40,880 --> 00:04:42,050
Uh, over here.

59
00:04:42,080 --> 00:04:46,580
This is just like a fun project which I have created, and I wanted to show you how we can utilize all

60
00:04:46,580 --> 00:04:51,170

61

63
00:04:58,820 --> 00:05:05,300
your agent more powerful and more meaningful, to return you the answer that you're looking for?

And you will notice that this book, this summarize, should be sufficient to understand the bias in

177

this article.

179
