# Section 13: Understanding EvaluatingTesting of LLM Application


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,140 --> 00:00:05,870
Welcome to the next Section of our course building and testing AI agents, rags and chatbots using Lang

2
00:00:05,870 --> 00:00:09,500
chain with Olama and local large language models.

3
00:00:09,530 --> 00:00:15,710
And in this Section we'll be talking about testing, also known as evaluation of our large language

4
00:00:15,710 --> 00:00:16,460
models.

5
00:00:16,460 --> 00:00:21,470
So we are going to understand how we can actually perform that evaluation of large language model in

6
00:00:21,470 --> 00:00:22,250
this particular section.

7
00:00:22,250 --> 00:00:29,090
So Llms are typically evaluated using standardized set of data sets designed to assess their performance

8
00:00:29,090 --> 00:00:35,570
across a diverse range of tasks, such as text summarization or open book question answering or code

9
00:00:35,570 --> 00:00:42,140
generation and language understanding, and these data sets serves as a benchmark to provide a fair

10
00:00:42,140 --> 00:00:48,650
and competitive analysis of different models, helping researchers and developers gauge their effectiveness

11
00:00:48,680 --> 00:00:50,870
in handling real world scenarios.

12
00:00:50,870 --> 00:00:54,140
So that is how we can really assess a large language model.

13
00:00:54,140 --> 00:01:01,070
It's not something like a normal software code that you write in a Python, and then you assess it,

14
00:01:01,070 --> 00:01:06,130
that whatever that we have written in the Lang chain and then verify some unit test and write it with

15
00:01:06,130 --> 00:01:07,210
some end to end test.

16
00:01:07,240 --> 00:01:12,310
Now those things, we can't do it because we are really accessing an large language model in here in

17
00:01:12,310 --> 00:01:13,480
order for us to test them.

18
00:01:13,480 --> 00:01:16,960
These are the things that we have to do to make this happen.

19
00:01:18,160 --> 00:01:24,880
And in the large language model, evaluation metrics used in the benchmarking LMS vary depending on

20
00:01:24,880 --> 00:01:26,650
the type of task being assessed.

21
00:01:26,680 --> 00:01:29,830
Most tasks are evaluated using traditional LNP metrics.

22
00:01:29,830 --> 00:01:31,360
Some of these metrics are.

23
00:01:31,360 --> 00:01:36,580
The traditional metrics are like exact match, which measures whether the model generates the answer

24
00:01:36,790 --> 00:01:40,000
is an exact word to word match with the ground truth.

25
00:01:40,030 --> 00:01:45,820
And there are other common metrics includes the blue or bilingual evaluation, understudy for machine

26
00:01:45,820 --> 00:01:52,990
translation, and there is also called as rogue or otherwise called as recall oriented understudy for

27
00:01:53,170 --> 00:01:54,070
gisting evaluation.

28
00:01:54,070 --> 00:01:57,340
So these are the things that is already there to validate it.

29
00:01:57,340 --> 00:02:02,230
And the final one is the F1 score for the classification and information retrieval task.

30
00:02:02,230 --> 00:02:08,140
So you can see that these are the traditional way of testing the large language models.

31
00:02:08,140 --> 00:02:15,530
So if you have really got my other course in Udemy on understanding, test and fine tuning the AI models

32
00:02:15,530 --> 00:02:16,550
with hugging face.

33
00:02:16,580 --> 00:02:22,670
In this particular course, I have also talked about how we can do functional testing of the AI models

34
00:02:22,670 --> 00:02:28,160
using temperature testing, repeatability testing, question answering, named entity recognition and

35
00:02:28,160 --> 00:02:28,610
stuff.

36
00:02:28,610 --> 00:02:36,380
And I have also talked about the evaluation of the AI model using the unseen data as question and answering

37
00:02:36,380 --> 00:02:43,670
models using NLP libraries, and also how we can do the fuzzy match logic and also the bias fairness

38
00:02:43,670 --> 00:02:45,260
and testing of the AI models.

39
00:02:45,260 --> 00:02:49,490
So these are things that you can see that most of these testing that we are doing in this particular

40
00:02:49,490 --> 00:02:57,410
course are exclusively for testing the large language models in such a way that they actually respond

41
00:02:57,410 --> 00:03:00,530
as based on the ground truth that we have got.

42
00:03:00,530 --> 00:03:05,690
And the same thing happens over here as well with the exact match.

43
00:03:05,690 --> 00:03:14,360
Blue, rogue and F1 score for the classification and the LM evaluation metrics fall into two broad categories.

44
00:03:14,360 --> 00:03:17,200
One is the traditional and non-traditional, as I just told you.

45
00:03:17,200 --> 00:03:20,800
So traditional we just saw in our last slide.

46
00:03:20,800 --> 00:03:26,290
And the non-traditional metrics leverages the semantic understanding and the model's own capability

47
00:03:26,290 --> 00:03:27,970
to assess generated text.

48
00:03:27,970 --> 00:03:34,870
And this can work with or without reference text using techniques like embedding similarities, perplexity,

49
00:03:34,870 --> 00:03:36,970
and LM based scoring.

50
00:03:37,030 --> 00:03:45,100
You might have seen these things like embedding similarities and perplexity in our earlier lectures

51
00:03:45,100 --> 00:03:49,690
of this course or earlier sections of this course where we are doing embedding, while we are doing

52
00:03:49,690 --> 00:03:52,840
the vector database to store the data.

53
00:03:52,840 --> 00:03:59,290
And we needed the embedding models to do the embedding of the conversion of the text to vectors.

54
00:03:59,290 --> 00:04:01,420
So we were using an embedding models.

55
00:04:01,420 --> 00:04:03,790
So embedding is a technique to do that.

56
00:04:03,790 --> 00:04:05,440
And uh similarities.

57
00:04:05,440 --> 00:04:12,340
We also saw the similarities score for the vector database while we were trying to pass a text.

58
00:04:12,340 --> 00:04:14,080
And it was giving us a response.

59
00:04:14,080 --> 00:04:16,420
And we were doing the embedding similarities over there.

60
00:04:16,420 --> 00:04:20,630
So those things we have already seen even in our earlier sections of this course.

61
00:04:20,630 --> 00:04:26,510
And that's exactly what they're talking about over here, as well as one of the non-traditional metrics

62
00:04:26,510 --> 00:04:30,710
that you can use to evaluate how your LLM really behaves.

63
00:04:31,100 --> 00:04:37,370
And we'll talk about the another most important part of the evaluation is the bird score.

64
00:04:37,370 --> 00:04:40,040
So this is a bidirectional encoding based approach.

65
00:04:40,040 --> 00:04:46,340
That is candidates text and reference text are fed into the DL model separately to obtain embeddings.

66
00:04:46,340 --> 00:04:52,310
And the token level embeddings are then used to calculate the pairwise cosine similarity matrix over

67
00:04:52,310 --> 00:04:52,850
here.

68
00:04:52,850 --> 00:04:58,730
So this is the way that you can use the embedding uh, to perform the similarities of the text.

69
00:04:59,330 --> 00:05:05,600
So you can see that these are the different ways that we can use the techniques to perform the testing,

70
00:05:05,600 --> 00:05:07,430
uh, in the large language models.

71
00:05:07,430 --> 00:05:12,170
But because we are not going to be focusing just on the large language model, but we are focusing on

72
00:05:12,170 --> 00:05:19,730
how we can test the application, which is built with lang chain like AI agents or Rag based applications

73
00:05:19,730 --> 00:05:21,500
or even the chatbots.

74
00:05:21,500 --> 00:05:23,540
How do we actually perform these operations?

75
00:05:23,540 --> 00:05:26,270
We are going to talk about that in our next lecture.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,080 --> 00:00:04,640
In this lecture we talk about non-traditional evaluation of large language models.

2
00:00:04,640 --> 00:00:10,640
So beyond traditional metrics like blue or rogue, more advanced evaluation techniques focuses on semantic

3
00:00:10,670 --> 00:00:13,430
understanding and fluency of the text.

4
00:00:13,460 --> 00:00:18,410
Three such techniques are embedding similarities perplexity and LM based scoring.

5
00:00:18,440 --> 00:00:20,600
Like how we saw in our last lecture.

6
00:00:20,600 --> 00:00:23,240
So what is this embedding similarity is all about?

7
00:00:23,240 --> 00:00:29,180
Well, embedding similarity measures how semantically close two pieces of text are, rather than just

8
00:00:29,180 --> 00:00:31,100
comparing exact word.

9
00:00:31,130 --> 00:00:33,800
This is done by using similarity between two vectors.

10
00:00:33,800 --> 00:00:39,950
Generated text and reference text is calculated using the cosine similarities Euclidean distance or

11
00:00:39,980 --> 00:00:40,970
dot product.

12
00:00:40,970 --> 00:00:45,920
So these are the things that we use to do the vector comparison over there.

13
00:00:45,920 --> 00:00:52,490
And if you remember, we were also doing this vector similarity using the vector store of similarity

14
00:00:52,490 --> 00:00:56,870
underscore search with score we try to provide like explain.

15
00:00:56,870 --> 00:00:58,280
The attention is all you need.

16
00:00:58,310 --> 00:01:03,470
And we also had one more result which is saying explain attention in transformer.

17
00:01:03,470 --> 00:01:08,550
So we were having two uh same similar kind of the text over there.

18
00:01:08,580 --> 00:01:10,260
They talk about the same thing.

19
00:01:10,260 --> 00:01:12,060
You can see that attention is all you need.

20
00:01:12,090 --> 00:01:13,740
And attention in transformer.

21
00:01:13,860 --> 00:01:19,350
While this vector database had got the similar storage of the text in there after embedding.

22
00:01:19,350 --> 00:01:22,350
So we saw the score were pretty close for both of them.

23
00:01:22,350 --> 00:01:24,060
So they are not like different at all.

24
00:01:24,060 --> 00:01:25,350
They're pretty close to that.

25
00:01:25,440 --> 00:01:27,660
And that was just printed for us over here.

26
00:01:27,660 --> 00:01:33,900
So this is how we verified like how semantically close two pieces of text are rather than just comparing

27
00:01:33,900 --> 00:01:35,160
the exact word.

28
00:01:35,190 --> 00:01:38,280
So this we already saw in our earlier sections of this course before.

29
00:01:39,510 --> 00:01:41,370
And the next one is the perplexity.

30
00:01:41,370 --> 00:01:46,770
So perplexity is the measure of how well a language model predicts a given text.

31
00:01:46,800 --> 00:01:50,640
Lower perplexity means better fluency and coherence.

32
00:01:50,640 --> 00:01:57,690
So that is another way that you can measure or predict how a given text is going to be performed during

33
00:01:57,690 --> 00:01:59,010
the given point of time.

34
00:01:59,010 --> 00:02:01,560
And finally, the LM based scoring.

35
00:02:01,560 --> 00:02:06,600
This is quite interesting because we are going to be doing this LM based scoring in this particular

36
00:02:06,600 --> 00:02:11,040
course most of the time, because that is amazing, because you will see that instead of relying on

37
00:02:11,040 --> 00:02:15,960
traditional metrics, LMS themselves can be used to evaluate generated text.

38
00:02:15,990 --> 00:02:22,470
This can be done using prompt based evaluation, where another LM is prompted to rate the generated

39
00:02:22,470 --> 00:02:27,960
response based on criteria like relevance, coherence, factuality, or and grammatically.

40
00:02:27,960 --> 00:02:30,960
And then it also does the ranking based evaluation.

41
00:02:30,990 --> 00:02:33,810
The LM compares multiple outputs and rank them.

42
00:02:33,810 --> 00:02:36,240
And finally self-consistency.

43
00:02:36,270 --> 00:02:40,740
The LM generates multiple responses and evaluates them for consistency.

44
00:02:40,740 --> 00:02:48,840
So you will notice that in here, for the very first time, we are going to be using another LMS, which

45
00:02:48,840 --> 00:02:55,680
is going to act as a teacher LMS, which is going to act as a teacher, LMS, which is going to really,

46
00:02:55,680 --> 00:02:58,140
really stand aside.

47
00:02:58,140 --> 00:03:06,420
And then it is going to be it is going to be helping you to test how you can actually verify the prompt

48
00:03:06,420 --> 00:03:10,050
based evaluation or ranking based evaluation and self-consistency.

49
00:03:10,050 --> 00:03:13,980
So there are going to be two LMS to perform an action in here.

50
00:03:13,980 --> 00:03:21,330
And this is the one way of you to verify the LM based scoring to see if they are as expected.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:04,580
In this lecture we'll be talking about LMS evaluation libraries.

2
00:00:04,580 --> 00:00:09,950
So what are the different LMS evaluation libraries available to perform these operations that we were

3
00:00:09,950 --> 00:00:13,310
talking about like traditional and non-traditional way of testing them.

4
00:00:13,310 --> 00:00:17,300
There are many libraries available to evaluate the large language models.

5
00:00:17,300 --> 00:00:22,070
Some of the most popular among the community are the OpenAI's evals library.

6
00:00:22,070 --> 00:00:25,730
And there is another one is called as the Ragas Library.

7
00:00:25,730 --> 00:00:27,380
So OpenAI's evolves.

8
00:00:27,380 --> 00:00:33,620
As it mentioned, the evals provide a framework for evaluating large language models or systems built

9
00:00:33,620 --> 00:00:34,550
using LMS.

10
00:00:34,580 --> 00:00:43,460
It offers an existing registry of evals to test different dimensions of OpenAI models, and the ability

11
00:00:43,460 --> 00:00:51,050
to write your own custom evolves for use case you care about, and this is mainly focused on how you

12
00:00:51,050 --> 00:00:58,340
can do testing of your models with OpenAI specific models, which is that's where this eval is very

13
00:00:58,340 --> 00:00:58,730
good at.

14
00:00:58,730 --> 00:01:00,560
And it does this for you.

15
00:01:00,560 --> 00:01:05,890
And there is another library, as I told you, is going to be the Ragas Library and the ragas is the

16
00:01:05,890 --> 00:01:12,340
open source evaluation library built to supercharge evaluation of large language model applications.

17
00:01:12,370 --> 00:01:21,310
It uses a Sota LMS assisted method to quantify the performance of LMS application using automated metrics,

18
00:01:21,310 --> 00:01:28,000
so you can also evaluate the performance of the pipeline that you have built using frameworks like Lama

19
00:01:28,000 --> 00:01:32,920
index, using it without needing an annotated evaluation data set.

20
00:01:32,920 --> 00:01:35,320
So you can do all these things in ragas.

21
00:01:35,350 --> 00:01:40,840
And just to keep things very, very straightforward, I'm going to quickly show you these two libraries

22
00:01:40,840 --> 00:01:44,470
pages and you will understand how these libraries are really built.

23
00:01:44,470 --> 00:01:52,420
So if I just go to go and search for the open AI evals like that, so you see that this is the page

24
00:01:52,420 --> 00:01:53,860
it's going to take me up through.

25
00:01:53,860 --> 00:01:57,430
And this is the same text that I just copy pasted from here on the slide.

26
00:01:57,430 --> 00:02:03,370
So it says that if you are building with LMS, creating high quality eval is one of the most impactful

27
00:02:03,370 --> 00:02:05,200
things that you can do without evals.

28
00:02:05,200 --> 00:02:10,610
It can be very difficult time intensive to understand how different models version might affect your

29
00:02:10,610 --> 00:02:11,330
use case.

30
00:02:11,330 --> 00:02:15,890
And that is what this the president of the OpenAI really says in here.

31
00:02:15,920 --> 00:02:17,930
The setup operation is quite straightforward.

32
00:02:17,930 --> 00:02:20,180
All you need to have is the OpenAI key.

33
00:02:20,420 --> 00:02:27,860
And then you can you can verify that using the using this installation of this particular library over

34
00:02:27,860 --> 00:02:28,220
here.

35
00:02:28,220 --> 00:02:30,590
And you can see how they are doing the testing.

36
00:02:30,590 --> 00:02:35,330
So you can see that there is a walkthrough of the testing process of building an evolve over here,

37
00:02:35,360 --> 00:02:43,670
like how you can build things and how you can measure, uh, different, uh, operation using the eval.

38
00:02:43,700 --> 00:02:49,310
The focus of this particular course is not about talking about the OpenAI's evolve, but we are going

39
00:02:49,340 --> 00:02:56,090
to be talking about the ragas, which is the library again, which performs the same kind of evaluation.

40
00:02:56,090 --> 00:03:03,530
But again, ragas is a library which will helps you to perform a wide range of testing in wide range

41
00:03:03,530 --> 00:03:05,840
of models, as you can see over here.

42
00:03:05,840 --> 00:03:10,760
And they also support Lang chain, which can also be a plus for us because we are using land chain for

43
00:03:10,760 --> 00:03:15,200
our, uh, for our development as well as they support Olama.

44
00:03:15,200 --> 00:03:19,010
So we can use these capabilities while we do the testing.

45
00:03:19,010 --> 00:03:20,480
So that is the major power.

46
00:03:20,510 --> 00:03:24,080
And that's the reason why I want to go with the ragas in this particular course.

47
00:03:24,350 --> 00:03:27,260
And you can see that they have got some details over here.

48
00:03:27,260 --> 00:03:33,140
And they also have got a GitHub repo which tells you like how you can use with the ragas itself.

49
00:03:33,140 --> 00:03:35,660
So the installation process is very straightforward.

50
00:03:35,660 --> 00:03:41,120
All you have to do is just install this particular ragas using this pip install ragas uh over here and

51
00:03:41,120 --> 00:03:42,830
then you can start doing the evaluation.

52
00:03:42,830 --> 00:03:46,610
Just five lines of code will do that evaluation for you.

53
00:03:46,640 --> 00:03:49,640
We are going to run these kind of things in our uh, discussion.

54
00:03:49,670 --> 00:03:52,070
I will quickly show you like how you can achieve that.

55
00:03:52,190 --> 00:03:55,130
Again, they are using what is called as the chat OpenAI.

56
00:03:55,160 --> 00:04:01,400
But because we have the, uh, LMS, the Lang chain support, we can use different models as well if

57
00:04:01,400 --> 00:04:02,240
you wanted to.

58
00:04:02,480 --> 00:04:05,900
Um, yeah, that's about the the ragas library.

59
00:04:05,900 --> 00:04:08,570
But I have not gone very deep into this particular library yet.

60
00:04:08,570 --> 00:04:14,210
But you can see that this is the library that we are going to be using for our discussion.


━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,200 --> 00:00:03,380
In this lecture we are going to be understanding ragas a bit.

2
00:00:03,410 --> 00:00:10,010
So the ragas is otherwise called as the Retrieval Augmented Generation Assurance Score library is designed

3
00:00:10,010 --> 00:00:18,620
to evaluate Rag systems and large language model systems by assessing various aspects like the retrieval

4
00:00:18,620 --> 00:00:23,540
accuracy or generation quality and factual correctness.

5
00:00:23,540 --> 00:00:31,310
That is the main purpose of this ragas itself, and you'll be wondered that using this particular ragas,

6
00:00:31,340 --> 00:00:33,980
we can check key metrics.

7
00:00:33,980 --> 00:00:34,910
Something like this.

8
00:00:34,940 --> 00:00:42,770
We can check the context, relevance, faithfulness, answer relevance and factual consistency or otherwise

9
00:00:42,770 --> 00:00:46,850
called as correctness of our large language models responses.

10
00:00:46,970 --> 00:00:53,510
It can be anything, it can be LMS, or it can also be the one from the Rag application or agent.

11
00:00:53,510 --> 00:00:57,410
You can verify all of them using ragas, something like this.

12
00:00:58,280 --> 00:01:05,270
So if you talk about the context precision, this measurement of this is the measurement of how many.

13
00:01:05,300 --> 00:01:08,240
Retrieved contexts are actually relevant.

14
00:01:08,240 --> 00:01:10,190
So the higher the precision, the fewer.

15
00:01:10,220 --> 00:01:13,100
Irrelevant documents are there in the retrieval.

16
00:01:13,100 --> 00:01:14,540
That is what is called as the.

17
00:01:14,570 --> 00:01:15,920
Context precision.

18
00:01:15,920 --> 00:01:18,860
So these are things that we are going to be using a bit while we.

19
00:01:18,890 --> 00:01:27,200
Are going to be doing the evaluation of our matrices using evaluation of our LMS using.

20
00:01:27,230 --> 00:01:27,890
Ragas.

21
00:01:27,890 --> 00:01:29,750
So the context precision is one thing.

22
00:01:29,750 --> 00:01:31,580
And then there is also called as context.

23
00:01:31,610 --> 00:01:37,580
Recall which measure how many relevant contexts were retrieved out of all possible.

24
00:01:37,610 --> 00:01:38,510
Relevant ones.

25
00:01:38,510 --> 00:01:44,360
That is what is called as this context recall and the high the most relevant documents were retrieved.

26
00:01:44,420 --> 00:01:45,320
The lawyer.

27
00:01:45,350 --> 00:01:51,350
Is very less documents were retrieved based on the given context and then response relevance.

28
00:01:51,350 --> 00:01:53,330
So this is the measure.

29
00:01:53,330 --> 00:01:58,640
If the answer is actually addresses the query that you have asked.

30
00:01:58,670 --> 00:02:02,870
The higher the response is a meaningful and on the topic response that you get it.

31
00:02:02,900 --> 00:02:03,380
If not.

32
00:02:03,410 --> 00:02:05,180
You're not really getting what you're looking for.

33
00:02:05,200 --> 00:02:06,730
finally faithfulness.

34
00:02:06,760 --> 00:02:13,690
This is, I think, very important because it is the measure for you to check if the factually grounded

35
00:02:13,720 --> 00:02:17,320
truth truth is coming up for you in the response or not.

36
00:02:17,350 --> 00:02:23,260
The higher the response, the factual grounded, which means you are really avoiding quite a lot of

37
00:02:23,260 --> 00:02:26,680
different hallucination generated by your large language models.

38
00:02:26,710 --> 00:02:32,830
So this is another measure that you are going to be verifying while you test your application using

39
00:02:32,830 --> 00:02:33,580
ragas.

40
00:02:33,610 --> 00:02:38,680
So these are the different types of matrices that you can really measure with ragas.

41
00:02:38,680 --> 00:02:45,040
And we are going to see a few of them, at least three of them from here in our testing to verify if

42
00:02:45,040 --> 00:02:47,590
we get the responses like how we are looking for.

43
00:02:47,590 --> 00:02:54,190
So these are the theoretical aspects of all the different ways of testing the large language models

44
00:02:54,190 --> 00:02:59,080
and LMS applications, as well as the agents or rags.

45
00:02:59,080 --> 00:03:05,950
But starting our next lecture, we are going to see how we can actually start using ragas to do the

46
00:03:05,950 --> 00:03:08,800
testing itself in a much, much better fashion.

00:04:26,890 --> 00:04:29,980

67

68

69
00:04:33,460 --> 00:04:42,790
So while we say score is one, which means we are really saying that the LM is really matching the,

70
00:04:42,790 --> 00:04:44,530
uh, the actual response.

71
00:04:44,530 --> 00:04:50,380
So the user input and the response is actually matching based on the large language models that we are

72
00:04:50,380 --> 00:04:50,980
passing in.

73
00:04:51,010 --> 00:04:54,580
So let's go and see if the Lang Smith has got any detail over there.

74
00:04:54,580 --> 00:04:56,200
And you can see that we have got it.

75
00:04:56,200 --> 00:04:59,980
So there is an um summary accuracy.

76
00:04:59,980 --> 00:05:02,770
So this is a singleton aspect critic prompt.

77
00:05:02,770 --> 00:05:03,910
You can see over here.

78
00:05:03,910 --> 00:05:08,590
And it gives us a um a response for us over there.

79
00:05:09,190 --> 00:05:12,550
So the human is asking this question, uh, over here.

80
00:05:12,550 --> 00:05:15,190
And now the output is this particular value.

81
00:05:15,190 --> 00:05:21,520
So the human asks for this question and the response is pretty close to what they are expecting.

82
00:05:21,520 --> 00:05:24,070
That's the reason why the verdict is one, which means it's correct.

83
00:05:24,070 --> 00:05:31,240
So this is how we can see that we could able to evaluate this particular answer and this kind of testing

84
00:05:31,240 --> 00:05:31,930
in ragas.

85
00:05:31,930 --> 00:05:33,730
They call it a singleton sample.

86
00:05:33,730 --> 00:05:40,330
The reason why this is called a singleton sample is because the evaluation has got only single turn

87
00:05:40,330 --> 00:05:41,590
interaction, which is this one.

88
00:05:41,890 --> 00:05:43,450
There is only one interaction here.

89
00:05:43,450 --> 00:05:45,790
So there is one user input and one response.

90
00:05:45,790 --> 00:05:48,790
And that's why it is called a singleton sample.

91
00:05:48,790 --> 00:05:53,890
If you have multiple user input and multiple responses, and you're going to be verifying how the interactions

92
00:05:53,890 --> 00:05:58,390
is really happening with your local large language model, then they are called as multi-turn samples.

93
00:05:58,390 --> 00:06:00,880
So you can just see that there is something called as multi-turn samples.

94
00:06:00,880 --> 00:06:02,830
So you can pass that.

95
00:06:02,830 --> 00:06:08,170
And then you can verify if the Multi-turn sample is also working in that particular fashion.

96
00:06:08,170 --> 00:06:14,620
So this is how you can actually verify the different different things within your large language models

97
00:06:14,620 --> 00:06:15,910
with ragas.

98
00:06:16,180 --> 00:06:23,200
And I hope you got the idea of the first test with the ragas over here with your local large language

99
00:06:23,200 --> 00:06:23,740
model.

And the next example that we are going to talk about is the multi-turn sample in the ragas.

00:00:05,180 --> 00:00:09,620
After we discussed this singleton sample in our last lecture of this course.

3
00:00:09,620 --> 00:00:15,020
So you see that in the multi-turn sample, the only difference from the singleton and the multi-turn

4
00:00:15,020 --> 00:00:19,850
is that in the singleton you will have only one user input and one reference context.

5
00:00:19,850 --> 00:00:22,790
And then you just do a communication with the LM.

6
00:00:22,790 --> 00:00:28,880
And then you see if the response has got the accuracy, relevance and fairness based on the input that

7
00:00:28,880 --> 00:00:30,980
you have given from the large language model.

8
00:00:30,980 --> 00:00:34,670
That is what you are going to do in this particular singleton sample.

9
00:00:34,670 --> 00:00:41,870
But in Multi-turn sample you are going to be interacting between humans eyes or optionally even tools.

10
00:00:41,900 --> 00:00:46,130

11

12

13

14

17

of messages while we do the communication with raga.

which is the human message.

# Section 14: Evaluating RAG Systems built with LangChain and RAGAs



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,140 --> 00:00:02,030
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:02,030 --> 00:00:09,680
And in this Section we are going to talk about bit more complex option of testing our systems using

3
00:00:09,710 --> 00:00:10,250
ragas.

4
00:00:10,250 --> 00:00:16,670
So we are going to be testing our Raag based system using a ragas over here.

5
00:00:16,670 --> 00:00:23,930
So if you remember in our last sections of our course, we talked about the uh Raag system, like how

6
00:00:23,930 --> 00:00:31,400
we built a RAC by reading the data from the PDF file and how we extracted those data and stored into

7
00:00:31,430 --> 00:00:36,350
a vector database, uh, in the Croma database, these things.

8
00:00:36,350 --> 00:00:37,790
And we also retrieved the data.

9
00:00:37,820 --> 00:00:46,370
Now, our idea is to test this Rag system in such a way that we can, uh, we can actually verify if

10
00:00:46,370 --> 00:00:55,850
our, uh, agent is going to get the correct data from this particular database, which is being extracted.

11
00:00:55,850 --> 00:01:05,380
And the way that we can evaluate this particular Rag system essentially, is to Uh, have a test set

12
00:01:05,380 --> 00:01:12,370
of data which we are going to be injecting and verifying if the response that we're looking for is absolutely

13
00:01:12,370 --> 00:01:17,710
correct or not, but for doing that, definitely with the human intervention, if you do it, it's going

14
00:01:17,740 --> 00:01:23,020
to take a lot of time because you need to create quite a lot of big data set for doing that, but instead

15
00:01:23,020 --> 00:01:32,020
you can actually create a sample data set and then try to evaluate using the large language model as

16
00:01:32,020 --> 00:01:33,160
an evaluator.

17
00:01:33,160 --> 00:01:34,750
So you can do that as well.

18
00:01:34,750 --> 00:01:37,000
That is going to make your life even more simpler.

19
00:01:37,000 --> 00:01:43,090
That is what the non-traditional testing we were talking about in our last Section of this course.

20
00:01:43,090 --> 00:01:49,060
So we're going to be using the non-traditional testing approach, where the LMS being the evaluator,

21
00:01:49,060 --> 00:01:51,580
because it will have all the context of what to verify.

22
00:01:51,580 --> 00:01:58,720
And you will have the vector database is going to give you the details, which gives the detail to the

23
00:01:58,720 --> 00:01:59,230
LMS.

24
00:01:59,230 --> 00:02:03,010
And you also will have an expected outcome that you are looking for.

25
00:02:03,010 --> 00:02:10,550
And then you can evaluate all of these together Within your, uh, within your ragas over here.

26
00:02:10,550 --> 00:02:12,680
So you'll notice that in here.

27
00:02:12,800 --> 00:02:17,810
Um, so you have got a prompt that has been sent over here and this particular prompt data that you're

28
00:02:17,810 --> 00:02:20,900
going to have or nothing but your test data.

29
00:02:20,900 --> 00:02:33,860
So here is where all your test data, uh, with uh, expected and actual, uh, prompt, uh, request

30
00:02:34,640 --> 00:02:37,370
and response are going to be sitting.

31
00:02:37,370 --> 00:02:44,300
So let me make this as uh, medium, very large so that you can see a bit more clearly.

32
00:02:44,300 --> 00:02:46,910
So that is what we are going to be doing over here as a prompt.

33
00:02:46,910 --> 00:02:52,670
And then we're going to be sending to um, uh, sending not to the, to the model directly, but we

34
00:02:52,700 --> 00:02:59,330
are going to be sending it to the, uh, to the evaluator so that we can verify whether we have got

35
00:02:59,330 --> 00:03:05,750
the context, recall faithfulness and factual correctness of the responses that we are looking for.

36
00:03:05,750 --> 00:03:08,630
So that is the whole idea over here.

37
00:03:08,630 --> 00:03:15,920
So in order for us to perform this testing operation, we do require our earlier sections knowledge.

38
00:03:15,920 --> 00:03:23,270
We are going to be using the chroma database, which is going to store the data, uh, or the documents.

39

00:03:51,290 --> 00:03:52,130

And the agent keep retrying, retrying, retrying with the the operation Uh, until the chat, uh,

57
00:03:19,560 --> 00:03:23,730
OpenAI gave us a result, and we got an output over here.

58
00:03:23,760 --> 00:03:24,420
Look at that.

59
00:03:24,450 --> 00:03:26,460
Like this is the JSON value.

60
00:03:26,490 --> 00:03:28,830
We can now fine tune this even further.

61
00:03:28,830 --> 00:03:30,120
Like this is a statement.

62
00:03:30,120 --> 00:03:34,950
There is a reason, uh, and it's not giving us the verdict correctly.

63
00:03:34,950 --> 00:03:40,200
But if we try to fine tune our tool a bit and the way we write the prompt, these things can be even

64
00:03:40,200 --> 00:03:41,040
more better as well.

65
00:03:41,040 --> 00:03:48,330
But still, what we have achieved here is pretty close to how realistically you can test your rag as

66
00:03:48,330 --> 00:03:49,530
well as your agent.

67
00:03:49,620 --> 00:03:50,730
This is pretty cool guys.

68
00:03:50,730 --> 00:03:58,050
So this is how we can see that we could able to test our rag as well as an agent in much easier fashion

69
00:03:58,050 --> 00:03:59,850
using ragas.

70
00:04:00,750 --> 00:04:05,310
So this ends the complete advanced Section of how you can test your agents and ragas.

71
00:04:05,310 --> 00:04:10,590
And we have been discussing about the testing of the ragas quite a bit, and I'm sure this is going

72
00:04:10,620 --> 00:04:17,700
to give you an idea of how you can test your large language model, application for agents for rags,

73
00:04:17,700 --> 00:04:21,870
and also how you can take this further in your job.

74
00:04:21,900 --> 00:04:24,330
Congratulations and you guys have a great day.

So make sure while you run this test guys you need to have the OpenAI API key.

00:06:35,560 --> 00:06:36,850

00:06:39,070 --> 00:06:46,270

00:06:48,160 --> 00:06:50,470

111
00:06:50,470 --> 00:06:56,140
Player allows you to monitor, modify or block network request made by the web page during the testing.

112
00:06:56,350 --> 00:07:00,250
This feature is useful for debugging and ensuring that your application behaves correctly, but the

113
00:07:00,250 --> 00:07:04,150
expected output that I have proposed is the network interception.

114
00:07:04,150 --> 00:07:08,350
Playwright allows the user to modify, block or inspect network request and response, enabling better

115
00:07:08,350 --> 00:07:11,530
control over API calls and testing.

116
00:07:11,530 --> 00:07:19,580

# Section 17: Evaluating AI Agents Tool Calling with DeepEval



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,110 --> 00:00:01,760
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:01,760 --> 00:00:06,890
And in this Section we are going to talk about how we can test the AI agent tool callings.

3
00:00:06,890 --> 00:00:12,590
So tool calling is another important feature that we saw in our earlier sections of this course, where

4
00:00:12,590 --> 00:00:19,610
we were talking about how we can do use the tooling support that we have got over here that we have

5
00:00:19,640 --> 00:00:23,030
built, and we can fuse that with our AI agents.

6
00:00:23,030 --> 00:00:28,310
And the decision engine in this case is going to be our large language model.

7
00:00:28,310 --> 00:00:33,530
But still the tool is the one which is responsible for invoking the appropriate tool for us, for instance,

8
00:00:33,530 --> 00:00:39,020
the Wikipedia tool or the subtract custom tool or addition tool or playwright tool that we have built.

9
00:00:39,050 --> 00:00:44,240
So all these tools, it's going to be invoked by the AI agent for us.

10
00:00:44,240 --> 00:00:47,150
And these are the tools which are going to be invoked for us.

11
00:00:47,150 --> 00:00:52,430
And just consider this like we have got our organization with more than hundreds and thousands of tools,

12
00:00:52,430 --> 00:00:59,120
and we need to test how these tools are correctly being invoked based on a given input parameter.

13
00:00:59,120 --> 00:01:04,340
So in order for making that happen, we need to ensure that the AI agent calls the exact tool.

14
00:01:04,340 --> 00:01:11,060
And in order to measure this, we are going to be using our deep eval feature with tool correctness

15
00:01:11,110 --> 00:01:12,370
evaluation metrics.

16
00:01:12,370 --> 00:01:17,050
So this is the feature where it is going to be helping us to perform the same kind of operation that

17
00:01:17,050 --> 00:01:18,040
you are seeing over here.

18
00:01:18,040 --> 00:01:21,880
We are going to be passing the same kind of data to our deep valve.

19
00:01:21,880 --> 00:01:30,040
But just that over here we are going to verify whether the invoked tool coming from the actual tool

20
00:01:30,040 --> 00:01:34,270
is matching to the expected tool invocation that we have got.

21
00:01:34,300 --> 00:01:39,670
And the way that we can do it is using the tool correctness metrics available in deep eval, as you

22
00:01:39,670 --> 00:01:40,570
can see over here.

23
00:01:40,570 --> 00:01:47,380
So this tool correctness metric is an a generic LM metrics that assess your large language models agents

24
00:01:47,380 --> 00:01:49,360
function tool calling ability.

25
00:01:49,360 --> 00:01:57,010
And it calculated by comparing whether every tool that is expected to be used was intended as called

26
00:01:57,010 --> 00:01:57,880
there or not.

27
00:01:57,910 --> 00:02:01,000
And you can see that they have got some required arguments.

28
00:02:01,000 --> 00:02:04,840
You need to pass the input actual output tool called an expected tool.

29
00:02:04,840 --> 00:02:09,040
So these are the only thing which is as a required argument for this tool correctness metrics.

30
00:02:09,040 --> 00:02:11,590
And they have given a simple example over here.

31
00:02:11,590 --> 00:02:17,290
And most importantly you can notice that this particular tool metrics the tool correctness metrics.

32
00:02:17,290 --> 00:02:25,110
You can't really use it with the confident I portal so you can see that they don't really have an evaluate

33
00:02:25,110 --> 00:02:28,020
method like deep eval dot evaluate method.

34
00:02:28,020 --> 00:02:29,370
You can't really use it over here.

35
00:02:29,370 --> 00:02:35,550
So if you just go to the contextual relevance metrics over here, you can see that you can evaluate

36
00:02:35,550 --> 00:02:38,700
in bulk using this evaluate method.

37
00:02:38,700 --> 00:02:44,940
And this evaluate method is the one which is going to call the confident AI's portal.

38
00:02:44,940 --> 00:02:48,780
And it's going to show you the graph and all those things that we were doing in our last lecture section

39
00:02:48,780 --> 00:02:49,620
of this course.

40
00:02:49,620 --> 00:02:56,370
But over here, uh, in this particular tool, correctness, you can't see that there is going to be

41
00:02:56,370 --> 00:02:58,200
an evaluate method supported.

42
00:02:58,200 --> 00:03:03,780
So evaluate method is not supported to perform this operation at the moment during the time of recording.

43
00:03:03,780 --> 00:03:05,190
So maybe in future they will.

44
00:03:05,220 --> 00:03:10,830
But at the moment they don't really have the way to do a comparison of the tool correctness and things

45
00:03:10,830 --> 00:03:11,580
of that nature.

46
00:03:11,580 --> 00:03:18,990
So you don't see those information in your, uh, confident AI portal that we were discussing in our

47
00:03:18,990 --> 00:03:20,340
last Section of the course.

48
00:03:20,340 --> 00:03:23,490
So you don't see that fancy, uh, you know, graphs and stuff.

49
00:03:23,490 --> 00:03:25,800
But over here it is very straightforward.

50
00:03:25,800 --> 00:03:30,150
All you have to do is you need to just prepare the data for the LM test case, like how we did in our

51
00:03:30,150 --> 00:03:31,350
earlier Section of the course.

52
00:03:31,410 --> 00:03:34,350
And the only thing is you need to just call the tool correctness metrics.

53
00:03:34,350 --> 00:03:35,910
And then you can do a measure.

54
00:03:35,910 --> 00:03:37,770
And that is going to do the measurement for you.

55
00:03:37,770 --> 00:03:43,470
And they have got a lot of different parameters that you can pass in, uh, to ensure that the tool

56
00:03:43,470 --> 00:03:45,150
correctness mechanism works fine or not.

57
00:03:45,150 --> 00:03:50,760
And the way it is calculated is basically tool correctness is the number of correctly used tool, which

58
00:03:50,760 --> 00:03:54,600
is from the input and output parameter and the total number of tools being called.

59
00:03:54,630 --> 00:03:58,830
Based on that, it is going to give you the average of the tool correctness over here.

60
00:03:59,610 --> 00:04:06,360
And you will also notice that for the tool called parameter we need to have, we need to call a class

61
00:04:06,360 --> 00:04:07,440
called as tool call.

62
00:04:07,440 --> 00:04:09,540
And you need to pass the name of the tool.

63
00:04:09,540 --> 00:04:15,480
And you can also pass not just the tool call, but you can also pass the input parameter output parameter

64
00:04:15,660 --> 00:04:19,110
in this particular tool called so that it will become even more realistic.

65
00:04:19,110 --> 00:04:23,940
Because every tool that we build, we will have an input parameter and an output parameter, or maybe

66
00:04:23,940 --> 00:04:26,880
an output itself for from that particular tool.

67
00:04:26,880 --> 00:04:29,220
So we can pass those informations as well.

68
00:04:29,310 --> 00:04:33,690
So we will see how we can make all of these happen in this particular section.

69
00:04:33,690 --> 00:04:41,880
And we can ensure that we can also test the AI agents tool calling feature from within our tool Correctness

70
00:04:41,880 --> 00:04:42,660
metrics.

So what is the what is two minus four.

Then it's going to be minus two.

But if you have a if you have a situation where you also need to check for the queries, you can also

21
00:01:29,700 --> 00:01:32,790
do that in in multiple different orders as well.

22
00:01:32,820 --> 00:01:38,250
You remember, uh, in our earlier sections of this course, while I was showing you with the agent,

23
00:01:38,250 --> 00:01:44,430
you can ask two questions, uh, together, for instance, you can ask what is the sum of two and four?

24
00:01:44,430 --> 00:01:47,760
And did Donald Trump won 2024 presidential election?

25
00:01:47,760 --> 00:01:52,620
Then these two questions are going to be executing two chain calls.

26
00:01:52,680 --> 00:01:56,430
One is the Wikipedia and also another one is the ad numbers.

27
00:01:57,000 --> 00:02:02,220
So if you're going to verify these two are going to be called in a sequence of order.

28
00:02:02,220 --> 00:02:04,590
Or maybe they are called together as well.

29
00:02:04,590 --> 00:02:07,410
If you're going to verify in these kind of fashion you can do that.

30
00:02:07,410 --> 00:02:11,370
So all these metrics you can evaluate using this tool collection metrics.

31
00:02:11,580 --> 00:02:15,150
So in order to show you what are the things that you can pass over here.

32
00:02:15,150 --> 00:02:18,090
You can pass a threshold, let's say 0.5.

33
00:02:18,120 --> 00:02:19,710
I mean, the one is the threshold in our case.

34
00:02:19,710 --> 00:02:22,500
So I can just say it should be always one.

35
00:02:22,500 --> 00:02:23,520
You can do that.

36
00:02:23,520 --> 00:02:26,970
And you can pass the evaluation parameters over here.

37
00:02:26,970 --> 00:02:33,360
And the parameters is nothing but the the LM test case I think there is an there is something called

38
00:02:33,360 --> 00:02:37,980
as LM test case, uh, of uh test case parameters.

39
00:02:37,980 --> 00:02:39,990
So you can pass the params.

40
00:02:40,140 --> 00:02:43,200
And over here you can pass the input.

41
00:02:43,350 --> 00:02:46,650
So that is going to verify the input parameter.

42
00:02:46,770 --> 00:02:51,990
And similarly you can just say uh actual output.

43
00:02:51,990 --> 00:02:53,520
You can verify that.

44
00:02:53,520 --> 00:03:01,230
And then you can also verify uh the expected output and expected tools.

45
00:03:02,280 --> 00:03:07,920
So you can verify all of these in the evaluation parameter like how we have got all these informations

46
00:03:07,950 --> 00:03:09,720
in our data already.

47
00:03:10,110 --> 00:03:10,530
Right.

48
00:03:10,560 --> 00:03:13,830
These things you can verify it as well which is pretty cool.

49
00:03:14,070 --> 00:03:23,910
And you can also verify should include a reason true and should exact match, which is true because

50
00:03:23,910 --> 00:03:25,020
it should be exactly matching.

51
00:03:25,020 --> 00:03:27,030
The tool name should consider order.

52
00:03:27,060 --> 00:03:32,580
Probably not, because at the moment we don't really have any of these sequence or ordering, but I'm

53
00:03:32,580 --> 00:03:37,770
just going to say true because we just have only one tool at the moment and you can say strict mode

54
00:03:37,920 --> 00:03:39,960
as true as well if you wanted to.

55
00:03:40,410 --> 00:03:43,920
There is one bug if you try to include the verbose mode.

56
00:03:44,070 --> 00:03:47,280
I tried doing that like true, it just fails abruptly.

57
00:03:47,280 --> 00:03:48,540
I will show you in a minute.

58
00:03:48,900 --> 00:03:52,950
But these are the things that you can pass this tool metrics and this tool metric.

59
00:03:52,980 --> 00:03:56,070
You can pass it over here.

60
00:03:56,070 --> 00:04:01,530
So basically instead of creating the tool collection something like this, you can just say tool metrics

61
00:04:01,530 --> 00:04:02,670
dot measure.

62
00:04:02,760 --> 00:04:06,240
So this will measure every single things for you.

63
00:04:06,240 --> 00:04:08,550
So you get the score you get the reason.

64
00:04:08,760 --> 00:04:11,610
Um guess what I can just say score.

65
00:04:11,880 --> 00:04:27,360
Uh and reason and I can also print probably print uh metrics dot uh include reason and I can also print

66
00:04:27,480 --> 00:04:38,780
uh metrics dot expected tools, and I can also print a metrics dot evaluation parameters.

67
00:04:38,780 --> 00:04:43,520
So I can put all these over here for my reporting purpose, so that I can see that everything works

68
00:04:43,520 --> 00:04:45,080
as expected or not as well.

69
00:04:45,080 --> 00:04:45,650
Look at that.

70
00:04:45,650 --> 00:04:48,260
It's just going to show me every single things over there.

71
00:04:48,260 --> 00:04:54,620
So you see that this is this is the power guys like we have got like all of these information coming

72
00:04:54,620 --> 00:04:55,790
up for us over here.

73
00:04:55,790 --> 00:05:00,260
Like it is going to have the name like the parsing result.

74
00:05:00,260 --> 00:05:02,000
All the expected tools are coming up.

75
00:05:02,000 --> 00:05:07,610
And the true is for this, uh, is for the reason which is true.

76
00:05:07,610 --> 00:05:11,180
And then this is the tool being called uh, which is the include reason.

77
00:05:11,180 --> 00:05:13,430
And then we have got all this information.

78
00:05:13,430 --> 00:05:17,030
So expected tools for some reason is not coming up over there.

79
00:05:17,030 --> 00:05:19,010
But we have got the information.

80
00:05:19,010 --> 00:05:22,130
So but so yeah this is how you can see everything.

81
00:05:22,130 --> 00:05:23,210
You can print it out.

82
00:05:23,240 --> 00:05:29,270
As I told you, if you just give verbose mode as true for some reason it just gives me it runs in a

83
00:05:29,270 --> 00:05:31,130
loop and then it fails abruptly.

84
00:05:31,130 --> 00:05:31,970
Look at that.

85
00:05:32,180 --> 00:05:33,890
It just keeps on running like that.

86
00:05:34,160 --> 00:05:41,880
And at the at the end it just fails, saying that the maximum recursion depth exceeded and then it fails.

87
00:05:41,880 --> 00:05:47,280
So that's the reason why I don't want to show you the verbose mode as true there.

88
00:05:47,550 --> 00:05:50,370
But other than that, everything looks quite right.

89
00:05:50,400 --> 00:05:51,720
It just works fine.

90
00:05:51,990 --> 00:05:55,830
And this is how you can see the results are all coming up for you.

91
00:05:55,830 --> 00:06:01,050
So yeah, these are the things that we can actually do it from our tool correction metrics.

92
00:06:01,050 --> 00:06:03,930
We can pass multiple different parameters and we can get the response out.

93
00:06:03,960 --> 00:06:09,660
As I told you, the only bummer of this entire thing is that we can't we can't run the evaluate method,

94
00:06:09,660 --> 00:06:15,900
like how we did in our last section, which can run on the open AI and get the deep values result and

95
00:06:15,900 --> 00:06:23,310
get into the into the metrics in the dashboard, the confident AI, those things are still not supported.

96
00:06:23,430 --> 00:06:28,470
So you can't see something like these kind of fancy metrics, but at least you got the idea.

97
00:06:28,500 --> 00:06:30,780
You got the response coming up over here.

98
00:06:30,780 --> 00:06:35,580
This is how we can validate our agent calling the correct tool.

99

And it gets the responses like how we are looking for.

That's it guys.

# Section 18: Building MCP Server with FastMCP



━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1
00:00:00,160 --> 00:00:02,200
Welcome to the next Section of our course.

2
00:00:02,200 --> 00:00:07,720
And in this Section we are going to talk about how we can start building model Context Protocol server,

3
00:00:07,760 --> 00:00:11,280
otherwise called as the MCP server within our code.

4
00:00:12,280 --> 00:00:16,640
So what is Model Context protocol and what is MCP server in first place?

5
00:00:16,880 --> 00:00:22,720
We already discussed about MCP server briefly in our earlier sections of the course, like how we can

6
00:00:22,760 --> 00:00:30,960
invoke the MCP server as an agent, which can act like an bridge between your large language model and

7
00:00:30,960 --> 00:00:32,760
your local systems.

8
00:00:32,760 --> 00:00:34,200
That's exactly what is MCP.

9
00:00:34,200 --> 00:00:40,120
So basically, MCP is an open protocol that standardizes how application provides context to the large

10
00:00:40,120 --> 00:00:41,000
language model.

11
00:00:41,400 --> 00:00:45,560
Think of MCP is like an a USB-C port for an AI application.

12
00:00:45,560 --> 00:00:52,800
Just as an a USB-C port provides a standardized way to connect your devices to various peripherals and

13
00:00:52,800 --> 00:01:00,480
accessories, MCP provides a standardized way to connect AI models to different data sources and Toolings,

14
00:01:00,960 --> 00:01:07,680
and MCP enables you to build agents and complex workflows on the top of LMS and connect your models

15
00:01:07,680 --> 00:01:08,760
with the world.

16
00:01:09,040 --> 00:01:11,800
While they say with the world, it's like everything.

17
00:01:11,800 --> 00:01:17,280
So MCP, you can just think of that, like you can connect with whatever that you wanted to using the

18
00:01:17,280 --> 00:01:19,200
power of this open protocol.

19
00:01:19,200 --> 00:01:25,200
And LM gets access to almost everything, like how we did in our earlier sections of the course, using

20
00:01:25,360 --> 00:01:31,360
like long chain, like how we're able to connect with multiple different sources of our data.

21
00:01:31,400 --> 00:01:35,560
That's exactly what we're going to be doing in this particular MCP as well.

22
00:01:36,240 --> 00:01:43,840
So MCP follows a client server architecture where an MCP host an application like cloud code or cloud

23
00:01:43,840 --> 00:01:50,960
Desktop, or even cursor IDE or Visual Studio Code, GitHub Copilot or windsurf, whatever that you

24
00:01:51,000 --> 00:01:59,520
name it, any supported IDE or any supported systems which supports MCP Can act as an MCP host, which

25
00:01:59,560 --> 00:02:02,840
establishes connections to one or more MCP servers.

26
00:02:02,840 --> 00:02:06,000
So you don't have to just rely on only one MCP server.

27
00:02:06,000 --> 00:02:12,880
You can have multiple different MCP server while working with it, and the MCP host accomplishes this

28
00:02:12,880 --> 00:02:17,400
by creating one MCP client for each MCP server.

29
00:02:17,400 --> 00:02:25,160
And each MCP client maintains a dedicated one on one connection with its corresponding MCP server.

30
00:02:25,200 --> 00:02:26,800
That's how it really works over here.

31
00:02:26,800 --> 00:02:31,160
You see that how the connectivity happens with MCP host with multiple different clients, and once they

32
00:02:31,200 --> 00:02:33,240
are connected, they can just talk with each other.

33
00:02:33,240 --> 00:02:34,200
That's how they really work.

34
00:02:34,200 --> 00:02:37,200
That's the MCP server architecture.

35
00:02:38,240 --> 00:02:42,480
And we already discussed about the same thing even before in our earlier sections, like how MCP really

36
00:02:42,480 --> 00:02:42,720
works.

37
00:02:42,720 --> 00:02:45,320
So MCP has introduce act like a universal bridge.

38
00:02:45,320 --> 00:02:47,560
It has a client and server architecture.

39
00:02:47,560 --> 00:02:51,000
So in here the cloud desktop is the MCP server.

40
00:02:51,000 --> 00:02:58,240
And we're going to connect with multiple different local systems using the MCP servers via the MCP protocols,

41
00:02:58,240 --> 00:03:03,960
so there can be an MCP server which can access your local machines or GitHub so you can have one server

42
00:03:03,960 --> 00:03:04,720
or something like that.

43
00:03:04,760 --> 00:03:08,160
You can also have a server which is going to be connecting with your file systems.

44
00:03:08,160 --> 00:03:12,640
You can also have a MCP server which is going to connect with your Slack or Google Drive.

45
00:03:12,840 --> 00:03:19,480
Or you can also have a MCP server, which is going to act as an automation testing tools like playwright

46
00:03:19,480 --> 00:03:20,440
for that matter.

47
00:03:20,680 --> 00:03:23,720
Or so that's how this MCP server is going to really work.

48
00:03:23,720 --> 00:03:26,240
And that's the client server architecture in place.

49
00:03:27,040 --> 00:03:33,600
So we are going to build an MCP server in this particular course in this particular session, to be

50
00:03:33,600 --> 00:03:33,960
honest.

51
00:03:33,960 --> 00:03:35,200
So how are we actually going to do that.

52
00:03:35,200 --> 00:03:36,160
There are two ways to do it.

53
00:03:36,160 --> 00:03:43,880
One is official MCP server library, which is going to be for JavaScript and Python and C, something

54
00:03:43,880 --> 00:03:44,320
like that.

55
00:03:44,600 --> 00:03:48,880
And there is a fast MCP 2.0 which is very, very specific to Python.

56
00:03:48,880 --> 00:03:56,000
And we are going to be focusing on fast MCP 2.0 because we know that the python is our language of choice

57
00:03:56,000 --> 00:04:00,760
and we are going to build everything with fast MCP 2.0 approach, which is really, really fast.

58
00:04:00,760 --> 00:04:06,720
And I'm telling you, the way that you build this MCP server over here with the other toolings that

59
00:04:06,720 --> 00:04:13,920
we have built in earlier in our long chain approach by just noting, like add tools, pretty much same

60
00:04:13,960 --> 00:04:19,800
approach that we're going to be doing over here as well in the MCP to create an MCP server.

61
00:04:19,840 --> 00:04:20,760
This is really good.

62
00:04:20,760 --> 00:04:22,440
The fast MCP is really awesome.

63
00:04:22,440 --> 00:04:27,720
If you're going to be using the node version of MCP, you know how hard it is because I already built

64
00:04:27,720 --> 00:04:33,120
myself the playwright MCP server as well as the database MCP server using the node.

65
00:04:33,160 --> 00:04:37,840
It is really, really terrible because the code starts becoming very, very complex in due course of

66
00:04:37,840 --> 00:04:38,440
time.

67
00:04:38,440 --> 00:04:42,120
But if you're going to be using this fast MCP 2.0, it's really going to be amazing.

68
00:04:43,280 --> 00:04:43,760
So fast.

69
00:04:43,760 --> 00:04:48,080
MCP makes building MCP server and client simple and intuitive.

70
00:04:48,120 --> 00:04:55,920
It creates tools, exposes resources, defines prompts and more with clear pythonic code and fast.

71
00:04:55,960 --> 00:05:01,920
Mcpp is a standard framework for working with MCPs, which is Model Context, protocol and fast.

72
00:05:01,920 --> 00:05:10,920
MCP 1.0 was incorporated into the official MCP Python SDK in 2024, so it is already an official integration

73
00:05:10,920 --> 00:05:11,880
happen over there.

74
00:05:11,880 --> 00:05:16,960
So it's just going to work with the uh with the official MCP Python SDK already so fast.

75
00:05:17,000 --> 00:05:18,560
MCP is is pretty good.

76
00:05:18,600 --> 00:05:20,520
They have already seen the benefits.

77
00:05:20,520 --> 00:05:26,800
So the model context protocol library or SDK has already incorporated fast MCP in there.

78
00:05:26,800 --> 00:05:28,720
So that's what we are going to be using in as well.

79
00:05:29,040 --> 00:05:30,880
So how the code is really going to look like.

80
00:05:30,880 --> 00:05:36,200
Well, I keep telling that the MCP coding is really really easier if you have already watched all the

81
00:05:36,200 --> 00:05:40,960
sections before, if you have already tried out everything so far that we have discussed, you should

82
00:05:40,960 --> 00:05:43,200
see the code looks very, very simple.

83
00:05:43,200 --> 00:05:49,520
All we have to do while using this fast MCP server is to follow these steps over here.

84
00:05:49,520 --> 00:05:52,920
So first of all, in the past MCP server we need to create a basic server.

85
00:05:52,920 --> 00:05:58,080
So this is going to be the server which is going to act like a bridge to talk with your desktop which

86
00:05:58,080 --> 00:06:06,800
is the cloud desktop or the Ides like the GitHub copilot agent or the cursor IDE or windsurf.

87
00:06:07,000 --> 00:06:10,680
So you need to talk your your client needs to talk with the servers.

88
00:06:10,680 --> 00:06:12,360
That's why you need to have a server.

89
00:06:12,360 --> 00:06:14,120
That is the first thing that you need to create.

90
00:06:14,120 --> 00:06:18,120
And then you need to have all the toolings pretty much like how we did in our lab earlier.

91
00:06:18,120 --> 00:06:20,040
So that's what we'll be doing over here.

92
00:06:20,240 --> 00:06:23,480
And then we'll be exposing the data with resources.

93
00:06:23,480 --> 00:06:29,880
So this is something we really require because that's what the, uh, the large language model really

94
00:06:29,880 --> 00:06:31,080
requires by then.

95
00:06:31,120 --> 00:06:34,600
And then you're going to generate the dynamic content with a resource template.

96
00:06:34,600 --> 00:06:35,880
So this is something more advanced.

97
00:06:35,880 --> 00:06:39,400
We are not going to touch about the resource template and things over here.

98
00:06:39,400 --> 00:06:41,080
But but yeah that's what you have to doing.

99
00:06:41,280 --> 00:06:43,080
And then finally you need to run the server.

100
00:06:43,080 --> 00:06:47,200
So once you have everything over here like everything's been wired up into the server.

101
00:06:47,240 --> 00:06:49,080
Then just have to run the server, that's all.

102
00:06:49,160 --> 00:06:51,680
That's how you need to start the MCP server.

103
00:06:51,680 --> 00:06:52,920
It's really, really easier.

104
00:06:52,960 --> 00:06:55,960
I've just I'm just going to use a one, two and three and five.

105
00:06:56,000 --> 00:06:56,960
Not even the four.

106
00:06:57,240 --> 00:06:59,960
Um but it's going to be very straightforward.

107
00:06:59,960 --> 00:07:02,360
And the code if you I'm going to show you the code now.

108
00:07:02,400 --> 00:07:04,320
You just mind blown how easy it is.

109
00:07:04,320 --> 00:07:05,200
So this is the code.

110
00:07:05,240 --> 00:07:10,560
To be honest, this is a simple calculator program like how we built even earlier in our sections to

111
00:07:10,600 --> 00:07:12,640
add numbers, subtract the number.

112
00:07:12,640 --> 00:07:16,360
This is the same code I just copy pasted from there and I've just built it.

113
00:07:16,400 --> 00:07:21,320
See I'm just importing the MCP server dot MCP uh over here.

114
00:07:21,320 --> 00:07:22,960
And then I'm creating a server.

115
00:07:22,960 --> 00:07:26,520
This is the way that you create a simple calculator.

116
00:07:26,680 --> 00:07:28,480
Uh, MCP server, that's all.

117
00:07:28,480 --> 00:07:30,480
Once you create it, you add all the toolings.

118
00:07:30,480 --> 00:07:35,280
I'm not even adding any resources here because it doesn't really require for this particular, uh,

119
00:07:35,280 --> 00:07:38,080
MCP server and then running the server.

120
00:07:38,600 --> 00:07:39,800
So that's all.

121
00:07:39,800 --> 00:07:42,440
Once you do that, you have an MCP server.

122
00:07:42,440 --> 00:07:44,840
You can now run this MCP server right away.

123
00:07:45,080 --> 00:07:49,320
You can wire this up with your cloud desktop and we can quickly see how this works.

124
00:07:49,640 --> 00:07:50,600
This is amazing.

125
00:07:50,640 --> 00:07:55,520
I will quickly show you all of these in this particular section, but in the next lecture we're going

126
00:07:55,560 --> 00:07:58,280
to see how we can start installing everything.

127
00:07:58,280 --> 00:08:03,800
And for the first time in this particular course, we're going to be deviating from using our existing

128
00:08:03,800 --> 00:08:09,480
code repository that we have been building in Visual Studio Code so far into a new project.

129
00:08:09,480 --> 00:08:10,680
So I'm going to create a new project.

130
00:08:10,680 --> 00:08:13,520
I don't want to mix them up in the same project.

131
00:08:13,520 --> 00:08:19,080
I'm going to be creating a new folder structures everything so that I can just start everything from

132
00:08:19,080 --> 00:08:21,960
the scratch, and I can show you how the MVP servers can be built.

133
00:08:22,000 --> 00:08:28,160
And again, MVP server cannot be always matched with the long chain that we have built because long

134

chain is even more powerful.

135
00:08:29,520 --> 00:08:35,040
But if you want to create a quick solution, uh, like an agent which can talk with a large language

136
00:08:35,040 --> 00:08:40,400
model and all the capabilities, MVP server is really, really helpful and it's very fast to build it.

49

00:02:44,260 --> 00:02:44,980

00:02:44,980 --> 00:02:47,540
Now it's again uh, calling that.

00:02:56,460 --> 00:02:58,340

59
00:03:08,740 --> 00:03:16,620

60
00:03:16,620 --> 00:03:23,300
which is basically to evaluate the, uh, the page uh, locators using the JavaScript functions.

61
00:03:23,300 --> 00:03:26,020

62
00:03:26,020 --> 00:03:29,020
Actually, I have built the same thing, even my server.

63
00:03:29,020 --> 00:03:32,140
So we can actually use that to perform these operation.

See, the moment I, uh, the moment the player MVP server couldn't able to handle this operation is

00:02:53,150 --> 00:02:55,790
going to call the evaluate JS to perform this operation.

49
00:02:55,830 --> 00:02:57,630
See, that is pretty nice and neat.

50
00:02:57,990 --> 00:03:00,150
And then it it actually entered everything.

51
00:03:00,150 --> 00:03:04,310
And it also performed the log of operation which is amazing.

52
00:03:04,310 --> 00:03:05,430
So look at that.

53
00:03:05,430 --> 00:03:07,750
That's created every single thing for us.

54
00:03:07,750 --> 00:03:12,870
And it also performed a log of operation and it has logged out successfully.

55
00:03:13,310 --> 00:03:13,710
Uh.

56
00:03:14,110 --> 00:03:17,550
See also doing a get text to see if it has successfully logged out.

57
00:03:18,030 --> 00:03:19,910
And our MVP server is ready.

58
00:03:19,910 --> 00:03:27,630
Guys, you have successfully implemented a playwright MVP server all by yourself by using the power

59
00:03:27,630 --> 00:03:28,670
of fast MVP.

60
00:03:28,830 --> 00:03:31,670
Congratulations and thanks for purchasing this course.
